For an object to be detectable by a sensor, it must span **at least 2–3 pixels** (Rayleigh-style criterion). So the *minimum detectable object size* ≈ $3 \cdot \text{GSD}$.

$$D_{\min} = 3 \cdot 6.63 \text{ cm} \approx 19.9 \text{ cm}$$

Since the weed is **5 cm** and $5 \text{ cm} \ll 19.9 \text{ cm}$, **the drone cannot resolve the weed** at 100 m altitude. To detect a 5 cm weed, the GSD must be ≤ ~1.67 cm, requiring the drone to fly lower.

$$H_{\text{required}} = \frac{\text{GSD}_{\text{required}} \cdot f}{p} = \frac{0.0167 \cdot 5.2 \times 10^{-3}}{3.45 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 25.2 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H_{\text{required}} \approx 25 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Radiometric Resolution & SNR

A soil-moisture sensor has **12-bit** radiometric resolution.

**Find:** (a) Number of grey levels, (b) Theoretical SNR, (c) Approx. SNR if upgraded to 14-bit.

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the number of distinct intensity levels.**

$$L = 2^n = 2^{12}$$

$$\boxed{L = 4096 \text{ levels}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal SNR formula.**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$$

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76$$

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 72.24 + 1.76$$

$$\boxed{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 74.0 \text{ dB}}$$

**Step 3 — Upgrade to 14-bit.**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \cdot 14 + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76$$

$$\boxed{\text{SNR}_{14} = 86.04 \text{ dB}}$$

**Gain in SNR:**

$$\Delta\text{SNR} = 86.04 - 74.0 = 12.04 \text{ dB}$$

**Interpretation:** Each extra bit of radiometric resolution adds **~6 dB of SNR** — equivalent to doubling the signal clarity. For precision agriculture, 14-bit is preferred for subtle canopy vigour gradients.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Raster File Size & Compression

A farmer's drone captures a **multispectral orthomosaic** of a 50 ha coconut farm:
- Pixel size = **5 cm × 5 cm** (GSD = 0.05 m)
- **5 bands** (RGB + NIR + RedEdge)
- **16-bit** radiometric depth
- **Lossless compression ratio** = 3 : 1

**Find:** (a) Number of pixels per band, (b) Uncompressed file size, (c) Compressed file size.

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute farm area in m² and pixels per band.**

$$A = 50 \text{ ha} = 50 \times 10 000 = 5 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{\text{pixel}} = 0.05 \cdot 0.05 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$N_{\text{pixels per band}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ pixels}$$

$$\boxed{N_r \cdot N_c = 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ pixels per band}}$$

**Step 2 — Uncompressed file size.**

$$S = N_r \cdot N_c \cdot N_b \cdot \frac{n}{8}$$

$$S = 2 \times 10^{8} \cdot 5 \cdot \frac{16}{8}$$

$$S = 2 \times 10^{8} \cdot 5 \cdot 2 = 2 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 2 \text{ GB uncompressed}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply 3:1 lossless compression.**

$$S_{\text{compressed}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{9}}{3} \approx 6.67 \times 10^{8} \text{ bytes}$$

$$\boxed{S_{\text{compressed}} \approx 667 \text{ MB}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — NDVI from Sample Raster Pixels

A Sentinel-2 satellite image of a paddy field gives the following pixel values (surface reflectance × 10 000):

| Pixel Location | NIR band (B8) | Red band (B4) |
|---|---|---|
| Healthy patch | 4500 | 800 |
| Stressed patch | 2800 | 1400 |
| Water body | 1800 | 600 |
| Bare soil | 2400 | 2200 |

**Find:** NDVI for each class and interpret.

### Step-by-step Derivation

**General formula:**

$$\text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$$

**Pixel 1 — Healthy patch:**

$$\text{NDVI}_1 = \frac{4500 - 800}{4500 + 800} = \frac{3700}{5300} \approx 0.698$$

**Pixel 2 — Stressed patch:**

$$\text{NDVI}_2 = \frac{2800 - 1400}{2800 + 1400} = \frac{1400}{4200} \approx 0.333$$

**Pixel 3 — Water body:**

$$\text{NDVI}_3 = \frac{1800 - 600}{1800 + 600} = \frac{1200}{2400} = 0.500$$

*(Note: water typically gives low/negative NDVI in raw radiance; here the values are post-correction — still a flag to re-validate).*

**Pixel 4 — Bare soil:**

$$\text{NDVI}_4 = \frac{2400 - 2200}{2400 + 2200} = \frac{200}{4600} \approx 0.043$$

**Interpretation Table:**

| Class | NDVI | Inference |
|---|---|---|
| Healthy patch | 0.698 | Dense, vigorous canopy — no action |
| Stressed patch | 0.333 | Moderate stress — scout for pests / irrigate |
| Water body | 0.500 | Suspect — recheck atmospheric correction |
| Bare soil | 0.043 | Near-zero as expected — fallow or pre-planting |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Polygon Area via Shoelace Formula

A vector polygon (a paddy plot) has vertices listed in clockwise order:
- V1 = (100, 200)
- V2 = (300, 200)
- V3 = (300, 400)
- V4 = (100, 400)

**Find:** Area in m² and hectares.

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Apply the Shoelace formula:**

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$$

**Expand term by term (closing the loop V5 = V1):**

$$\text{Term}_1 = x_1 y_2 - x_2 y_1 = (100)(200) - (300)(200) = 20\,000 - 60\,000 = -40\,000$$

$$\text{Term}_2 = x_2 y_3 - x_3 y_2 = (300)(400) - (300)(200) = 120\,000 - 60\,000 = 60\,000$$

$$\text{Term}_3 = x_3 y_4 - x_4 y_3 = (300)(400) - (100)(400) = 120\,000 - 40\,000 = 80\,000$$

$$\text{Term}_4 = x_4 y_1 - x_1 y_4 = (100)(200) - (100)(400) = 20\,000 - 40\,000 = -20\,000$$

**Sum:**

$$\sum = -40\,000 + 60\,000 + 80\,000 - 20\,000 = 80\,000$$

**Final area:**

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left\vert 80\,000 \right\vert = 40\,000 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A = 4 \text{ ha}}$$

> **Verification:** A rectangle 200 m × 200 m = 40 000 m² ✓

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Vector vs. Raster

| Attribute | Vector | Raster |
|---|---|---|
| **Primitive** | Points, lines, polygons | Grid of cells (pixels) |
| **Data structure** | Coordinate list + attribute table | 2-D numeric array |
| **Topology** | Explicit (nodes, edges, faces) | Implicit (neighbour = adjacent cell) |
| **Storage** | Compact for sparse features | Compact for continuous fields |
| **Zoom behaviour** | Infinite zoom, no loss | Pixelation at high zoom |
| **Best for** | Discrete features (boundaries, networks) | Continuous fields (NDVI, elevation, soil EC) |
| **Map algebra** | Hard (requires polygon overlay) | Trivial (cell-wise arithmetic) |
| **Network analysis** | Native support | Requires vectorization |
| **Common agricultural use** | Field boundaries, drip-line, GPS tracks | NDVI maps, soil maps, DEMs |
| **File formats** | Shapefile (.shp), GeoJSON, KML, GDB | GeoTIFF (.tif), IMG, NetCDF, ECW |
| **Resolution control** | None (coordinate-based) | Cell size defines resolution |
| **Projection issues** | Slight distortion at edges | Cell stretching at high latitudes |
| **Computational load** | High for complex overlays | High for huge rasters (memory) |
| **Cartographic quality** | Publication-quality line work | "Blocky" appearance when zoomed |
| **Update frequency** | Easy to edit individual features | Re-render whole layer |

---

## 3.7 Decision Matrix — Which Data Format for Which Agricultural Task?

| Agricultural Task | Preferred Format | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Variable-rate fertilizer map | **Raster** (after interpolation) | Continuous rate values; map algebra for blending |
| Field boundary digitization | **Vector** polygon | Crisp legal boundary, scale-independent |
| Drone NDVI orthomosaic | **Raster** (GeoTIFF) | Pixel-based reflectance, multi-band stack |
| Soil sampling point location | **Vector** point | Precise GPS coordinates |
| Yield monitor data per row | **Vector** point cloud | Thousands of GPS-tagged measurements |
| Elevation / slope map | **Raster** (DEM) | Continuous surface, derived from LiDAR/photogrammetry |
| Irrigation pipe network | **Vector** polyline + topology | Need connectivity for flow simulation |
| Pest-incidence map | **Vector** points (with attributes) | Discrete observations linked to date and species |
| Weed patches | **Both** — vector polygons for patches, raster NDVI for detection | Combine attribute richness + spatial analysis |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Four Resolutions — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Resolution in Remote Sensing]:::root --> B[Spatial Resolution]:::br

    C --> C2[Number and width of bands]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Multispectral vs Hyperspectral]:::leaf

    D --> D2[Number of grey levels L equals 2 to power n]:::leaf
    D --> D3[Affects subtle intensity differences]:::leaf
    E --> E1[Unit: days revisit time]:::leaf
    E --> E2[Constellation dependent]:::leaf
    E --> E3[Critical for change detection]:::leaf
```

## 4.6 Resolution Trade-off — The "Resolution Cube"

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Resolution Trade off]:::root --> B[High Spatial]:::br
    A --> C[High Spectral]:::br
    A --> D[High Temporal]:::br
    A --> E[High Radiometric]:::br
    B --> B1[Low swath area]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Large file size]:::leaf
    C --> C1[Lower SNR per band]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Complex processing]:::leaf
    D --> D1[Limited swath width]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Constellation cost]:::leaf
    E --> E1[Higher data volume]:::leaf
    E --> E2[More expensive sensor]:::leaf
    A --> F[Trade off: Cannot Maximize All Four]:::warn

    classDef root fill:#7030a0,stroke:#3d1a5c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef br fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#7030a0,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px

    classDef warn fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#660000,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.6:** Every remote-sensing mission makes a conscious trade-off. Example: **Sentinel-2** optimized *spatial + spectral + temporal* (10–60 m, 13 bands, 5 days) at the cost of moderate radiometry (12-bit). **WorldView-3** optimized *spatial + spectral* (0.3 m, 16 bands) at the cost of poor temporal (≈1 day tasking, multi-week revisit). **PlanetScope** optimized *spatial + temporal* (3 m, daily) with limited spectral (4–8 bands).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Differentiate between spatial resolution and spectral resolution in remote sensing. Give one agricultural example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Spatial Resolution | Spectral Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Smallest object that can be distinguished on the ground | Ability to distinguish between different wavelength intervals |
| Unit | meters per pixel (GSD) | nanometers (bandwidth, Δλ) |
| Determines | *How small* a feature can be seen | *What type* of feature can be detected by its spectral signature |
| Agricultural Example | A 5 cm GSD drone can detect individual weeds in a paddy row | A 10 nm bandwidth hyperspectral sensor can detect early nitrogen stress in rice before visible yellowing |

**Awarding note (3 marks):** 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for unit, 1 mark for agricultural example.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three differences between vector and raster data formats in GIS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Representation:** Vector represents features as points, lines, or polygons defined by coordinates; raster represents the world as a grid of equal-sized cells (pixels), each holding a single value.
2. **Best Use Case:** Vector is preferred for discrete features with sharp boundaries (e.g., farm boundaries, irrigation networks); raster is preferred for continuous phenomena (e.g., NDVI, soil moisture, elevation).
3. **Storage & Zoom:** Vector storage is compact for sparse data and supports infinite zoom without quality loss; raster file size grows with resolution and exhibits pixelation when over-zoomed.
4. *(Bonus — for extra credit)* Vector supports native network analysis and topology; raster supports map algebra directly.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students frequently *list* the four resolutions without stating their **units** — KTU deducts 1 mark per missing unit.
> - In vector/raster questions, students forget to mention **topology vs. map algebra** as the fundamental mathematical difference.
> - In GIS applications, students give generic GIS uses (e.g., "for mapping") instead of **precision-agriculture-specific** uses (e.g., "for variable-rate prescription generation"). Always anchor answers to *agriculture*.
> - When computing NDVI, students forget the **range is $-1$ to $+1$** and may give unscaled values — full marks only if normalised.
> - For file-size calculations, students miss the $\dfrac{n}{8}$ conversion (bits to bytes) — this costs 1 mark.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**Question A(a) [7 marks]:**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**
**Explain the four types of resolution in remote sensing with a neat diagram. Discuss how each affects a precision agriculture application.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Resolution in remote sensing is a composite property describing the sensor's ability to discriminate features along four orthogonal dimensions: space, spectrum, intensity, and time.

**Spatial Resolution (1.5 marks):** Defined by GSD = $H \cdot p / f$, measured in metres per pixel. Determines the *smallest field feature* that can be mapped. In agriculture, a 0.05 m GSD drone image can detect individual weeds; a 10 m Sentinel-2 image can only resolve field-scale vegetation zones.

**Spectral Resolution (1.5 marks):** Defined by bandwidth Δλ in nm and number of bands. Determines the *type of biophysical property* measurable. Multispectral (4–15 bands, 60–120 nm bandwidth) suffices for NDVI/EVI; hyperspectral (>100 bands, <10 nm bandwidth) is needed for specific disease pigments and chlorophyll fluorescence.

**Radiometric Resolution (1.5 marks):** Defined by bit-depth *n*; grey levels $L = 2^n$. Determines the *subtlety of intensity differences* recordable. For canopy chlorophyll or soil-moisture gradients where reflectance differences are <2%, a 12-bit or 16-bit sensor is mandatory.

**Temporal Resolution (1.5 marks):** Defined by revisit time (days). Determines *how fast changes can be tracked*. For irrigation scheduling, daily revisit is required; for seasonal fertilizer decisions, weekly/monthly is sufficient.

---

**Question A(b) [7 marks]:**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
**Compare vector and raster data models in GIS. State the precision-agriculture use case where each is most appropriate, with justification.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Vector model** — Stores features as geometric primitives (point, polyline, polygon) with explicit (x, y) coordinates and optional topology, linked to an attribute table.
- **Raster model** — Stores the landscape as a 2-D grid of equally-sized cells, each cell holding a single numeric value; multi-band stacks represent multi-spectral data.

**Comparative Table (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Vector | Raster |
|---|---|---|
| Data primitive | Coordinate-based geometry | Cell-based grid |
| Spatial accuracy | High (sub-cm possible) | Limited by cell size |
| Topology | Explicit (nodes, edges, faces) | Implicit (4 or 8 neighbours) |
| Map algebra | Requires overlay processing | Direct cell-wise arithmetic |
| Best for | Discrete features | Continuous surfaces |
| Storage | Compact for sparse | Compact for dense grids |
| Common agri-format | Shapefile, GeoJSON | GeoTIFF, IMG |

**Precision Agriculture Use Cases (2 marks):**
- **Vector** is most appropriate for **delineating farm boundaries, drip-line layouts, and recording soil sample points** — where crisp, scale-independent boundaries and topology are critical.
- **Raster** is most appropriate for **NDVI, soil-EC, and prescription maps** — where values are continuous across space and map algebra is used to combine layers.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Question B(a) [7 marks]:**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alt]**
**What is GIS? Describe the components of a GIS. Explain in detail how GIS is used in precision farming with a suitable workflow.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Geographic Information System (GIS) is a computer-based system for capturing, storing, retrieving, analysing, and displaying geographically-referenced data. In precision farming, GIS integrates multi-source spatial data (imagery, GPS, sensor logs) to support site-specific crop management.

**Five Components of GIS (2 marks):**
1. **Hardware** — GPS, UAV, computer, plotter.
2. **Software** — ArcGIS, QGIS, SAGA, Google Earth Engine.
3. **Data** — Spatial (raster + vector) and non-spatial (soil reports, yield logs).
4. **People** — Agronomist, GIS analyst, drone pilot.
5. **Procedures** — SOPs for data collection, processing, validation.

**GIS in Precision Farming — Detailed Workflow (4 marks):**

1. **Data Acquisition** — GPS survey of field boundaries; drone flight for orthomosaic + NDVI; soil sampling at grid points; yield-monitor data from harvester.
2. **Preprocessing** — Radiometric calibration, geometric correction, atmospheric correction of satellite imagery.
3. **Database Construction** — Vector layer (boundaries) + raster layers (NDVI, soil EC) integrated in a spatial database (e.g., PostGIS).
4. **Spatial Analysis** — Interpolation (Kriging/IDW) of soil pH; overlay of NDVI + soil pH + elevation to delineate **management zones**.
5. **Prescription Map Generation** — Reclassify zones, assign variable rate of N, P, K, water; export as ISO-XML or shapefile.
6. **Variable Rate Application** — Tractor-mounted controller reads the map and actuates fertiliser/pesticide in real time.
7. **Feedback Loop** — Post-application yield monitoring → geostatistical analysis → next-season decision refinement.

---

**Question B(b) [7 marks]:**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alt]**
**A drone carries a multispectral camera with the following specifications: flight altitude H = 120 m, pixel pitch p = 4.0 μm, focal length f = 8.0 mm, detectors across-track = 1456, 5 bands, 12-bit radiometric resolution.**

**Compute:**
**(i) Spatial resolution (GSD) and swath width.**
**(ii) Number of grey levels and theoretical SNR in dB.**
**(iii) Discuss, with reasoning, whether this system is suitable for detecting 10 cm-wide weeds in a paddy field.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) GSD and Swath Width (3 marks):**

$$\text{GSD} = \frac{H \cdot p}{f} = \frac{120 \cdot 4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{8.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-4}}{8.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{GSD} = 0.06 \text{ m} = 6 \text{ cm}}$$

$$\text{Swath} = N_x \cdot \text{GSD} = 1456 \cdot 0.06$$

$$\boxed{\text{Swath} = 87.36 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Grey Levels and SNR (2 marks):**

$$L = 2^{12} = \boxed{4096 \text{ levels}}$$

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = \boxed{74.0 \text{ dB}}$$

**(iii) Detectability Discussion (2 marks):**

For a feature to be reliably detectable, it should span **at least 2–3 pixels** (Rayleigh-style resolution criterion).

$$D_{\min} \approx 3 \cdot \text{GSD} = 3 \cdot 6 = 18 \text{ cm}$$

The weed is **10 cm wide**, which is **less than 18 cm** — therefore the system **cannot reliably resolve individual 10 cm weeds** at 120 m altitude. To detect 10 cm weeds, the GSD must be ≤ 3.3 cm:

$$H_{\text{required}} = \frac{0.033 \cdot 8.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 66 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** Fly the drone at **≤ 66 m altitude** to detect 10 cm weeds. Alternatively, use a higher-resolution camera (smaller pixel pitch or longer focal length). The 12-bit radiometric depth is *adequate* for weed detection; the limitation is purely *spatial*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 ESE**

**Resolution — Must Remember:**
- ☐ Four types: **Spatial, Spectral, Radiometric, Temporal** — never answer with one alone.
- ☐ Spatial resolution **GSD = H·p / f** (m); IFOV on ground = H·tan(θ) (m).
- ☐ Spectral resolution: **Δλ in nm**; multispectral (3–15 bands) vs. hyperspectral (>100 bands).
- ☐ Radiometric resolution: **L = 2ⁿ**; SNR ≈ **6.02·n + 1.76 dB**; every extra bit = +6 dB SNR.
- ☐ Temporal resolution: **revisit time** in days; constellations reduce it (e.g., 16→8 days with two Landsats).
- ☐ Object detectability requires feature to span **≥ 2–3 pixels**.

**Vector vs. Raster — Must Remember:**
- ☐ Vector = **point, polyline, polygon**; coordinates + topology + attributes.
- ☐ Raster = **grid of cells**; each cell = one number; multi-band = multi-spectral.
- ☐ Vector for **boundaries, networks, points**; raster for **continuous fields**.
- ☐ Vector pros: crisp zoom, topology, network analysis. Cons: complex overlay.
- ☐ Raster pros: map algebra, easy overlay, multi-band. Cons: pixelation, big files.
- ☐ File size: $S = N_r \cdot N_c \cdot N_b \cdot (n/8)$ bytes uncompressed.
- ☐ **NDVI = (NIR − Red) / (NIR + Red)** — range **−1 to +1**.

**GIS for Precision Farming — Must Remember:**
- ☐ GIS = **Capture, Store, Retrieve, Analyse, Display** of spatial data.
- ☐ Five components: **Hardware, Software, Data, People, Procedures** (Burrough's model).
- ☐ Key operations: **Buffer, Overlay, Interpolation (IDW, Kriging), Reclassification, Zonal Statistics**.
- ☐ **Workflow**: Acquisition → Preprocessing → Indices → Zone Delineation → Prescription → VRA → Yield Monitor → Feedback.
- ☐ Precision-agriculture-specific outputs: **Variable Rate (VR) maps, management zones, prescription files (ISO-XML, shp)**.
- ☐ Two classic data structures: **vector** (Shapefile, GeoJSON) and **raster** (GeoTIFF, IMG, NetCDF).

**Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU Exam:**
- ☐ Don't say "resolution = GSD" — always name all four types.
- ☐ Don't forget units (m, nm, bits, days).
- ☐ Don't confuse **GSD** (m/pixel) with **IFOV** (radians).
- ☐ Don't forget to convert bits → bytes via $n/8$ in file-size problems.
- ☐ Always state the *agricultural* use case, not just a generic GIS use case.
- ☐ In numeric problems, show **each substitution step** — KTU awards partial credit for intermediate work.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Calculator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT861 - SENSORS, AI AND ROBOTICS IN PRECISION AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Field Calculator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Calculator** is a powerful geoprocessing tool embedded within Geographic Information System (GIS) platforms (ArcGIS, QGIS, GRASS GIS, ERDAS Imagine) that allows the user to dynamically compute and populate values in an attribute table, raster dataset, or feature class by applying **mathematical expressions**, **conditional logic**, **string manipulations**, or **Python/VB Script** functions to existing data fields.

In the context of **Precision Agriculture (PA)**, the Field Calculator acts as the computational backbone for translating raw multispectral, hyperspectral, thermal, LiDAR, and yield-monitor data into **actionable agronomic indices** such as NDVI, EVI, GNDVI, LAI, and soil-adjusted vegetation indices. It transforms static tabular data into *spatially-explicit decision variables* that drive Variable Rate Technology (VRT).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Farm Spreadsheet with Superpowers"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine an enormous digital ledger (a spreadsheet) where every single row represents one **plot of land**, one **pixel of satellite imagery**, or one **GPS-recorded sample point** in your farm. Each row contains columns of raw measurements — say, Red light reflectance, Near-Infrared reflectance, soil pH, moisture content, and yield in kg.

> **The Field Calculator is like a "magic pen"** that automatically walks down every row of this giant ledger and, based on a formula *you* write, fills in a new column with a calculated result. You don't manually type 10,000 values — you write *one* equation, and the calculator fills all 10,000 cells instantly.

**Real-World Farm Example:**
A farmer has drone imagery with **Red band** and **NIR band** values for every 0.5 m × 0.5 m pixel of a 50-hectare wheat field. Instead of manually computing vegetation health for ~2,000,000 pixels, the Field Calculator applies the NDVI formula to every pixel automatically in seconds.

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|------|------------|--------------------------|
| **Attribute Table** | Tabular data linked to spatial features (points, lines, polygons, pixels) | The data structure on which Field Calculator operates |
| **Raster** | Grid of pixels where each cell holds a numeric value (e.g., reflectance) | Common input for vegetation index calculations |
| **Vector** | Points/lines/polygons with associated attributes (e.g., soil sample sites) | Common input for zone-based prescription maps |
| **Parser** | The interpreter that converts user expressions into machine code | Determines supported syntax (Python, VB, R, SQL) |
| **Expression** | The mathematical/logical statement written by the user | The "recipe" for the calculation |
| **Output Field** | The destination column/pixel band receiving the result | Must be correctly typed (integer, float, text) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded Standard Metric:** The output of Field Calculator operations is typically a **continuous floating-point raster** (32-bit or 64-bit float) when computing vegetation indices, ensuring precision down to **±0.001 reflectance units** for sensitive agronomic decisions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How the Field Calculator Thinks

The Field Calculator operates on a **row-by-row iteration paradigm** (for vector/attribute tables) or a **cell-by-cell band-math paradigm** (for rasters). The processing pipeline is:

1. **Input Identification:** The parser recognizes whether the input is a vector attribute table, a raster stack, or a mixed geospatial dataset.
2. **Field/Variable Binding:** Each column name or band name referenced in the expression is mapped to a memory pointer.
3. **Expression Parsing:** The expression is tokenized and converted into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
4. **Iterative Evaluation:** For each row/pixel, the AST is evaluated, substituting the bound values.
5. **Output Writing:** Results are written to the specified output field/band, respecting the data type (integer, float, string, date).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Precision Agriculture:** Field sensors, drones, and satellites generate massive spatial datasets. The Field Calculator enables **on-the-fly analytics** without exporting data to external statistical software, preserving the geospatial linkage critical for VRT maps.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Vegetation Index Calculations

The following table summarizes the **most frequently tested Field Calculator expressions** for precision agriculture. All equations assume reflectance values normalized to the interval $[0, 1]$.

| Index | Field Calculator Expression | Range | Agronomic Purpose |
|-------|---------------------------|-------|-------------------|
| **NDVI** | $\frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | General vegetation vigor |
| **EVI** | $2.5 \cdot \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + 6\cdot\text{Red} - 7.5\cdot\text{Blue} + 1}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Dense canopy health (reduces soil noise) |
| **GNDVI** | $\frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Green}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Green}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Chlorophyll concentration |
| **SAVI** | $1.5 \cdot \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red} + 0.5}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Sparse canopy (soil-adjusted) |
| **NDWI** | $\frac{\text{Green} - \text{NIR}}{\text{Green} + \text{NIR}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Leaf/canopy water content |
| **NDRE** | $\frac{\text{NIR} - \text{RE}}{\text{NIR} + \text{RE}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Mid-late season nitrogen status |
| **Yield (kg/ha)** | $\frac{\text{Total Yield (kg)}}{\text{Area (m}^2\text{)}} \times 10{,}000$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Spatial productivity mapping |
| **VRA N-Rate (kg/ha)** | $\text{N}_{\text{target}} - f(\text{NDVI})$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Variable-rate nitrogen prescription |

---

## 2.3 Key Logical & Conditional Expressions

Precision agriculture frequently requires **rule-based zoning**. The Field Calculator supports conditional logic:

$$
\text{Zone} = \begin{cases} \text{Stressed}, & \text{if } \text{NDVI} < 0.3 \\ \text{Moderate}, & \text{if } 0.3 \le \text{NDVI} \le 0.6 \\ \text{Healthy}, & \text{if } \text{NDVI} > 0.6 \end{cases}
$$

In QGIS syntax (often tested):

```text
CASE
  WHEN "NDVI" < 0.3 THEN 'Stressed'
  WHEN "NDVI" >= 0.3 AND "NDVI" <= 0.6 THEN 'Moderate'
  WHEN "NDVI" > 0.6 THEN 'Healthy'
END
```

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Fertilization:** Converts NDRE rasters into pixel-wise nitrogen prescription maps for spreader control systems.
- **Irrigation Management:** Computes Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI) from thermal imagery to trigger zone-specific drip irrigation.
- **Yield Forecasting:** Aggregates field-calculator-derived biomass estimates with weather APIs to predict harvest tonnage.
- **Disease Detection:** Threshold-based Field Calculator outputs feed machine-learning pipelines for early pest outbreak detection.
- **Soil Sampling Design:** Stratifies fields into management zones based on calculated ECa (apparent electrical conductivity) clusters.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — NDVI from Two-Band Imagery Using Field Calculator

### Problem Setup
A precision-agriculture drone captures a multispectral image with **4 bands**: Blue ($\rho_{\text{B}}$), Green ($\rho_{\text{G}}$), Red ($\rho_{\text{R}}$), and Near-Infrared ($\rho_{\text{NIR}}$). For every pixel $i$, we must compute NDVI and assign a textual vigor class. Derive the complete Field Calculator workflow.

---

### Step 1 — Initialize the Output Field
A new floating-point field named `"NDVI"` is created in the raster attribute table or as a new raster band. Data type: **32-bit float**, NoData value: **-9999**.

### Step 2 — Write the Field Calculator Expression
The user enters:

```text
( "NIR@1" - "RED@1" ) / ( "NIR@1" + "RED@1" )
```

The parser interprets the `@1` band-index qualifier as a reference to band 1 of the raster stack.

### Step 3 — Per-Pixel Algebraic Expansion
For an arbitrary pixel $i$, the parser substitutes the band values:

$$
\text{NDVI}_i = \frac{\rho_{\text{NIR},i} - \rho_{\text{R},i}}{\rho_{\text{NIR},i} + \rho_{\text{R},i}}
$$

### Step 4 — Denominator Safety Check (Division by Zero)
When $\rho_{\text{NIR},i} + \rho_{\text{R},i} = 0$ (theoretically impossible in real reflectance but computationally possible due to NoData), the expression must be guarded:

$$
\text{NDVI}_i = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\rho_{\text{NIR},i} - \rho_{\text{R},i}}{\rho_{\text{NIR},i} + \rho_{\text{R},i}}, & \text{if } \rho_{\text{NIR},i} + \rho_{\text{R},i} \neq 0 \\ \text{NoData}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Illustration
Suppose for pixel $i$:
- $\rho_{\text{NIR},i} = 0.48$
- $\rho_{\text{R},i} = 0.08$

Compute:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.48 - 0.08 = 0.40
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 0.48 + 0.08 = 0.56
$$

$$
\text{NDVI}_i = \frac{0.40}{0.56} = 0.7143
$$

Since $0.7143 > 0.6$, this pixel is classified as **"Healthy canopy"**.

### Step 6 — Vectorized Iteration
The above calculation is replicated for all $N$ pixels:

$$
\mathbf{NDVI} = \left\{ \frac{\rho_{\text{NIR},i} - \rho_{\text{R},i}}{\rho_{\text{NIR},i} + \rho_{\text{R},i}} \;\middle|\; i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, N\} \right\}
$$

where the cardinality $\vert \mathbf{NDVI} \vert = N$ (the number of pixels in the raster).

### Step 7 — Zonal Aggregation (Optional Next Step)
If the farm is divided into $Z$ management zones, the Field Calculator (or Zonal Statistics tool) computes the **mean NDVI** per zone:

$$
\overline{\text{NDVI}}_z = \frac{1}{\vert S_z \vert} \sum_{i \in S_z} \text{NDVI}_i, \quad z \in \{1, 2, \ldots, Z\}
$$

where $S_z$ is the set of pixels belonging to zone $z$, and $\vert S_z \vert$ is the pixel count of that zone.

### Step 8 — Final Decision Rule for Variable-Rate Application
The nitrogen prescription per zone is:

$$
N_z \, (\text{kg/ha}) = N_{\max} \cdot (1 - \overline{\text{NDVI}}_z)
$$

This converts each calculated NDVI mean into a tangible fertilizer recommendation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Field Calculator Operational Modes

| Mode | Input Data Type | Parser Language | Use Case in PA |
|------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Attribute Mode** | Vector attribute table | Python / VB / SQL | Soil pH zonation, yield per polygon |
| **Raster Band Math** | Multi-band raster | Raster calculator syntax | NDVI, EVI, GNDVI computation |
| **Geometry Mode** | Feature coordinates | Python (e.g., `$area`, `$length`) | Field area calculation, buffer width |
| **Conditional Mode** | Mixed attributes | `CASE WHEN` syntax | Stress classification, VRA prescriptions |
| **String Mode** | Text fields | String functions (concatenate, substring) | Farm ID labels, hybrid name tagging |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Field Calculator in a Precision Agriculture Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts how the Field Calculator integrates into a complete decision-support pipeline — from raw sensor capture to variable-rate actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Drone Multispectral Sensor] --> B[Raw 4-Band Raster Stack]
    C[Soil EC Sensor] --> D[Soil Sample Point Layer]
    E[Yield Monitor Combine] --> F[Yield Point Layer]
    B --> G[Field Calculator Module]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[NDVI Raster Output]
    G --> I[Management Zone Polygons]
    G --> J[Yield per Hectare Table]
    H --> K[Decision Support Engine]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Variable Rate Prescription Map]
    L --> M[GPS-Guided Tractor Actuation]
    M --> N[Site-Specific Fertilizer Application]
    style G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **gold-highlighted block (G)** is the Field Calculator — the analytical core.
- **Blue/green inputs (B, D, F)** feed heterogeneous geospatial data.
- The **green-highlighted Decision Engine (K)** consumes Field Calculator outputs to generate actionable prescriptions.

---

## 4.2 Logical Decision Flow for Conditional Field Calculator Zoning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pixel NDVI Value Calculated] --> Q{NDVI Less Than 0.3?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Assign Zone: Stressed Canopy]
    Q -- No --> S{NDVI Between 0.3 and 0.6?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Assign Zone: Moderate Vigor]
    S -- No --> U[Assign Zone: Healthy Canopy]
    R --> V[Trigger High N-Rate VRA]
    T --> W[Trigger Standard N-Rate VRA]
    U --> X[Trigger Reduced N-Rate VRA]
    style P fill:#87CEEB
    style R fill:#FF6347
    style T fill:#FFD700
    style U fill:#32CD32
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Color Coding:** Red = critical intervention, Yellow = balanced management, Green = maintenance-level input. This mirrors real-world agronomic decision dashboards.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define the Field Calculator tool as used in GIS-based Precision Agriculture. List any two vegetation indices commonly computed using it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Field Calculator** is a GIS tool that computes new attribute values or raster values by applying user-defined mathematical, conditional, or string expressions to existing fields/bands of a geospatial dataset (1 mark).

**Two commonly computed vegetation indices:**

1. **NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index):** $\frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ — assesses canopy greenness and vigor (1 mark).

2. **EVI (Enhanced Vegetation Index):** $2.5 \cdot \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + 6\cdot\text{Red} - 7.5\cdot\text{Blue} + 1}$ — corrects soil background and atmospheric noise in dense canopies (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain how the Field Calculator handles division-by-zero and NoData values during raster band math. Why is this important in precision agriculture?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Field Calculator wraps user expressions in **conditional guards** that explicitly check for zero denominators and NoData pixel values before evaluation (1 mark). When a NoData pixel is encountered, the output is also set to NoData, propagating the mask through subsequent operations (1 mark). This is critical in precision agriculture because faulty or saturated sensor readings (e.g., cloud-contaminated pixels) must not corrupt prescription maps, otherwise VRA equipment may over- or under-apply inputs, causing economic loss and environmental harm (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the mathematical formulation of the **Soil-Adjusted Vegetation Index (SAVI)** and explain its advantage over NDVI when used in precision agriculture field operations. Write the complete Field Calculator expression syntax.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given a 10-hectare wheat field subdivided into 3 management zones with the following mean NDVI values — Zone 1: 0.25, Zone 2: 0.50, Zone 3: 0.78 — and a target maximum nitrogen application of $N_{\max} = 120$ kg/ha, compute the variable-rate nitrogen prescription for each zone using the rule $N_z = N_{\max} \cdot (1 - \overline{\text{NDVI}}_z)$.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — SAVI Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Motivation:**
NDVI saturates in high-biomass canopies and is contaminated by soil background reflectance in sparse vegetation (1 mark).

**Step 2 — Introduction of Soil Adjustment Factor (L):**
Huete (1988) introduced a soil-brightness correction coefficient $L$, typically $L = 0.5$ for intermediate vegetation cover (1 mark).

**Step 3 — Mathematical Derivation:**

$$
\text{SAVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red} + L} \cdot (1 + L)
$$

Substituting $L = 0.5$:

$$
\text{SAVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red} + 0.5} \cdot 1.5
$$

Equivalent form:

$$
\text{SAVI} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red} + 0.5}
$$

(2 marks for the derivation)

**Step 4 — Field Calculator Expression (QGIS Raster Calculator syntax):**
```text
1.5 * ( "NIR@1" - "Red@1" ) / ( "NIR@1" + "Red@1" + 0.5 )
```
(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Advantage over NDVI:**
SAVI minimizes soil-background influence by adding $L$ to the denominator and scaling the numerator by $(1+L)$, making it superior for **early-season sparse canopies** where NDVI overestimates soil brightness (2 marks).

---

#### Part (b) — Variable-Rate N Prescription (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $N_{\max} = 120$ kg/ha
- Zone 1: $\overline{\text{NDVI}}_1 = 0.25$
- Zone 2: $\overline{\text{NDVI}}_2 = 0.50$
- Zone 3: $\overline{\text{NDVI}}_3 = 0.78$

**Step 1 — Apply formula for Zone 1:**

$$
N_1 = 120 \cdot (1 - 0.25) = 120 \cdot 0.75 = 90 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Apply formula for Zone 2:**

$$
N_2 = 120 \cdot (1 - 0.50) = 120 \cdot 0.50 = 60 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Apply formula for Zone 3:**

$$
N_3 = 120 \cdot (1 - 0.78) = 120 \cdot 0.22 = 26.4 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Zone 1 (low NDVI) receives the highest N dose due to suspected deficiency; Zone 3 (vigorous canopy) receives the lowest dose, avoiding lodging and nitrate leaching (1 mark).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 — Alternative]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a block diagram the complete workflow of using the Field Calculator to convert raw multispectral drone imagery into a Variable Rate Application (VRA) nitrogen prescription map. Label all data inputs, intermediate products, and outputs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **EVI** for a maize canopy pixel with the following reflectance values: $\rho_{\text{NIR}} = 0.55$, $\rho_{\text{R}} = 0.06$, $\rho_{\text{B}} = 0.04$. State the agronomic interpretation.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Workflow Block Diagram (7 marks)

The complete workflow comprises six sequential stages:

1. **Data Acquisition Stage:** Drone-mounted multispectral sensor captures Blue, Green, Red, NIR bands (1 mark).
2. **Pre-processing Stage:** Radiometric calibration, atmospheric correction, orthomosaicking (1 mark).
3. **Field Calculator Stage:** Apply NDVI/EVI expressions to compute vegetation indices per pixel (1 mark).
4. **Zonal Statistics Stage:** Aggregate pixel-level indices into management zones (polygons) (1 mark).
5. **Decision Engine Stage:** Apply agronomic threshold rules (e.g., $N_z = N_{\max}(1 - \overline{\text{NDVI}}_z)$) (1 mark).
6. **Actuation Stage:** Export prescription map to GPS-guided variable-rate spreader/sprayer (1 mark).

**Diagram (1 mark):**
Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — the same architecture, fully labeled.

---

#### Part (b) — EVI Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recall the EVI formula:**

$$
\text{EVI} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{\rho_{\text{NIR}} - \rho_{\text{R}}}{\rho_{\text{NIR}} + 6 \cdot \rho_{\text{R}} - 7.5 \cdot \rho_{\text{B}} + 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given reflectance values:**

$$
\text{Numerator term} = 0.55 - 0.06 = 0.49
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 0.55 + 6(0.06) - 7.5(0.04) + 1
$$

$$
= 0.55 + 0.36 - 0.30 + 1
$$

$$
= 1.61
$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

$$
\text{EVI} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{0.49}{1.61} = 2.5 \cdot 0.3043 = 0.7608
$$

(5 marks for calculation)

**Step 5 — Agronomic Interpretation:**
An EVI of $\approx 0.76$ indicates a **very healthy, dense maize canopy** with high photosynthetic activity. Such values typically occur at peak growth stages (VT-R1). For nitrogen management, this zone requires minimal supplemental N (1 mark).

**Step 6 — Sanity Check:**
Since EVI is bounded in $[-1, +1]$, the value $0.7608 \in [-1, +1]$ is valid (1 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the soil adjustment constant (L) in SAVI:** Many students write SAVI as $\frac{\text{NIR}-\text{Red}}{\text{NIR}+\text{Red}}$ — this is NDVI, not SAVI. You will lose **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Unit confusion in yield calculations:** When converting kg/m² to kg/ha, students often forget to multiply by $10{,}000$. This is a frequent **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Not stating the data type of the output field:** Always specify **32-bit float** for continuous indices; integer fields will truncate and corrupt your results.
> 4. **Missing NoData handling:** Examiners specifically test whether you understood that the Field Calculator must guard against division-by-zero and NoData propagation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Summary:**

- ✅ **Field Calculator** = GIS tool that computes new field/band values from existing data using expressions.
- ✅ It supports **vector attribute tables** AND **multi-band rasters**.
- ✅ The most tested index in KTU exams is **NDVI**: $\frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$, range $[-1, +1]$.
- ✅ **EVI** adds Blue band and reduces atmospheric/soil noise — formula has constant $2.5$ in numerator and denominator coefficients $\{6, -7.5, 1\}$.
- ✅ **SAVI** introduces soil adjustment factor $L = 0.5$; preferred for **sparse, early-season canopies**.
- ✅ **NDRE** uses Red-Edge band instead of Red; used in **mid-late season nitrogen assessment**.
- ✅ **Variable-Rate N rule:** $N_z = N_{\max} \cdot (1 - \overline{\text{NDVI}}_z)$ — lower NDVI gets more N.
- ✅ Always handle **NoData and division-by-zero** with conditional guards.
- ✅ Output field must be **32-bit float** for continuous vegetation indices.
- ✅ Field Calculator expressions use **parser-specific syntax** (Python in QGIS, VB/Python in ArcGIS).
- ✅ It is the **analytical bridge** between raw sensor data and GPS-guided variable-rate actuation.
- ✅ Conditional zoning uses `CASE WHEN ... THEN ... END` syntax in QGIS.
- ✅ Zonal aggregation uses: $\overline{\text{NDVI}}_z = \frac{1}{\vert S_z \vert} \sum_{i \in S_z} \text{NDVI}_i$.
- ✅ In QGIS raster syntax, band references use `@1`, `@2`, etc. (e.g., `"NIR@1"`).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convert to Grid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT861 - SENSORS, AI AND ROBOTICS IN PRECISION AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Convert to Grid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** *"Convert to Grid"* is a fundamental Spatial Interpolation operation in Geographic Information Systems (GIS) that transforms discrete vector-based point samples (e.g., soil nutrient readings, yield monitor data) into a continuous raster surface represented as a regular grid of pixels (cells).

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Convert to Grid** (also referred to as *Surface Interpolation* or *Rasterization of Point Data*) is the computational process of estimating attribute values at unsampled locations across a geographic area by mathematically modeling the spatial autocorrelation of a known set of sampled point measurements. The output is a **raster grid** — a tessellation of equal-sized square cells, where each cell is assigned a numerical value derived from the surrounding input samples.

In precision agriculture, this operation is the foundational step for generating **Prescription Maps** (Variable Rate Application maps), **Yield Maps**, **Soil Nutrient Maps**, **Elevation Models (DEMs)**, and **Crop Health Index Maps** from raw sensor-derived point data.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Vector Data** | Discrete points, lines, or polygons defined by exact x,y (and z) coordinates. |
| **Raster Data** | Continuous surface stored as a matrix of cells (pixels), each holding a single value. |
| **Cell (Pixel)** | The smallest unit of a raster grid, with uniform dimensions (e.g., 5 m × 5 m). |
| **Spatial Resolution** | The ground distance represented by one grid cell. Higher resolution = smaller cell = more detail. |
| **Interpolation** | Mathematical estimation of a value at an unsampled location based on weighted nearby samples. |
| **Search Radius** | The maximum distance within which the algorithm considers neighboring points for estimation. |
| **Spatial Autocorrelation** | The statistical property where values closer in space tend to be more similar (Tobler's First Law of Geography). |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Weather Map:**
> Imagine you have temperature readings from **only 10 weather stations** scattered across Kerala. To draw a continuous "isotherm" map showing how temperature varies from Kochi to Kasaragod, you must **guess (interpolate)** the temperature at every point between the stations. *Convert to Grid* does exactly this for agricultural fields: it converts a few hundred GPS-tagged soil samples into a continuous, color-coded nutrient map covering every square meter of the farm.

The mathematical "guess" is governed by the assumption that **samples close to each other are more alike than samples far apart** — this is the core hypothesis (Tobler's First Law of Geography, 1970) that makes interpolation scientifically valid.

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics Used

- **Grid Cell Size:** Commonly **1 m to 30 m** for field-scale precision agriculture; **10 m / 30 m** for satellite-derived products (Sentinel-2 = **10 m**, Landsat-8 = **30 m**).
- **Search Radius:** Typically **50% to 200%** of the average sample spacing.
- **Minimum Number of Points:** Usually **8 to 12** neighboring samples used per cell estimation.
- **Coordinate System:** Outputs are referenced in WGS-84 (EPSG:4326) or a local projected CRS (e.g., UTM zone 43N for Kerala).
- **Coordinate Standard:** **Spatial Reference ID (SRID)** must match across input and output for valid overlay.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Pipeline — Why & How

The *Convert to Grid* workflow executes the following sequential logic steps:

1. **Input Validation**
   - *Why:* Ensures data integrity.
   - *How:* The GIS engine verifies that input points possess (a) valid geographic coordinates, (b) a non-null attribute field for the variable to be interpolated (e.g., soil pH, yield t/ha), and (c) a consistent Coordinate Reference System (CRS).

2. **Grid Definition (Parameterization)**
   - *Why:* Defines the spatial "canvas" onto which values are painted.
   - *How:* The analyst specifies the **output extent** (bounding box), **cell size** (resolution), and **number of rows/columns**. The output grid = ((Xmax − Xmin) / cell_size) × ((Ymax − Ymin) / cell_size) cells.

3. **Spatial Search (Neighborhood Identification)**
   - *Why:* Determines which input points influence each cell's value.
   - *How:* For each grid cell centroid, the algorithm searches within the **Search Radius** for the **N nearest points** or all points within radius.

4. **Weight Assignment**
   - *Why:* Closer points should have greater influence.
   - *How:* A mathematical weighting function (e.g., inverse distance, Gaussian, exponential) assigns each neighbor a weight based on its separation distance.

5. **Value Estimation (Interpolation Core)**
   - *Why:* Produces the predicted value at the cell centroid.
   - *How:* A weighted linear combination of neighbor values is computed using the chosen interpolation method.

6. **Output Surface Assembly**
   - *Why:* Packages results for GIS display and further analysis.
   - *How:* All estimated cell values are assembled into a raster matrix and saved (GeoTIFF, ArcInfo ASCII Grid, etc.).

7. **Validation (Cross-Validation / RMSE)**
   - *Why:* Quantifies prediction accuracy.
   - *How:* Leave-one-out cross-validation removes one point, re-interpolates, and compares predicted vs observed values. Metric: **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)**.

### 2.2 Interpolation Methods — Comparative Analysis

| Method | Mathematical Basis | Best Use Case | KTU Exam Frequency |
|--------|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **IDW (Inverse Distance Weighting)** | Deterministic; weighted average by inverse distance | Dense, evenly distributed samples | ★★★★★ |
| **Kriging (Ordinary / Universal)** | Geostatistical; uses semivariogram | Spatially correlated data, sparse samples | ★★★★★ |
| **Spline** | Mathematical function (minimizes curvature) | Smooth surfaces (e.g., elevation, pH) | ★★★★ |
| **Nearest Neighbor (Thiessen Polygon)** | Assigns nearest point's value | Categorical data, soil class maps | ★★★ |
| **Natural Neighbor** | Voronoi tessellation-based | Irregular sample distributions | ★★ |
| **TIN-based (Triangulation)** | Linear interpolation across triangles | Terrain modeling, irregular terrain | ★★★ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize These for ESE**

**Table 2.A — Core Interpolation Equations**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $$Z(x_0) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{Z(x_i)}{d_i^p}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{d_i^p}}$$ | **Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW)** | $Z(x_0)$ = predicted value; $d_i$ = distance; $p$ = power (typically 1, 2, or 3); $n$ = neighbor count |
| 2 | $$w_i = \frac{1}{d_i^p}, \quad \sum w_i = 1$$ | **IDW Weight Normalization** | Weights sum to unity (dimensionless) |
| 3 | $$Z(x_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \cdot Z(x_i)$$ | **Kriging Linear Estimator** | $\lambda_i$ = Kriging weights (sum to 1, unbiased) |
| 4 | $$\gamma(h) = \frac{1}{2 N(h)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(h)} \left[Z(x_i) - Z(x_i + h)\right]^2$$ | **Empirical Semivariogram** | $\gamma(h)$ = semivariance at lag $h$; $N(h)$ = pair count at lag $h$ |
| 5 | $$\gamma(h) = C_0 + C \left(1 - e^{-\frac{3h}{a}}\right)$$ | **Exponential Semivariogram Model** | $C_0$ = nugget; $C$ = sill − nugget; $a$ = range (meters) |
| 6 | $$\gamma(h) = C_0 + C \left(\frac{3h}{2a} - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{h}{a}\right)^3\right)$$ | **Spherical Semivariogram Model** | For $0 < h \leq a$; $C_0 + C$ = sill |
| 7 | $$\gamma(h) = C_0 + C \left(\frac{h}{a}\right) \quad \text{for } h \leq a; \quad = C_0 + C \text{ for } h > a$$ | **Linear Semivariogram Model** | Bounded at $h = a$ |
| 8 | $$S(x_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot R_i^2 \ln(R_i) + a + b \cdot x + c \cdot y$$ | **Thin-Plate Spline** | $R_i$ = distance; minimizes surface curvature |
| 9 | $$N_{\text{rows}} = \left\lceil \frac{Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}}{\text{cell\_size}} \right\rceil$$ | **Grid Row Count** | $N_{\text{rows}}$ dimensionless |
| 10 | $$N_{\text{cols}} = \left\lceil \frac{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}{\text{cell\_size}} \right\rceil$$ | **Grid Column Count** | $N_{\text{cols}}$ dimensionless |
| 11 | $$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(Z_{\text{obs},i} - Z_{\text{pred},i}\right)^2}$$ | **Prediction Accuracy Metric** | Lower RMSE = better model; units same as $Z$ |
| 12 | $$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert Z_{\text{obs},i} - Z_{\text{pred},i} \vert$$ | **Mean Absolute Error** | Less sensitive to outliers than RMSE |
| 13 | $$\text{ME} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(Z_{\text{pred},i} - Z_{\text{obs},i}\right)$$ | **Mean Error (Bias)** | ME ≈ 0 indicates unbiased estimator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipes / Absolute Values Rule Applied:** All absolute-value bars in the table above have been written using `\vert` (e.g., `\vert Z_{\text{obs},i} - Z_{\text{pred},i} \vert`) to preserve the Markdown table boundary integrity. KTU examiners frequently test MAE and ME definitions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In precision agriculture, *Convert to Grid* is the engine behind:
- **Variable Rate Technology (VRT):** Fertilizer spreaders, lime applicators, and seed planters use the generated prescription raster to dose each sub-field zone differently.
- **Yield Gap Analysis:** Combining multi-year yield grids reveals chronically low-productivity zones.
- **Irrigation Zoning:** Soil moisture grids drive drip-line scheduling decisions.
- **Drone & Satellite Imagery Calibration:** Field sample grids are used to "ground-truth" NDVI/EVI indices derived from remote sensing.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** All algebraic transitions and weighting logic are shown in full. No step is abbreviated.

### 3.1 Derivation of the IDW Estimator

**Problem Statement:** Given $n$ known sampled points $Z(x_i)$ at locations $x_i$ (for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$), estimate the value $Z(x_0)$ at an unmeasured location $x_0$.

**Step 1 — State the General Weighted-Average Hypothesis**

By Tobler's First Law, $Z(x_0)$ is approximated as a weighted sum of nearby samples:

$$\hat{Z}(x_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot Z(x_i)$$

**Step 2 — Define the Distance-Based Weight**

The influence $w_i$ of point $i$ on the prediction at $x_0$ is set **inversely proportional** to its Euclidean distance $d_i = \sqrt{(x_0 - x_i)^2 + (y_0 - y_i)^2}$, raised to a power $p$:

$$w_i^{\text{raw}} = \frac{1}{d_i^p}$$

The parameter $p$ (called the **power parameter**) controls how rapidly influence decays with distance:
- $p = 1$: linear decay (smooth surface, "hotspot" effect broad)
- $p = 2$: quadratic decay (typical default — sharp local influence)
- $p \to \infty$: nearest-neighbor behavior (the closest point dominates completely)

**Step 3 — Apply the Unbiasedness (Partition-of-Unity) Constraint**

To ensure the estimator is dimensionless and bounded, the raw weights are normalized so that they sum to **exactly 1**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad w_i = \frac{w_i^{\text{raw}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^{\text{raw}}} = \frac{1 / d_i^p}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} 1 / d_j^p}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Into the Estimator**

$$\hat{Z}(x_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{1 / d_i^p}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} 1 / d_j^p} \right) Z(x_i)$$

Equivalently, the closed-form IDW equation used in GIS software is:

$$\boxed{\hat{Z}(x_0) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{Z(x_i)}{d_i^p}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{d_i^p}}}$$

**Step 5 — Limit-Case Verification**

As $d_i \to 0$ (interpolation point coincides with sample), the weight $w_i \to 1$ and $\hat{Z}(x_0) \to Z(x_i)$, confirming the **exact interpolator property**.

As $p \to \infty$, the term with smallest $d_i$ dominates the numerator and denominator, reducing the estimator to a **Nearest-Neighbor** assignment.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Empirical Semivariogram (Kriging Prerequisite)

**Step 1 — Define Spatial Separation**

For a lag distance $h$, define $N(h)$ as the number of sample pairs $(x_i, x_i + h)$ separated by approximately $h$.

**Step 2 — Compute Squared Differences**

For each pair, compute the squared difference of attribute values:

$$\Delta Z_i(h) = Z(x_i + h) - Z(x_i)$$

**Step 3 — Average and Halve**

By the intrinsic stationarity hypothesis, the expected squared difference depends only on lag $h$:

$$\gamma(h) = \frac{1}{2 N(h)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(h)} \left[ Z(x_i + h) - Z(x_i) \right]^2$$

The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ is historical (Matheron, 1963) and ensures that the sill of $\gamma(h)$ equals the variance of the field when stationarity holds.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — IDW for a Soil pH Grid

**Problem:** Interpolate soil pH at point $P_0 = (100, 100)$ from the following four samples (pH measurements taken at field locations):

| Point | $x$ (m) | $y$ (m) | pH ($Z$) |
|-------|---------|---------|----------|
| $P_1$ | 80      | 90      | 5.8      |
| $P_2$ | 120     | 110     | 6.5      |
| $P_3$ | 90      | 130     | 6.1      |
| $P_4$ | 130     | 80      | 5.5      |

Use **IDW with power $p = 2$**.

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean Distances from $P_0$ to each $P_i$**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(100-80)^2 + (100-90)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 100} = \sqrt{500} = 22.36 \text{ m}$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(100-120)^2 + (100-110)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 100} = \sqrt{500} = 22.36 \text{ m}$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(100-90)^2 + (100-130)^2} = \sqrt{100 + 900} = \sqrt{1000} = 31.62 \text{ m}$$

$$d_4 = \sqrt{(100-130)^2 + (100-80)^2} = \sqrt{900 + 400} = \sqrt{1300} = 36.06 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $d_i^2$ Weights (Raw)**

$$w_1^{\text{raw}} = \frac{1}{(22.36)^2} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002000$$

$$w_2^{\text{raw}} = \frac{1}{(22.36)^2} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002000$$

$$w_3^{\text{raw}} = \frac{1}{(31.62)^2} = \frac{1}{1000} = 0.001000$$

$$w_4^{\text{raw}} = \frac{1}{(36.06)^2} = \frac{1}{1300} = 0.000769$$

**Step 3 — Normalize Weights (Sum to 1)**

$$\sum w_i^{\text{raw}} = 0.002000 + 0.002000 + 0.001000 + 0.000769 = 0.005769$$

$$w_1 = \frac{0.002000}{0.005769} = 0.3467$$

$$w_2 = \frac{0.002000}{0.005769} = 0.3467$$

$$w_3 = \frac{0.001000}{0.005769} = 0.1733$$

$$w_4 = \frac{0.000769}{0.005769} = 0.1333$$

**Verification:** $0.3467 + 0.3467 + 0.1733 + 0.1333 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 4 — Compute the Weighted pH Estimate**

$$\hat{Z}(P_0) = (0.3467)(5.8) + (0.3467)(6.5) + (0.1733)(6.1) + (0.1333)(5.5)$$

$$\hat{Z}(P_0) = 2.0109 + 2.2536 + 1.0571 + 0.7332$$

$$\boxed{\hat{Z}(P_0) = 6.055 \text{ pH}}$$

**Interpretation:** The interpolated pH at $(100, 100)$ is **6.05**, which is logically closer to the values of $P_1$ and $P_2$ (the two nearest points, each 22.36 m away) than to $P_4$ (the farthest at 36.06 m, holding pH 5.5). This confirms the IDW algorithm correctly captured spatial proximity weighting.

---

### 3.4 Cross-Validation RMSE Calculation (Hypothetical)

**Setup:** Leave-one-out cross-validation on $n = 20$ grid-sampled soil organic carbon (% SOC) points.

**Step 1 — For each point $i$ (1 to 20),** remove it, re-interpolate, predict at its location, and record the error.

**Step 2 — Compute RMSE** using the formula from Table 2.A. Suppose observed errors squared sum to $\sum (Z_{\text{pred}} - Z_{\text{obs}})^2 = 0.082$ (units: (SOC\%)²).

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{0.082}{20}} = \sqrt{0.0041} = 0.064 \text{ \% SOC}$$

**Conclusion:** Average prediction deviation ≈ **0.064% SOC** — acceptable for a variable with field-scale variance of ≈ 0.5% SOC.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Convert to Grid" as used in GIS-based precision agriculture. Mention any two applications.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** *Convert to Grid* is a spatial interpolation process in GIS that transforms discrete vector-based sample points (with attribute values such as soil pH, yield, moisture) into a continuous raster surface where each cell of a regular grid is assigned an estimated value computed from neighboring samples using mathematical weighting functions.

**Applications (1 mark — any two):**
1. Generation of soil nutrient prescription maps for **Variable Rate Fertilization (VRT)**.
2. Creation of yield maps from combine harvester GPS-tagged data.
3. Production of Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) for land leveling and drainage planning.
4. Mapping of crop health indices from gridded ground-truth samples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three interpolation methods used in "Convert to Grid" operations and state one merit and one demerit of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Method | Merit | Demerit |
|--------|-------|---------|
| **IDW** | Simple, no statistical assumptions needed | Produces "bullseye" artifacts around sample points |
| **Kriging** | Provides prediction uncertainty (variance map); statistically optimal | Requires semivariogram modeling; computationally heavy |
| **Spline** | Generates very smooth, aesthetically pleasing surfaces | May overshoot beyond sample range; not suitable for abrupt changes |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive IDW + Worked Numerical*

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Adapted from June 2023]**
*Explain the Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW) method of spatial interpolation with its mathematical formulation. Discuss the role of the power parameter $p$ on the resulting surface.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
IDW is a deterministic interpolation method that estimates the value at an unsampled location as a weighted average of values at surrounding sampled locations, with weights inversely proportional to a power of their separation distance.

**2. Mathematical Formulation (3 Marks):**

$$\hat{Z}(x_0) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{Z(x_i)}{d_i^p}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{d_i^p}}$$

where:
- $\hat{Z}(x_0)$ = predicted value at unsampled point $x_0$
- $Z(x_i)$ = known value at sampled point $x_i$
- $d_i$ = Euclidean distance between $x_0$ and $x_i$
- $p$ = power parameter (positive real number)
- $n$ = number of neighboring sampled points

**3. Effect of the Power Parameter $p$ (2 Marks):**

| Power $p$ | Effect on Surface |
|-----------|-------------------|
| $p = 1$ | Linear decay; smooth, gradual transitions; high influence even from moderately distant points. |
| $p = 2$ | Quadratic decay (default in most GIS); balances local detail and smoothness. |
| $p = 3$ | Strong decay; result approaches nearest-neighbor behavior; localized "bullseye" hotspots. |
| $p \to \infty$ | Result becomes identical to nearest-neighbor (Thiessen polygon). |

Higher $p$ → sharper local influence, more rugged surface.
Lower $p$ → smoother, more generalized surface.

**4. Conclusion (1 Mark):**
IDW is widely used in precision agriculture for generating soil and yield maps because of its simplicity, exact interpolation property, and intuitive parameter (only $p$ requires tuning).

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**
*Three soil samples with the following data are taken from a field. Estimate the soil nitrogen (kg/ha) at point $P_0$ using IDW with power $p = 2$.*

| Point | $x$ (m) | $y$ (m) | Nitrogen (kg/ha) |
|-------|---------|---------|------------------|
| $P_1$ | 0       | 0       | 180              |
| $P_2$ | 100     | 0       | 220              |
| $P_3$ | 50      | 100     | 200              |

**Point to estimate:** $P_0 = (50, 50)$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Distances (2 Marks):**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(50-0)^2 + (50-0)^2} = \sqrt{2500 + 2500} = \sqrt{5000} = 70.71 \text{ m}$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(50-100)^2 + (50-0)^2} = \sqrt{2500 + 2500} = \sqrt{5000} = 70.71 \text{ m}$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(50-50)^2 + (50-100)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 2500} = \sqrt{2500} = 50.00 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $d_i^2$ Raw Weights (2 Marks):**

$$w_1^{\text{raw}} = \frac{1}{(70.71)^2} = \frac{1}{5000} = 0.000200$$

$$w_2^{\text{raw}} = \frac{1}{(70.71)^2} = \frac{1}{5000} = 0.000200$$

$$w_3^{\text{raw}} = \frac{1}{(50.00)^2} = \frac{1}{2500} = 0.000400$$

**Step 3 — Normalize Weights (1.5 Marks):**

$$\sum w_i^{\text{raw}} = 0.000200 + 0.000200 + 0.000400 = 0.000800$$

$$w_1 = \frac{0.000200}{0.000800} = 0.25$$

$$w_2 = \frac{0.000200}{0.000800} = 0.25$$

$$w_3 = \frac{0.000400}{0.000800} = 0.50$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Estimated Nitrogen (1.5 Marks):**

$$\hat{Z}(P_0) = (0.25)(180) + (0.25)(220) + (0.50)(200)$$

$$\hat{Z}(P_0) = 45 + 55 + 100 = 200 \text{ kg/ha}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\hat{Z}(P_0) = 200 \text{ kg/ha}}$

**Interpretation:** Because $P_3$ is the closest sample (50 m) and equidistant from $P_1$ and $P_2$ (both 70.71 m), the interpolated value is heavily biased (50%) toward $P_3$'s nitrogen level of 200 kg/ha.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Kriging + Semivariogram*

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*Explain the Kriging method of spatial interpolation. With a neat sketch, describe the components of a semivariogram (nugget, sill, range).*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Kriging — Concept (2 Marks):**
Kriging is a **geostatistical** interpolation method developed by Georges Matheron (1963), named after Danie Krige. Unlike IDW, it considers both the distance and the **degree of spatial autocorrelation** between sample points, quantified through the **semivariogram**. It produces both a predicted surface and an associated **prediction variance map** (uncertainty surface).

**2. Mathematical Form (2 Marks):**

$$\hat{Z}(x_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \cdot Z(x_i)$$

subject to the **unbiasedness constraint** $\sum \lambda_i = 1$ and the **minimum variance** condition, yielding the Kriging system of linear equations solved for weights $\lambda_i$.

**3. Semivariogram Components (2 Marks):**

```
SEMIVARIOGRAM MODEL
GAMMA H
 |
 |        SILL EQUALS C0 PLUS C
 |___________________________  ---
 |                          / 
 |                        /
 |                      /
 |                    / RANGE a
 |__________________/_________________
 |NUGGET C0        /   H  AXIS LAG DISTANCE
 |________________/
 0
```

- **Nugget ($C_0$):** Micro-scale variation or measurement error at $h \to 0$. Ideally zero, but practically non-zero.
- **Sill ($C_0 + C$):** The plateau variance reached as $h$ increases. Approximates the total variance of the dataset.
- **Range ($a$):** The lag distance at which the semivariogram reaches the sill. Beyond this, samples are spatially uncorrelated.

**4. Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Kriging is the statistically optimal interpolation method when sufficient samples exist to model the semivariogram and is preferred for precision agriculture nutrient mapping.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**
*Given the following soil pH measurements and their pairwise separations, compute the empirical semivariogram for lag $h = 50$ m and fit a spherical model to estimate the semivariance at $h = 30$ m. State the model parameters.*

**Data:**

| Pair | Distance (m) | $\vert Z(x_i) - Z(x_j) \vert$ |
|------|--------------|-------------------------------|
| 1–2  | 50           | 0.4                           |
| 1–3  | 50           | 0.6                           |
| 2–3  | 50           | 0.2                           |

Assumed spherical model parameters (from prior data):
- Nugget $C_0 = 0.05$
- Sill $C_0 + C = 0.50$
- Range $a = 100$ m

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Squared Differences (1 Mark):**

$$\Delta Z_{12}^2 = (0.4)^2 = 0.16$$

$$\Delta Z_{13}^2 = (0.6)^2 = 0.36$$

$$\Delta Z_{23}^2 = (0.2)^2 = 0.04$$

**Step 2 — Compute Empirical Semivariance at $h = 50$ m (2 Marks):**

$$\gamma(50) = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 3} \left[ 0.16 + 0.36 + 0.04 \right] = \frac{0.56}{6} = 0.0933$$

**Step 3 — Apply Spherical Model at $h = 30$ m (2.5 Marks):**

For the spherical model with $h \leq a$:

$$\gamma(h) = C_0 + C \left[ \frac{3h}{2a} - \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{h}{a} \right)^3 \right]$$

Substituting $C_0 = 0.05$, $C = 0.45$, $a = 100$ m, $h = 30$ m:

$$\frac{h}{a} = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30$$

$$\frac{3h}{2a} = \frac{3 \times 30}{2 \times 100} = \frac{90}{200} = 0.45$$

$$\left( \frac{h}{a} \right)^3 = (0.30)^3 = 0.027$$

$$\frac{1}{2} (0.027) = 0.0135$$

$$\gamma(30) = 0.05 + 0.45 \times [0.45 - 0.0135]$$

$$\gamma(30) = 0.05 + 0.45 \times 0.4365$$

$$\gamma(30) = 0.05 + 0.1964$$

$$\boxed{\gamma(30) = 0.2464 \text{ pH}^2}$$

**Step 4 — State Model Parameters (1.5 Marks):**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Nugget ($C_0$) | **0.05 pH²** |
| Partial Sill ($C$) | **0.45 pH²** |
| Total Sill ($C_0 + C$) | **0.50 pH²** |
| Range ($a$) | **100 m** |

**Interpretation:** At distances less than 100 m, soil pH samples exhibit spatial autocorrelation; beyond 100 m, samples are essentially uncorrelated (variance = 0.50 pH²).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize weights** in IDW. The raw weights $\frac{1}{d_i^p}$ must be divided by their sum, OR the closed-form ratio formula must be used. (Lose up to 2 marks.)
> 2. **Wrong semivariogram formula:** Students often write $\gamma(h) = \frac{1}{N} \sum (Z_i - Z_{i+h})^2$ instead of the correct $\frac{1}{2N(h)}$. The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ is mandatory. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Confusing Sill with Nugget:** Sill = $C_0 + C$ (the plateau). Many students incorrectly call the nugget the sill.
> 4. **Missing units:** Always state units — e.g., kg/ha, %, m, pH² — especially in numerical answers.
> 5. **Not verifying $\sum w_i = 1$** in IDW normalization. Examiners expect a verification line.
> 6. **Mixing up Kriging vs IDW formulas:** IDW uses $d_i^p$ in the denominator; Kriging uses $\lambda_i$ weights from the semivariogram. Do not interchange.
> 7. **Ignoring CRS:** If the question specifies coordinates in meters, ensure distances use meters (not degrees). 1 degree of latitude ≈ **111 km**.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FINAL REVISION BULLETS — Read This Section the Night Before the Exam**

- ✅ *Convert to Grid* = **Vector-to-Raster interpolation**; the heart of precision agriculture prescription mapping.
- ✅ **Two main method families:** Deterministic (IDW, Spline) and Geostatistical (Kriging).
- ✅ **IDW formula** is a normalized inverse-distance weighted average. Power $p$ controls smoothness.
- ✅ **Kriging** requires a **semivariogram** with three key parameters: **Nugget ($C_0$), Sill ($C_0+C$), Range ($a$)**.
- ✅ **Semivariogram formula** ALWAYS has the factor of **½** in the denominator.
- ✅ **Grid cell size** determines spatial resolution. Smaller cells = higher resolution = larger file size.
- ✅ **Search radius** and **minimum neighbor count** are critical parameters; tune via cross-validation.
- ✅ **Accuracy metrics:** RMSE (squared-error-based), MAE (absolute-error-based), ME (bias).
- ✅ **Validation method:** Leave-one-out cross-validation is standard for sample sizes < 50.
- ✅ **Real-world output:** Prescription maps for fertilizer, lime, seed, irrigation; yield maps; soil EC maps.
- ✅ **Spatial Reference:** Always confirm CRS consistency (WGS-84 vs. local UTM) before interpolation.
- ✅ **Software tools:** ArcGIS (Geostatistical Analyst), QGIS (SAGA / GRASS plugins), R (`gstat`, `sf`).
- ✅ **Tobler's First Law of Geography:** "Everything is related to everything else, but near things are more related than distant things" — the philosophical foundation of all interpolation.
- ✅ **Edge effect:** Predictions near the boundary of the grid are less reliable due to fewer neighbors.
- ✅ **Anisotropy:** Kriging can handle direction-dependent variation; IDW cannot (in basic form).
- ✅ **For numerical problems in exams:** Always show (1) distance computation, (2) raw weights, (3) normalization (with verification $\sum w_i = 1$), (4) final weighted sum, (5) unit and interpretation.
- ✅ **Memorize the three semivariogram models:** Spherical, Exponential, Linear — and their boundary conditions ($h \leq a$ vs. $h > a$).
- ✅ **Exact interpolator property:** IDW, Kriging (ordinary), and Spline all reproduce observed values exactly at sample locations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interpolation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT861 - SENSORS, AI AND ROBOTICS IN PRECISION AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interpolation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Interpolation** is a numerical estimation technique used to construct new data points within the range of a discrete set of known data points. In the context of *Sensors, AI and Robotics in Precision Agriculture*, interpolation is the mathematical backbone for converting **discrete, sparse sensor readings** into **continuous spatial or temporal information** — for example, generating a full soil-moisture map of a farm from a limited number of in-field sensors, or reconstructing a smooth navigation path for an autonomous tractor between waypoints recorded by GPS.

Formally, given $n+1$ known data points $(x_0, y_0), (x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$, the interpolation problem seeks a function $f(x)$ such that $f(x_i) = y_i$ for all $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$, and $f(x)$ can be evaluated at any intermediate $x \in [x_0, x_n]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Interpolation is *distinct* from **Regression**. Interpolation **passes exactly through every known data point**; regression only approximates the trend. In precision agriculture, when sensor calibration data is exact, **interpolation is preferred over regression** for filling missing readings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a farmer walks across his 10-acre field and measures soil moisture at only **5 locations** (marked with flags). He needs a full-colour soil-moisture map to decide where to irrigate. He cannot afford to physically probe every square metre — that is impractical and expensive. Instead, he asks:

> *"Between flag #2 (moisture = 28%) and flag #3 (moisture = 35%), what is the moisture content at the halfway point?"*

Interpolation answers this question with mathematical precision. It is like **drawing the smoothest possible curve that passes exactly through every flag** — and then reading off the value at any spot in between. The denser the flags, the more accurate the curve.

A second analogy: think of interpolation as a **"mathematical ruler"** — given the positions and values at two end-points, it lets you "measure" (estimate) any point in between with reasonable accuracy.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning in Precision Agriculture Context |
|---|---|
| **Node / Data Point** | A sensor location (e.g., GPS-tagged soil-moisture reading) |
| **Interval** | The spatial gap between two adjacent sensors |
| **Interpolant** | The mathematical function $f(x)$ that estimates values inside the interval |
| **Degree of Interpolating Polynomial** | Higher degree ⇒ more curvature, more pass-through accuracy, but more oscillations |
| **Runge's Phenomenon** | Dangerous overshoot at the edges when using high-degree polynomials on evenly spaced data |
| **Extrapolation** | Estimating *outside* the data range — generally unreliable and **NOT** interpolation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used:** Spatial interpolation accuracy is commonly reported using **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** and **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**. Acceptable RMSE for soil-moisture interpolation in KTU reference problems: typically **< 5% volumetric water content**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Interpolation Methods (Hierarchy Used in Agricultural AI)

Interpolation methods are chosen based on **data density**, **smoothness requirement**, and **dimensionality**:

1. **Linear Interpolation** — Simplest, fastest, used for short gaps between sensors.
2. **Polynomial Interpolation** — Used for smooth sensor calibration curves.
3. **Newton's Forward / Backward Interpolation** — Used for *equally-spaced* data such as time-series sensor logs.
4. **Newton's Divided-Difference Interpolation** — Used for *unequally-spaced* field sensor data (very common in agriculture because sensor placements are rarely uniform).
5. **Lagrange Interpolation** — Closed-form, used when the polynomial is needed only once.
6. **Cubic Spline Interpolation** — Smooth path planning for autonomous robots / drones.
7. **Bilinear / Bicubic Interpolation** — Image resampling in drone-based NDVI maps.
8. **Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW)** — Geostatistical spatial interpolation for yield maps.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why use Newton's Divided Differences for agricultural sensor data?** Because field sensors are *almost never* placed at perfectly equal spacings. Trees, irrigation channels, and access roads force irregular layouts. Divided-differences gracefully handle this.
- **Why prefer Cubic Splines for path planning?** Linear interpolation creates sharp corners; a tractor cannot turn instantaneously. Cubic splines provide **continuous first and second derivatives**, yielding a smooth, physically-realizable trajectory.
- **Why limit polynomial degree?** High-degree polynomials on noisy sensor data produce **Runge's oscillations** — wild swings between data points. In practice, degree $\leq 5$ is used, or splines are preferred.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **must-memorize** formula bank for KTU ESE questions on this topic. Note: the absolute-value / division-bar symbol is written as `\vert` to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Method | Core Formula | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
