| 1 | **Linear Interpolation** | $y(x) = y_0 + \dfrac{(y_1 - y_0)}{(x_1 - x_0)}(x - x_0)$ | Filling one missing sensor value between two adjacent readings |
| 2 | **Lagrange Form** | $f(x) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n} y_i \prod_{\substack{j=0 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \dfrac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}$ | One-shot interpolation from 2, 3, or 4 known sensor points |
| 3 | **Newton's Divided-Difference** | $f(x) = f[x_0] + f[x_0,x_1](x-x_0) + f[x_0,x_1,x_2](x-x_0)(x-x_1) + \cdots$ | Unevenly spaced in-field sensors (most common in KTU problems) |
| 4 | **Newton's Forward (equal spacing $h$)** | $y(x) = y_0 + s\,\Delta y_0 + \dfrac{s(s-1)}{2!}\Delta^{2} y_0 + \dfrac{s(s-1)(s-2)}{3!}\Delta^{3} y_0 + \cdots$ where $s = \dfrac{x - x_0}{h}$ | Estimating near the **beginning** of a sensor log |
| 5 | **Newton's Backward (equal spacing $h$)** | $y(x) = y_n + s\,\nabla y_n + \dfrac{s(s+1)}{2!}\nabla^{2} y_n + \cdots$ where $s = \dfrac{x - x_n}{h}$ | Estimating near the **end** of a sensor log |
| 6 | **Cubic Spline (clamped, segment $i$)** | $S_i(x) = a_i + b_i(x-x_i) + c_i(x-x_i)^{2} + d_i(x-x_i)^{3}$ | Smooth trajectory for autonomous tractor / UAV |
| 7 | **Bilinear Interpolation** | $f(x,y) = (1-\alpha)(1-\beta)f_{00} + \alpha(1-\beta)f_{10} + (1-\alpha)\beta f_{01} + \alpha\beta f_{11}$ | Drone NDVI image pixel resampling |
| 8 | **Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW)** | $\hat{z}(x_0) = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{z_i}{d_i^{p}}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{d_i^{p}}}$ with $p = 2$ typical | Spatial interpolation of soil properties / yield maps |
| 9 | **Finite-Difference Operator** | $\Delta y_i = y_{i+1} - y_i$ | Building Newton's Forward table |
| 10 | **Divided-Difference Operator** | $f[x_i, x_j] = \dfrac{f(x_i) - f(x_j)}{x_i - x_j}$ | Building Newton's Divided-Difference table |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Soil-Moisture Mapping:** Sparse sensors across a field are interpolated into a continuous heat-map that drives **variable-rate irrigation** systems.
- **Yield Prediction:** Combine harvester yield monitors sample every 1–2 seconds; interpolation fills GPS dead-zones so a complete yield map is produced.
- **Drone NDVI Image Stitching:** When drone images are ortho-rectified and mosaicked, bilinear/bicubic interpolation is used to align pixel grids.
- **Autonomous Navigation:** GPS waypoints are sparse; cubic-spline interpolation creates a smooth, continuous, jerk-limited path that the tractor's steering controller can follow.
- **Sensor Calibration Curves:** Calibration data from laboratory instruments is interpolated into a continuous lookup table for the embedded controller.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Lagrange Interpolation for Soil-Moisture Sensor Data

> **[Problem Setup — Typical KTU Board Question]**
> A precision-agriculture field has three soil-moisture sensors at the following GPS-tagged locations:
> - Sensor A: $x_0 = 0$ m, $y_0 = 22\%$ volumetric water content (VWC)
> - Sensor B: $x_1 = 10$ m, $y_1 = 28\%$ VWC
> - Sensor C: $x_2 = 20$ m, $y_2 = 25\%$ VWC
>
> Using **Lagrange interpolation**, estimate the soil-moisture at $x = 12$ m (location of a dry patch the farmer wants to investigate).

#### Step 1 — Write the general Lagrange form for $n = 2$

For three points ($n=2$):

$$
f(x) = y_0 \cdot L_0(x) + y_1 \cdot L_1(x) + y_2 \cdot L_2(x)
$$

where each Lagrange basis polynomial is:

$$
L_i(x) = \prod_{\substack{j=0 \\ j \neq i}}^{2} \dfrac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute $L_0(x)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0(x) &= \dfrac{(x - x_1)(x - x_2)}{(x_0 - x_1)(x_0 - x_2)} \\[4pt]
       &= \dfrac{(x - 10)(x - 20)}{(0 - 10)(0 - 20)} \\[4pt]
       &= \dfrac{(x - 10)(x - 20)}{200}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute $L_1(x)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1(x) &= \dfrac{(x - x_0)(x - x_2)}{(x_1 - x_0)(x_1 - x_2)} \\[4pt]
       &= \dfrac{(x - 0)(x - 20)}{(10 - 0)(10 - 20)} \\[4pt]
       &= \dfrac{x(x - 20)}{-100} \\[4pt]
       &= -\dfrac{x(x - 20)}{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute $L_2(x)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_2(x) &= \dfrac{(x - x_0)(x - x_1)}{(x_2 - x_0)(x_2 - x_1)} \\[4pt]
       &= \dfrac{(x - 0)(x - 10)}{(20 - 0)(20 - 10)} \\[4pt]
       &= \dfrac{x(x - 10)}{200}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Assemble $f(x)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= 22 \cdot \dfrac{(x-10)(x-20)}{200} + 28 \cdot \left(-\dfrac{x(x-20)}{100}\right) + 25 \cdot \dfrac{x(x-10)}{200} \\[4pt]
     &= \dfrac{22(x-10)(x-20)}{200} - \dfrac{28\,x(x-20)}{100} + \dfrac{25\,x(x-10)}{200}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Simplify to a standard polynomial form

Multiplying out and combining (intermediate steps shown for full marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= \dfrac{22(x^{2} - 30x + 200)}{200} - \dfrac{28(x^{2} - 20x)}{100} + \dfrac{25(x^{2} - 10x)}{200} \\[6pt]
     &= \dfrac{22}{200}x^{2} - \dfrac{660}{200}x + \dfrac{4400}{200} - \dfrac{28}{100}x^{2} + \dfrac{560}{100}x + \dfrac{25}{200}x^{2} - \dfrac{250}{200}x \\[6pt]
     &= x^{2}\left(\dfrac{22}{200} - \dfrac{56}{200} + \dfrac{25}{200}\right) + x\left(-\dfrac{660}{200} + \dfrac{1120}{200} - \dfrac{250}{200}\right) + \dfrac{4400}{200} \\[6pt]
     &= x^{2}\left(\dfrac{-9}{200}\right) + x\left(\dfrac{210}{200}\right) + 22 \\[6pt]
     &= -\dfrac{9}{200}x^{2} + \dfrac{21}{20}x + 22
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7 — Evaluate at $x = 12$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(12) &= -\dfrac{9}{200}(144) + \dfrac{21}{20}(12) + 22 \\[4pt]
      &= -\dfrac{1296}{200} + \dfrac{252}{20} + 22 \\[4pt]
      &= -6.48 + 12.60 + 22.00 \\[4pt]
      &= 28.12\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **[Answer]** The estimated soil moisture at $x = 12$ m is **28.12 % VWC**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Newton's Divided-Difference Table (Uneven Sensor Spacing)

> A drone-mounted multispectral sensor recorded the canopy reflectance at four unevenly spaced points along a row of crop:
>
> | $i$ | $x_i$ (m) | $y_i$ (reflectance, unitless) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 0 | 0 | 0.42 |
> | 1 | 3 | 0.51 |
> | 2 | 7 | 0.48 |
> | 3 | 12 | 0.65 |
>
> Construct the divided-difference table and obtain the interpolating polynomial; then estimate reflectance at $x = 5$ m.

#### Step 1 — Build the first column (the $y$-values are $f[x_i]$)

$$
f[x_0] = 0.42,\quad f[x_1] = 0.51,\quad f[x_2] = 0.48,\quad f[x_3] = 0.65
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute first divided differences

$$
\begin{aligned}
f[x_0, x_1] &= \dfrac{0.51 - 0.42}{3 - 0} = \dfrac{0.09}{3} = 0.0300 \\[4pt]
f[x_1, x_2] &= \dfrac{0.48 - 0.51}{7 - 3} = \dfrac{-0.03}{4} = -0.0075 \\[4pt]
f[x_2, x_3] &= \dfrac{0.65 - 0.48}{12 - 7} = \dfrac{0.17}{5} = 0.0340
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute second divided differences

$$
\begin{aligned}
f[x_0, x_1, x_2] &= \dfrac{-0.0075 - 0.0300}{7 - 0} = \dfrac{-0.0375}{7} = -0.005357 \\[4pt]
f[x_1, x_2, x_3] &= \dfrac{0.0340 - (-0.0075)}{12 - 3} = \dfrac{0.0415}{9} = 0.004611
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the third divided difference

$$
\begin{aligned}
f[x_0, x_1, x_2, x_3] &= \dfrac{0.004611 - (-0.005357)}{12 - 0} = \dfrac{0.009968}{12} = 0.000831
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Write the Newton polynomial

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= f[x_0] + f[x_0,x_1](x-x_0) + f[x_0,x_1,x_2](x-x_0)(x-x_1) + f[x_0,x_1,x_2,x_3](x-x_0)(x-x_1)(x-x_2) \\[4pt]
     &= 0.42 + 0.0300(x) + (-0.005357)(x)(x-3) + (0.000831)(x)(x-3)(x-7)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Evaluate at $x = 5$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x-0)(x-3)(x-7)\big\vert_{x=5} &= (5)(2)(-2) = -20 \\[4pt]
(x)(x-3)\big\vert_{x=5} &= (5)(2) = 10 \\[4pt]
f(5) &= 0.42 + 0.0300(5) + (-0.005357)(10) + (0.000831)(-20) \\[4pt]
     &= 0.42 + 0.1500 - 0.05357 - 0.01662 \\[4pt]
     &= 0.4998
\end{aligned}
$$

> **[Answer]** Estimated canopy reflectance at $x = 5$ m is **0.500 (unitless)**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW) for a Yield Map

> Three harvest yield-monitor points are recorded around a target location $x_0$:
>
> | Point | Distance $d_i$ (m) | Yield $z_i$ (t/ha) |
> |---|---|---|
> | P1 | 10 | 5.2 |
> | P2 | 20 | 4.6 |
> | P3 | 25 | 6.1 |
>
> Estimate yield at $x_0$ using IDW with power parameter $p = 2$.

#### Step 1 — Compute weights

$$
w_i = \dfrac{1}{d_i^{2}}:\quad w_1 = 0.01000,\quad w_2 = 0.00250,\quad w_3 = 0.00160
$$

#### Step 2 — Sum of weights

$$
\sum w_i = 0.01000 + 0.00250 + 0.00160 = 0.01410
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute weighted yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i z_i &= 0.01000(5.2) + 0.00250(4.6) + 0.00160(6.1) \\
             &= 0.0520 + 0.0115 + 0.00976 \\
             &= 0.07326
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the IDW estimate

$$
\hat{z}(x_0) = \dfrac{0.07326}{0.01410} = 5.196\ \text{t/ha}
$$

> **[Answer]** Estimated yield at the unsampled location is **5.20 t/ha** (closest to the nearest point P1, as expected — IDW gives more weight to closer samples).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What in KTU Board Answers

| Situation in the Question | Recommended Method | Justification (must be written in exam) |
|---|---|---|
| Two known points, find one unknown | **Linear interpolation** | Simplest, exact, fastest |
| Three known points (equal spacing), one near the start | **Newton's Forward** | Uses forward difference table |
| Three known points (equal spacing), one near the end | **Newton's Backward** | Uses backward difference table |
| Three or four known points, **unequal spacing** | **Lagrange or Newton's Divided Difference** | Both handle unequal spacing; Lagrange is one-shot |
| Smooth curve through many points, no oscillation | **Cubic Spline** | Avoids Runge's phenomenon |
| Spatial interpolation across a field | **IDW or Kriging** | Geostatistical, respects distance weighting |
| Image pixel resampling | **Bilinear / Bicubic** | Standard 2-D grid interpolation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Interpolation Pipeline in a Precision-Agriculture Sensor System

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of how raw sparse sensor data is transformed into continuous actionable information (a yield map, an irrigation prescription, or a robot path) through an interpolation pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sparse Sensor Inputs] --> B[Data Pre-Processing]
    B --> B1[GPS Tagging]
    B --> B2[Outlier Removal]
    B --> B3[Timestamp Alignment]
    B1 --> C[Data Structuring]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D{Choose Interpolation Method}
    D -->|Linear| E1[Linear Interpolator]
    D -->|Polynomial| E2[Lagrange / Newton Engine]
    D -->|Spline| E3[Cubic Spline Engine]
    D -->|Spatial| E4[IDW or Kriging Module]
    D -->|Image| E5[Bilinear Resampler]
    E1 --> F[Continuous Field Map]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Downstream Application Layer]
    G --> G1[Variable Rate Irrigation]
    G --> G2[Autonomous Tractor Path]
    G --> G3[NDVI Crop Health Map]
    G --> G4[Yield Prediction Dashboard]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree for Selecting an Interpolation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose Interpolation Method] --> Q1{Are points equally spaced?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Near beginning, middle, or end?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{One-shot or iterative?}
    Q2 -->|Beginning| NF[Use Newton Forward]
    Q2 -->|Middle| NL[Use Newton Forward / Backward average]
    Q2 -->|End| NB[Use Newton Backward]
    Q3 -->|One-shot| LG[Use Lagrange]
    Q3 -->|Iterative / new data arriving| ND[Use Newton Divided Difference]
    Q1 -->|Need smooth curve through many points| CS[Use Cubic Spline]
    Q1 -->|Spatial / yield map| IDW[Use IDW or Kriging]
    Q1 -->|Image pixel resampling| BL[Use Bilinear or Bicubic]
```

### 4.3 Interpolation vs. Extrapolation — Boundary Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Point A: x0, y0] --- X1[Known Region]
    X1 --- P1[Point B: x1, y1]
    X1 --- P2[Point C: x2, y2]
    X1 --- P3[Point D: x3, y3]
    P3 --- EXT[Extrapolation Zone: UNRELIABLE beyond x3]
    P0 --- EXT2[Extrapolation Zone: UNRELIABLE before x0]
    style EXT fill:#ffcccc
    style EXT2 fill:#ffcccc
    style X1 fill:#ccffcc
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **green region** is the *safe interpolation domain* — between the leftmost and rightmost known data points. The **red regions** indicate **extrapolation**, which is mathematically possible but statistically unsafe and **not** part of the standard KTU interpolation syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
> **Q1.** Define **interpolation**. How is it different from **extrapolation**? Give **one** precision-agriculture application of each.

**Model Answer:**
Interpolation is the process of estimating an unknown value of a function *within* the range of its known data points, using a mathematical function that passes exactly through those known points. Extrapolation, in contrast, estimates values *outside* the known range.

In precision agriculture:
- **Interpolation example:** Estimating soil moisture at $x = 12$ m from sensors placed at $x = 0, 10, 20$ m.
- **Extrapolation example:** Predicting next week's crop yield based on a trend line fitted to last month's yield data — this is risky and is *not* interpolation.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
> **Q2.** State the **Lagrange interpolation formula** for $n+1$ data points. Why is it preferred for *one-time* interpolation of unevenly spaced sensor data?

**Model Answer:**

$$
f(x) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n} y_i \prod_{\substack{j=0 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \dfrac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}
$$

It is preferred because:
1. It is a **closed-form** expression — no need to construct a difference table.
2. It handles **unequal spacing** of sensor data natively.
3. It is **easy to program** for a small fixed number of points (typically $n \leq 4$ in board problems).
4. For *one-time* interpolation it is computationally cheaper than Newton's divided-difference scheme; however, if a new sensor reading arrives, Lagrange must be **completely recomputed**, unlike Newton's form.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Expected Style]`

---

#### **Question A — Lagrange Interpolation (14 Marks)**

**A.** (a) The following soil-moisture readings were recorded at four unevenly spaced locations in a precision-agriculture field:

| $i$ | $x_i$ (m) | $y_i$ (% VWC) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 20 |
| 1 | 4 | 28 |
| 2 | 9 | 22 |
| 3 | 16 | 30 |

(i) Construct the **Lagrange interpolating polynomial** of degree 3 that passes through all four points.  **(7 marks)**
(ii) Estimate the soil moisture at $x = 10$ m using the polynomial.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a)(i):**

The Lagrange polynomial is:

$$
f(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{3} y_i L_i(x)
$$

Compute each Lagrange basis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0(x) &= \dfrac{(x-4)(x-9)(x-16)}{(0-4)(0-9)(0-16)} = \dfrac{(x-4)(x-9)(x-16)}{-576} \\[4pt]
L_1(x) &= \dfrac{(x-0)(x-9)(x-16)}{(4-0)(4-9)(4-16)} = \dfrac{x(x-9)(x-16)}{288} \\[4pt]
L_2(x) &= \dfrac{(x-0)(x-4)(x-16)}{(9-0)(9-4)(9-16)} = \dfrac{x(x-4)(x-16)}{-315} \\[4pt]
L_3(x) &= \dfrac{(x-0)(x-4)(x-9)}{(16-0)(16-4)(16-9)} = \dfrac{x(x-4)(x-9)}{1344}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) = & \; 20 \cdot \dfrac{(x-4)(x-9)(x-16)}{-576} + 28 \cdot \dfrac{x(x-9)(x-16)}{288} \\
       & + 22 \cdot \dfrac{x(x-4)(x-16)}{-315} + 30 \cdot \dfrac{x(x-4)(x-9)}{1344}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution (a)(ii):**

Evaluate at $x = 10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0(10) &= \dfrac{(6)(1)(-6)}{-576} = \dfrac{-36}{-576} = 0.0625 \\[4pt]
L_1(10) &= \dfrac{(10)(1)(-6)}{288} = \dfrac{-60}{288} = -0.2083 \\[4pt]
L_2(10) &= \dfrac{(10)(6)(-6)}{-315} = \dfrac{-360}{-315} = 1.1429 \\[4pt]
L_3(10) &= \dfrac{(10)(6)(1)}{1344} = \dfrac{60}{1344} = 0.04464 \\[4pt]
f(10)   &= 20(0.0625) + 28(-0.2083) + 22(1.1429) + 30(0.04464) \\[4pt]
        &= 1.250 - 5.832 + 25.143 + 1.339 \\[4pt]
        &= 21.90\ \% \ \text{VWC}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **[Answer]** Estimated soil moisture at $x = 10$ m is **21.90 % VWC**.

---

#### **Question B — Newton's Divided Difference + IDW (14 Marks)**

**B.** (a) (i) Construct the **Newton's Divided-Difference** table for the following drone-recorded NDVI readings at unequal spacings: $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0.30)$, $(x_1, y_1) = (2, 0.42)$, $(x_2, y_2) = (5, 0.38)$, $(x_3, y_3) = (11, 0.55)$.  **(4 marks)**
(ii) Write down the corresponding **Newton's interpolating polynomial** in standard form.  **(3 marks)**

(b) Three ground-truth yield samples are located at distances 5 m, 12 m, and 20 m from an unsampled location, with yields 4.8, 5.5, and 4.2 t/ha respectively. Use the **Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW)** method with $p = 2$ to estimate yield at the unsampled location.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a)(i):**

First divided differences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f[x_0,x_1] &= \dfrac{0.42 - 0.30}{2 - 0} = 0.0600 \\[4pt]
f[x_1,x_2] &= \dfrac{0.38 - 0.42}{5 - 2} = -0.01333 \\[4pt]
f[x_2,x_3] &= \dfrac{0.55 - 0.38}{11 - 5} = 0.02833
\end{aligned}
$$

Second divided differences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f[x_0,x_1,x_2] &= \dfrac{-0.01333 - 0.0600}{5 - 0} = -0.01467 \\[4pt]
f[x_1,x_2,x_3] &= \dfrac{0.02833 - (-0.01333)}{11 - 2} = 0.004630
\end{aligned}
$$

Third divided difference:

$$
f[x_0,x_1,x_2,x_3] = \dfrac{0.004630 - (-0.01467)}{11 - 0} = 0.001754
$$

**Model Solution (a)(ii):**

$$
f(x) = 0.30 + 0.0600(x) + (-0.01467)(x)(x-2) + (0.001754)(x)(x-2)(x-5)
$$

**Model Solution (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 &= \dfrac{1}{5^{2}} = 0.04000, \quad w_2 = \dfrac{1}{12^{2}} = 0.006944, \quad w_3 = \dfrac{1}{20^{2}} = 0.002500 \\[6pt]
\sum w_i &= 0.04000 + 0.006944 + 0.002500 = 0.049444 \\[6pt]
\sum w_i z_i &= 0.04000(4.8) + 0.006944(5.5) + 0.002500(4.2) \\[4pt]
             &= 0.1920 + 0.03819 + 0.01050 = 0.24069 \\[6pt]
\hat{z} &= \dfrac{0.24069}{0.049444} = 4.868\ \text{t/ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **[Answer]** Estimated yield at unsampled location = **4.87 t/ha**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing interpolation with regression** — interpolation **must pass through every data point** exactly.
> 2. **Forgetting to check equal-spacing assumption** — using Newton's Forward/Backward on **unequal** data will yield wrong answers; KTU examiners frequently test this.
> 3. **Computational errors in divided-difference tables** — many students mistakenly use $\dfrac{y_{i+1}-y_i}{x_i}$ instead of the correct $\dfrac{y_{i+1}-y_i}{x_{i+1}-x_i}$. Lose 1 mark per error.
> 4. **Not simplifying the polynomial** — KTU awards a mark for the final simplified form $f(x) = ax^{3} + bx^{2} + cx + d$.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** — in Lagrange, always plug $x = x_i$ back in to verify $L_i(x_i) = 1$ and $L_j(x_i) = 0$ for $j \neq i$. Showing this earns a partial mark even if the final answer is wrong.
> 6. **Using IDW with $p = 0$ or $p = 1$ without justification** — typical KTU board convention is $p = 2$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Interpolation** estimates values *inside* the data range; **extrapolation** estimates outside (risky).
- ✅ **Linear interpolation** is the simplest; use it for short gaps and quick estimates.
- ✅ **Lagrange formula** is the most tested in KTU board exams for 2–4 point problems.
- ✅ **Newton's Forward Difference** is for **equally spaced, near-start** queries.
- ✅ **Newton's Backward Difference** is for **equally spaced, near-end** queries.
- ✅ **Newton's Divided Difference** is the **most flexible** — handles unequal spacing, common in field sensor data.
- ✅ **Cubic Splines** give smooth, oscillation-free curves; used in **robot/drone path planning**.
- ✅ **Bilinear interpolation** is used in **2-D image resampling** (drone NDVI maps).
- ✅ **IDW** is a **geostatistical spatial interpolation** technique used for **yield maps and soil property maps**; $p = 2$ is the default.
- ✅ **Runge's phenomenon** = oscillation when high-degree polynomials fit evenly-spaced noisy data — avoid in agriculture.
- ✅ Always **verify** $L_i(x_i) = 1$ after deriving Lagrange basis polynomials.
- ✅ Use **\vert** or **\mid** (not **\|**) inside markdown tables for absolute-value bars.
- ✅ Standard KTU answer format: state method → write formula → tabulate intermediate values → compute → box the final answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reclassification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT861 - SENSORS, AI AND ROBOTICS IN PRECISION AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reclassification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Reclassification** is a fundamental raster data manipulation operation in Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and Remote Sensing, in which the cell values of an existing raster dataset are systematically reassigned to new substitute values based on a user-defined lookup table, range rule, or threshold function.

In the context of **Precision Agriculture**, reclassification is a critical post-classification step applied to continuous surfaces (such as satellite-derived vegetation indices, soil moisture maps, or yield maps) to transform them into discrete, decision-ready thematic layers. It converts raw numerical pixel values into simplified categorical maps that directly support agronomic decision-making (e.g., variable rate fertilization, irrigation zoning, weed/pest hotspot identification).

The mathematical representation of a reclassification operation is given as a function:

$$
f : D_{old} \rightarrow D_{new}
$$

where $D_{old}$ is the original value domain of the raster, and $D_{new}$ is the transformed discrete domain.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of reclassification like sorting a fruit basket at a grocery store.**
>
> Imagine you have a basket containing 50 different individual fruits — apples, oranges, bananas, grapes, mangoes, etc. (this is your **classified raster** with 50 different land-cover classes). Before the grocery store can stack them on shelves and assign prices, the stock manager **reclassifies** them into just 3 broader categories:
> 1. **Citrus** (oranges, lemons)
> 2. **Tropical** (bananas, mangoes)
> 3. **Temperate** (apples, grapes, pears)
>
> This reclassification makes the data **simpler, faster to interpret, and ready for action** (pricing, stocking, billing). The fruits themselves don't change — only their *labels* do. Similarly, in precision agriculture, raw NDVI values (ranging from -1 to +1) are reclassified into meaningful categories like "Healthy Crop," "Stressed Crop," and "Bare Soil."

### Why Reclassification Matters in Precision Agriculture

- **Decision Simplification**: Converts thousands of continuous pixel values into a handful of agronomically meaningful zones.
- **Data Fusion Preparation**: Harmonizes rasters with different scales (e.g., a 0–255 satellite index with a 0–100% soil moisture map).
- **Cost Optimization**: Enables site-specific input application (e.g., spraying herbicide **only** on reclassified "weed-infested" zones, not the entire field).
- **Communication**: Field managers and farmers understand color-coded zonal maps far better than continuous raw values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Reclassification (KTU High-Yield)**
> - **NDVI Range**: $-1.0$ to $+1.0$ (dimensionless vegetation index)
> - **Reclassified Class Count**: Typically 3 to 7 zones for operational farm use
> - **Lookup Table Cardinality**: $|L|$ = number of unique old-to-new value mappings

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Reclassification

The reclassification process in a GIS environment operates on each pixel of an input raster and substitutes its existing value with a new value from a user-specified rule. This is governed by the following structured logic:

1. **Identify the Source Raster**: A continuous or classified raster layer (e.g., NDVI, LAI, soil EC, yield data).
2. **Define the Reclassification Rule**: Either:
   - **One-to-one mapping** (e.g., class 3 → class 1)
   - **Many-to-one grouping** (e.g., classes 1, 2, 3 → all become "Vegetation")
   - **Range-based slicing** (e.g., NDVI values 0.0–0.2 → "Bare Soil")
   - **Threshold-based binary** (e.g., NDVI > 0.5 = 1, otherwise = 0)
3. **Construct a Lookup Table (LUT)**: A two-column table mapping *Old Value* to *New Value* (or *Old Range* to *New Value*).
4. **Apply the Function Pixel-by-Pixel**: The raster engine iterates through every cell, looks up the old value in the LUT, and writes the corresponding new value into the output raster.
5. **Validate and Visualize**: The output is rendered as a discrete-class map (typically with a qualitative color scheme).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1** ensures the input is a raster (grid-based) dataset; vector reclassification is a separate operation (dissolve/merge).
- **Step 2** gives the analyst complete control — the same NDVI raster can be reclassified differently for different stakeholders (an agronomist may want 5 classes, a drone pilot may want only 2: "spray" vs. "no-spray").
- **Step 3** is the heart of the operation; the LUT is the rulebook.
- **Step 4** is computationally efficient: it uses either an array-based lookup (vectorized in NumPy-style operations) or a hash-map for very large LUTs.
- **Step 5** is critical because a poorly reclassified map can lead to faulty agronomic decisions (e.g., missing a fungal outbreak zone).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all essential formulas, rules, and notations required to answer KTU exam questions on Reclassification.**

| **Symbol / Term** | **Definition / Formula** | **Unit / Domain** | **KTU Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f(v_{old})$ | Reclassification function: maps old pixel value to new value | Dimensionless | Core operation notation |
| $D_{old}$ | Original raster value domain | e.g., $[-1, +1]$ for NDVI | Source data range |
| $D_{new}$ | New (output) raster value domain | e.g., $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | Discrete class labels |
| NDVI | $\frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | Dimensionless $[-1, +1]$ | Vegetation vigor |
| SAVI | $\frac{(NIR - Red)}{(NIR + Red + L)} \cdot (1+L)$ | Dimensionless $[-1, +1]$, $L = 0.5$ | Sparse canopy correction |
| EVI | $2.5 \cdot \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + 6 \cdot Red - 7.5 \cdot Blue + 1}$ | Dimensionless $[-1, +1]$ | Dense canopy index |
| $LUT$ | Lookup Table: $\{ (v_i^{old}, v_i^{new}) \vert i = 1, 2, \dots, n \}$ | Set of ordered pairs | Rule definition |
| $\vert L \vert$ | Cardinality of LUT (number of unique mappings) | Integer | LUT size |
| Threshold rule | $f(v) = 1$ if $v > T$, else $0$ | Binary output | Stress detection |
| Range rule | $f(v) = k$ if $v \in [a_k, b_k]$ | Discrete $k$ | Zoning |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In precision agriculture engineering, reclassification is the **bridge between data science and farm machinery**. For example:

- A **variable-rate fertilizer spreader** receives a reclassified prescription map (typically 3–5 management zones) and modulates its application rate zone-by-zone.
- A **smart sprayer** uses a binary reclassified weed-map (weed = 1, no weed = 0) to actuate nozzles only over weed pixels, reducing chemical usage by up to **70%**.
- A **drone-based multispectral survey** produces an NDVI raster that is reclassified into "Red Zone (Stressed)," "Yellow Zone (Moderate)," and "Green Zone (Healthy)" for an at-a-glance dashboard view on the farmer's tablet.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: NDVI to Management-Zone Reclassification

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style)**:
A farmer's field has been imaged by a drone-mounted multispectral camera. The computed NDVI raster of the field (5×5 grid subset shown below) has continuous float values between $-0.2$ and $+0.9$. Apply the following reclassification rule to produce a 5-class management-zone map:

$$
\begin{cases}
v < 0.0 & \rightarrow \text{Class 1 (Water / Non-Vegetation)} \\
0.0 \le v < 0.2 & \rightarrow \text{Class 2 (Bare Soil)} \\
0.2 \le v < 0.4 & \rightarrow \text{Class 3 (Sparse Vegetation)} \\
0.4 \le v < 0.6 & \rightarrow \text{Class 4 (Moderate Vegetation)} \\
v \ge 0.6 & \rightarrow \text{Class 5 (Dense / Healthy Vegetation)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Input NDVI Raster (5×5)**:

$$
\text{NDVI} = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.15 & 0.05 & 0.18 & 0.32 & 0.55 \\
0.10 & 0.25 & 0.42 & 0.68 & 0.75 \\
-0.05 & 0.15 & 0.38 & 0.52 & 0.81 \\
0.20 & 0.30 & 0.45 & 0.62 & 0.70 \\
0.05 & 0.22 & 0.48 & 0.58 & 0.88
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Lookup Table (LUT) Construction**:

$$
LUT = \begin{cases}
(-\infty, 0.0) & \rightarrow 1 \\
[0.0, 0.2) & \rightarrow 2 \\
[0.2, 0.4) & \rightarrow 3 \\
[0.4, 0.6) & \rightarrow 4 \\
[0.6, +\infty) & \rightarrow 5
\end{cases}
$$

**Step-by-Step Application (Pixel-by-Pixel Iteration)**:

We now apply the function $f(v)$ to each element of the NDVI matrix.

**Row 1 Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(-0.15) &= 1 \quad (\text{since } -0.15 < 0.0) \\
f(0.05) &= 2 \quad (\text{since } 0.0 \le 0.05 < 0.2) \\
f(0.18) &= 2 \quad (\text{since } 0.0 \le 0.18 < 0.2) \\
f(0.32) &= 3 \quad (\text{since } 0.2 \le 0.32 < 0.4) \\
f(0.55) &= 4 \quad (\text{since } 0.4 \le 0.55 < 0.6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 2 Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(0.10) &= 2 \quad (\text{since } 0.0 \le 0.10 < 0.2) \\
f(0.25) &= 3 \quad (\text{since } 0.2 \le 0.25 < 0.4) \\
f(0.42) &= 4 \quad (\text{since } 0.4 \le 0.42 < 0.6) \\
f(0.68) &= 5 \quad (\text{since } 0.68 \ge 0.6) \\
f(0.75) &= 5 \quad (\text{since } 0.75 \ge 0.6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 3 Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(-0.05) &= 1 \quad (\text{since } -0.05 < 0.0) \\
f(0.15) &= 2 \quad (\text{since } 0.0 \le 0.15 < 0.2) \\
f(0.38) &= 3 \quad (\text{since } 0.2 \le 0.38 < 0.4) \\
f(0.52) &= 4 \quad (\text{since } 0.4 \le 0.52 < 0.6) \\
f(0.81) &= 5 \quad (\text{since } 0.81 \ge 0.6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 4 Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(0.20) &= 3 \quad (\text{since } 0.2 \le 0.20 < 0.4) \\
f(0.30) &= 3 \quad (\text{since } 0.2 \le 0.30 < 0.4) \\
f(0.45) &= 4 \quad (\text{since } 0.4 \le 0.45 < 0.6) \\
f(0.62) &= 5 \quad (\text{since } 0.62 \ge 0.6) \\
f(0.70) &= 5 \quad (\text{since } 0.70 \ge 0.6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 5 Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(0.05) &= 2 \quad (\text{since } 0.0 \le 0.05 < 0.2) \\
f(0.22) &= 3 \quad (\text{since } 0.2 \le 0.22 < 0.4) \\
f(0.48) &= 4 \quad (\text{since } 0.4 \le 0.48 < 0.6) \\
f(0.58) &= 4 \quad (\text{since } 0.4 \le 0.58 < 0.6) \\
f(0.88) &= 5 \quad (\text{since } 0.88 \ge 0.6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Reclassified Output Raster (5×5)**:

$$
\text{Output} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 5 \\
1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
3 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 5 \\
2 & 3 & 4 & 4 & 5
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Class Distribution Summary

| **Class ID** | **Class Label** | **NDVI Range** | **Pixel Count** | **Coverage (%)** | **Agri-Action** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water / Non-Veg | $< 0.0$ | 2 | 8% | Skip / No action |
| 2 | Bare Soil | $[0.0, 0.2)$ | 6 | 24% | Investigate / Replant |
| 3 | Sparse Veg | $[0.2, 0.4)$ | 7 | 28% | Boost irrigation / N |
| 4 | Moderate Veg | $[0.4, 0.6)$ | 6 | 24% | Maintain schedule |
| 5 | Dense / Healthy | $\ge 0.6$ | 4 | 16% | No action (optimal) |
| **Total** | — | — | **25** | **100%** | — |

### 3.3 Boundary Condition Verification

- The lower boundary $v = 0.0$ falls into **Class 2** (closed interval on the left).
- The upper boundary $v = 0.6$ falls into **Class 5** (closed interval on the left).
- All intervals are **mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive** — no pixel is left unclassified.
- This guarantees $f$ is a **well-defined function** from $D_{old}$ to $D_{new}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reclassification Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Multispectral Image] --> B[Radiometric Calibration]
    B --> C[Atmospheric Correction]
    C --> D[Compute NDVI / EVI Raster]
    D --> E[Define Reclassification LUT]
    E --> F[Apply Lookup Function f v]
    F --> G[Reclassified Management Zone Map]
    G --> H[Decision Support System]
    H --> I[Variable Rate Controller]
    I --> J[Farm Machinery Action]
    J --> K[Feedback Loop Yield Monitoring]
    K --> D

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#ffe1f5
    style F fill:#e1ffe1
    style G fill:#f5e1ff
    style J fill:#ffe1e1
```

### 4.2 NDVI-to-Zone Reclassification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Input NDVI Pixel Value v] --> Q1{v less than 0.0}
    Q1 -- Yes --> C1[Class 1: Water / Non-Vegetation]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{v less than 0.2}
    Q2 -- Yes --> C2[Class 2: Bare Soil]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{v less than 0.4}
    Q3 -- Yes --> C3[Class 3: Sparse Vegetation]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{v less than 0.6}
    Q4 -- Yes --> C4[Class 4: Moderate Vegetation]
    Q4 -- No --> C5[Class 5: Dense Healthy Vegetation]
    C1 --> Out[Output Categorical Map]
    C2 --> Out
    C3 --> Out
    C4 --> Out
    C5 --> Out

    style Start fill:#e1f5ff
    style C1 fill:#a0c4ff
    style C2 fill:#ffadad
    style C3 fill:#ffd6a5
    style C4 fill:#caffbf
    style C5 fill:#2d6a4f
    style Out fill:#f5e1ff
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Precision Agriculture Reclassification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Sensor Input Layer]
        s1[Drone Multispectral Camera]
        s2[Satellite Sentinel 2]
        s3[Ground Soil EC Sensor]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Data Processing Layer]
        p1[Image Preprocessing]
        p2[Index Calculation NDVI EVI SAVI]
        p3[LUT Engine]
        p4[Reclassification Module]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Decision Output Layer]
        o1[Zonal Prescription Map]
        o2[Variable Rate Controller]
        o3[Farmer Dashboard Mobile App]
    end

    s1 --> p1
    s2 --> p1
    s3 --> p1
    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 --> p4
    p4 --> o1
    o1 --> o2
    o1 --> o3

    style InputLayer fill:#e1f5ff
    style ProcessingLayer fill:#fff4e1
    style OutputLayer fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **LUT Engine** is the heart of the reclassification module. In a software implementation, it can be realized using array-based masking operations in GIS platforms (QGIS, ArcGIS) or using simple boolean masking (NumPy `np.where` chains) in Python.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
Define reclassification in the context of GIS-based precision agriculture. List any two situations in which a precision-agriculture engineer would reclassify an NDVI raster.

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
*Reclassification* is the raster operation in which existing cell values of a dataset are systematically reassigned to new substitute values based on a user-defined rule, range, or lookup table. (2 marks)

Two situations in precision agriculture:
1. To group detailed land-cover classes into broader management zones (e.g., merging 10 crop types into 3 vigor classes for variable-rate fertilization).
2. To convert continuous NDVI values into binary stress/no-stress masks for site-specific pesticide spraying by UAVs. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
Differentiate between **one-to-one** and **many-to-one** reclassification. Give one precision-agriculture example for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **One-to-one reclassification**: Each unique old value is mapped to exactly one new value (a 1:1 substitution). Example: Renumbering a classified land-cover map (class 3 → 1, class 4 → 2) for database normalization. (1.5 marks)
- **Many-to-one reclassification**: Multiple old values (or ranges) are collapsed into a single new value. Example: Combining NDVI values in the range $0.0$–$0.2$ and $0.2$–$0.4$ into a single "Stressed" class for an alert dashboard. (1.5 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the conceptual workflow of reclassification in a precision agriculture decision-support system. Include a labelled block diagram and describe the role of the Lookup Table (LUT). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A precision-agriculture engineer collects a soil Electrical Conductivity (EC) raster with values (in dS/m) as follows for a 4×4 sample block:

$$
EC = \begin{bmatrix}
0.8 & 1.2 & 2.5 & 3.8 \\
1.0 & 1.8 & 2.9 & 4.5 \\
0.6 & 1.5 & 3.2 & 4.2 \\
1.1 & 2.0 & 3.5 & 5.0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Reclassify this raster into 3 salinity classes using: $EC < 2.0$ → Class 1 (Non-saline); $2.0 \le EC < 4.0$ → Class 2 (Moderately saline); $EC \ge 4.0$ → Class 3 (Highly saline). Show the output raster and compute the percentage area of each class. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**:

1. **Input raster acquisition** from UAV / satellite / proximal sensor (1 mark).
2. **Index computation** (e.g., NDVI, EC, moisture) producing a continuous raster (1 mark).
3. **LUT construction** by the agronomist/engineer based on field-specific thresholds (1 mark).
4. **Pixel-by-pixel application** of the function $f(v_{old}) = v_{new}$ (1 mark).
5. **Output zonal map** is generated with discrete integer class codes (1 mark).
6. **Decision support integration**: the zonal map drives variable-rate applicators and dashboards (1 mark).
7. **Diagram**: Block diagram showing sensor → index → LUT → reclassification → prescription map (1 mark).

The **Lookup Table (LUT)** is the rulebook that maps each old value (or range) to a new value. It can be implemented as a hash-table, an array, or a set of nested boolean masks, depending on the GIS software used.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**:

Applying the rule $f(v)$ to each cell:

**Row 1:**
- $f(0.8) = 1$
- $f(1.2) = 1$
- $f(2.5) = 2$
- $f(3.8) = 2$

**Row 2:**
- $f(1.0) = 1$
- $f(1.8) = 1$
- $f(2.9) = 2$
- $f(4.5) = 3$

**Row 3:**
- $f(0.6) = 1$
- $f(1.5) = 1$
- $f(3.2) = 2$
- $f(4.2) = 3$

**Row 4:**
- $f(1.1) = 1$
- $f(2.0) = 2$ (closed on the left)
- $f(3.5) = 2$
- $f(5.0) = 3$

**Reclassified Output (4×4)**:

$$
\text{Output} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 2 & 2 \\
1 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\
1 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\
1 & 2 & 2 & 3
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Class Distribution Calculation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class 1 count} &= 8 \text{ pixels} \Rightarrow \frac{8}{16} \times 100\% = 50\% \\
\text{Class 2 count} &= 5 \text{ pixels} \Rightarrow \frac{5}{16} \times 100\% = 31.25\% \\
\text{Class 3 count} &= 3 \text{ pixels} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{16} \times 100\% = 18.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Agronomic Interpretation**:
- 50% of the sampled area is non-saline (suitable for salt-sensitive crops like beans/maize).
- 31.25% is moderately saline (suitable for barley/cotton with gypsum amendment).
- 18.75% is highly saline (requires salt-tolerant varieties or reclamation).

*(1 mark for output raster + 2 marks for percentage calculations + 1 mark for agronomic interpretation = 4 marks of working + 3 marks for logic and rule application)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare the reclassification of a **continuous raster** (e.g., NDVI) versus a **categorical raster** (e.g., a supervised land-cover map). What unique challenges arise in each case? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An NDVI image of a sugarcane field is reclassified using the following rule:

$$
\begin{cases}
v < 0.1 & \rightarrow \text{Class 1: Bare/Water} \\
0.1 \le v < 0.3 & \rightarrow \text{Class 2: Poor} \\
0.3 \le v < 0.5 & \rightarrow \text{Class 3: Average} \\
0.5 \le v < 0.7 & \rightarrow \text{Class 4: Good} \\
v \ge 0.7 & \rightarrow \text{Class 5: Excellent}
\end{cases}
$$

If the reclassified histogram shows pixel counts of $N_1 = 120$, $N_2 = 350$, $N_3 = 800$, $N_4 = 600$, $N_5 = 130$, calculate (i) total pixels, (ii) the dominant class and its percentage, and (iii) the cumulative percentage of "Average or better" zones. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**:

| **Aspect** | **Continuous Raster (e.g., NDVI)** | **Categorical Raster (e.g., land cover)** |
|---|---|---|
| Value domain | Float / continuous (e.g., $[-1, +1]$) | Integer codes (e.g., $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$) |
| LUT type | Range-based (e.g., $[0.2, 0.4) \rightarrow 3$) | One-to-one or many-to-one (e.g., $\{1,2\} \rightarrow 1$) |
| Threshold selection | Requires agronomic expertise & ground truth | Class codes are fixed after classification |
| Challenge 1 | **Choosing thresholds** — wrong cutoffs lead to mis-zoning | **Class heterogeneity** — reclassifying hides intra-class variability |
| Challenge 2 | **Edge effects** at boundary values (use closed-open intervals) | **Semantic ambiguity** — what to merge is context-dependent |
| Challenge 3 | Sensitivity to sensor noise; may need smoothing first | Risk of over-generalization (information loss) |

*(3.5 marks for comparison + 3.5 marks for challenges with examples)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**:

**(i) Total pixels** (1 mark):

$$
N_{total} = N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + N_4 + N_5
$$

$$
N_{total} = 120 + 350 + 800 + 600 + 130 = 2000 \text{ pixels}
$$

**(ii) Dominant class** (3 marks):

The maximum count is $N_3 = 800$ (Class 3: Average).

$$
\text{Percentage of Class 3} = \frac{800}{2000} \times 100\% = 40\%
$$

**Dominant Class = Class 3 (Average)** with **40%** coverage.

**(iii) Cumulative "Average or better"** (3 marks):

"Average or better" includes Class 3, Class 4, and Class 5.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\ge Avg} &= N_3 + N_4 + N_5 \\
N_{\ge Avg} &= 800 + 600 + 130 = 1530
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Cumulative \%} = \frac{1530}{2000} \times 100\% = 76.5\%
$$

**Conclusion**: 76.5% of the field is at average vigor or better, indicating overall healthy crop growth with 23.5% of the area needing intervention.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary convention**: Always state whether the boundary value (e.g., NDVI = 0.2) belongs to the lower or upper class. KTU examiners check this.
> 2. **Unit omission**: When working with EC, pH, or moisture, **always include units** (dS/m, %, etc.). A missing unit costs 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Total pixel mismatch**: The sum of reclassified class counts must equal the input pixel count. If it doesn't, the student has either double-counted or missed a pixel — partial marks will be cut.
> 4. **No diagram in 7-mark theory questions**: A block diagram or flowchart is **mandatory** for full marks on workflow questions.
> 5. **Confusing reclassification with classification**: Classification creates classes from raw data; reclassification modifies an already-classified or continuous raster. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Reclassification**
>
> - **Definition**: Reclassification = reassigning cell values of an existing raster to new values via a lookup table.
> - **Function notation**: $f : D_{old} \rightarrow D_{new}$.
> - **Four rule types**: one-to-one, many-to-one, range-based, threshold (binary).
> - **NDVI formula**: $\text{NDVI} = \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$, range $[-1, +1]$.
> - **LUT** is the heart of the operation; it is rule-based and user-defined.
> - **Pixel-by-pixel** application; the function must be **well-defined** (mutually exclusive + collectively exhaustive intervals).
> - **Boundary convention**: state clearly whether boundary goes to lower or upper class.
> - **Agri-utility**: variable-rate fertilization, smart spraying, irrigation zoning, yield gap analysis.
> - **Validation step**: always cross-check class counts sum to total pixels.
> - **Difference from classification**: Reclassification works on **already-existing** rasters; classification works on **raw** imagery.
> - **Tools**: QGIS (r.reclass), ArcGIS (Reclassify tool), Google Earth Engine (`remap`), GRASS GIS.
> - **Common KTU keyword tags**: "management zone," "lookup table," "decision support," "variable rate," "threshold-based zoning."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Band Math
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT861 - SENSORS, AI AND ROBOTICS IN PRECISION AGRICULTURE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Band Math ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Band Math** is a pixel-level mathematical operation performed on multispectral or hyperspectral remote sensing imagery, where arithmetic, logarithmic, or trigonometric functions are applied directly to the **Digital Number (DN)** or **surface reflectance** values of two or more spectral bands to generate a new derived image (often called a *derived band* or *spectral index*). In the context of precision agriculture, Band Math is the computational backbone for transforming raw radiometric data into biophysically meaningful indices such as vegetation vigor, chlorophyll content, water status, and soil exposure maps. Formally, for a given pixel with coordinates $(x, y)$, if $B_k(x,y)$ denotes the reflectance (or DN) of the $k^{th}$ spectral band, then any Band Math operation produces a new pixel value: $$ I(x, y) = f\bigl(B_1(x,y), B_2(x,y), \ldots, B_n(x,y)\bigr) $$ where $f(\cdot)$ is a user-defined scalar or vector function and $I(x,y)$ is the resulting **spectral index image**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEAGT861 – Module 3):** > Band Math is treated as the *bridge* between raw multi-band imagery and **decision-ready agronomic intelligence**. Students must understand the physical meaning of common indices, the choice of bands, and their crop-physiology interpretation. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Mixing Paints to Read Plant Health" Imagine you are a chef holding a small bottle of each primary colour of paint (Red, Green, Blue, Near-Infrared). Each colour represents one **spectral band** of reflected light from a crop field. By themselves, a single colour tells you very little about whether the plants are healthy, thirsty, or nutrient-deficient. However, if you *mix* these colours in carefully chosen ratios — for example, *"lots of NIR minus a little Red, divided by their sum"* — the resulting mixture changes hue dramatically depending on the plant's health. That final "mixed colour" is essentially what **Band Math** computes. Instead of paint, it uses numerical reflectance values; instead of a brush, it uses algebraic formulas. The "painted image" is your new index map highlighting only the agronomic feature of interest. ### 1.3 Why Band Math Matters in Precision Agriculture - **Dimensionality reduction:** Compresses information from 4–200+ bands into a single decision-supportive image. - **Atmospheric compensation tolerance:** Ratios and normalized differences partially cancel multiplicative atmospheric effects. - **Biophysical correlation:** Many indices are linearly or exponentially related to **LAI (Leaf Area Index)**, biomass, chlorophyll, and water content. - **Sensor-agnostic:** The same mathematical operator works on Sentinel-2, Landsat-9, drone multispectral, and hyperspectral cubes. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Spectral Bands Used in Agriculture (Wavelength Ranges):** > - **Blue (B):** **0.45 – 0.52 µm** > - **Green (G):** **0.52 – 0.60 µm** > - **Red (R):** **0.63 – 0.69 µm** > - **Red Edge (RE):** **0.70 – 0.75 µm** > - **Near-Infrared (NIR):** **0.76 – 0.90 µm** > - **Short-Wave Infrared (SWIR-1):** **1.55 – 1.75 µm** > - **Short-Wave Infrared (SWIR-2):** **2.08 – 2.35 µm** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation Band Math relies on three physical principles: 1. **Beer–Lambert Law of Radiation Interaction** — Vegetation reflectance is governed by leaf mesophyll scattering (high NIR) and chlorophyll absorption (low Red). The differential response between two bands therefore carries physiological information. 2. **Spectral Signature Uniqueness** — Each land-cover class (soil, water, vegetation, senescent crop) has a unique *fingerprint* across wavelengths. Mathematical combinations amplify these fingerprints. 3. **Linearization of Non-Linear Phenomena** — Ratios convert non-linear multiplicative noise (e.g., illumination) into additive offsets, simplifying downstream analysis. ### 2.2 Operational Logic Steps - **Step 1 — Acquisition:** Acquire a multi-band image (satellite / UAV / hyperspectral camera). - **Step 2 — Pre-processing:** Perform radiometric calibration, atmospheric correction (e.g., **6S**, **FLAASH**, **Sen2Cor**), and geometric correction. The result is *surface reflectance* (range 0.0 – 1.0). - **Step 3 — Band Selection:** Identify bands that respond differently to the target variable (e.g., Red + NIR for biomass; Red Edge + NIR for chlorophyll). - **Step 4 — Mathematical Combination:** Apply the chosen equation *pixel-by-pixel*. The arithmetic is identical for every pixel; only the input reflectance values differ. - **Step 5 — Clamping & Scaling:** Indices like NDVI are naturally bounded in $[-1, 1]$. Values are often rescaled to $[0, 255]$ for visualization (8-bit display). - **Step 6 — Classification / Decision Layer:** Threshold the index map (e.g., NDVI > 0.5 = healthy crop) or feed it into AI / machine-learning models for prescriptive analytics. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Index Name | Formula | Bands Used | Output Range | Primary Agronomic Use | |---|------------|---------|------------|--------------|------------------------| | 1 | **NDVI** (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) | $\dfrac{NIR - R}{NIR + R}$ | NIR, Red | $[-1, +1]$ | Green biomass, canopy vigour | | 2 | **GNDVI** (Green NDVI) | $\dfrac{NIR - G}{NIR + G}$ | NIR, Green | $[-1, +1]$ | Chlorophyll concentration (mid-late season) | | 3 | **NDRE** (Normalized Difference Red Edge) | $\dfrac{NIR - RE}{NIR + RE}$ | NIR, Red Edge | $[-1, +1]$ | Canopy chlorophyll at high LAI | | 4 | **EVI** (Enhanced Vegetation Index) | $2.5 \cdot \dfrac{NIR - R}{NIR + 6R - 7.5B + 1}$ | NIR, Red, Blue | $[-1, +1]$ | LAI in dense canopies; reduces soil/atmosphere noise | | 5 | **SAVI** (Soil-Adjusted Vegetation Index) | $1.5 \cdot \dfrac{NIR - R}{NIR + R + 0.5}$ | NIR, Red | $[-1, +1]$ | Sparse vegetation over bright soil | | 6 | **MSAVI** (Modified SAVI) | $\dfrac{2NIR + 1 - \sqrt{(2NIR+1)^2 - 8(NIR - R)}}{2}$ | NIR, Red | $[-1, +1]$ | Self-adjusting soil correction | | 7 | **NDWI** (Normalized Difference Water Index) | $\dfrac{G - NIR}{G + NIR}$ | Green, NIR | $[-1, +1]$ | Canopy/leaf water content | | 8 | **NDMI** (Normalized Difference Moisture Index) | $\dfrac{NIR - SWIR_1}{NIR + SWIR_1}$ | NIR, SWIR-1 | $[-1, +1]$ | Vegetation moisture stress | | 9 | **BSI** (Bare Soil Index) | $\dfrac{(SWIR_1 + R) - (NIR + B)}{(SWIR_1 + R) + (NIR + B)}$ | SWIR-1, R, NIR, B | $[-1, +1]$ | Exposed soil mapping (tillage, erosion) | | 10 | **CIgreen** (Chlorophyll Index – Green) | $\dfrac{NIR}{G} - 1$ | NIR, Green | $[0, \infty)$ | Leaf chlorophyll (linear correlation) | | 11 | **CIred-edge** (Chlorophyll Index – Red Edge) | $\dfrac{NIR}{RE} - 1$ | NIR, Red Edge | $[0, \infty)$ | Chlorophyll in dense canopies | | 12 | **SR** (Simple Ratio) | $\dfrac{NIR}{R}$ | NIR, Red | $[0, \infty)$ | Biomass (Rouse 1974) | | 13 | **TVI** (Triangular Vegetation Index) | $0.5 \cdot \bigl[120(NIR - G) - 200(R - G)\bigr]$ | NIR, R, G | numeric | Chlorophyll, LAI | | 14 | **WDRVI** (Wide Dynamic Range V.I.) | $\dfrac{0.1 \cdot NIR - R}{0.1 \cdot NIR + R}$ | NIR, Red | $[-1, +1]$ | High-biomass saturation mitigation | > [!NOTE] > **Universal Reminder:** All band variables in the formula sheet are **surface reflectance values** (unitless, between 0 and 1) when used in scientific studies. If raw **DN** values are used, atmospheric and radiometric corrections are mandatory. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Variable Rate Technology (VRT):** Index maps drive real-time nitrogen and irrigation prescriptions. - **Yield Prediction:** Time-series NDVI / EVI curves feed crop growth models (**DSSAT**, **AquaCrop**). - **Disease & Pest Detection:** Subtle pre-visual stress captured by Red Edge indices. - **Robotic Spot-Spraying:** Real-time Band Math on UAV/UGV-mounted cameras triggers nozzles only over weed pixels. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of NDVI from the Simple Ratio (SR) The **Simple Ratio (SR)**, introduced by Jordan (1969) and popularized by Rouse et al. (1974), is the parent index of NDVI. We start with the empirical observation that vegetation reflectance satisfies $NIR \gg R$. $$ SR = \frac{NIR}{R} $$ **Problem with SR:** It is unbounded above, non-linear, and not normally distributed, making statistical thresholds difficult. **Step 1 — Normalize the ratio.** Subtract 1 and add 1 symmetrically to compress the dynamic range: $$ \frac{NIR/R - 1}{NIR/R + 1} $$ **Step 2 — Simplify the compound fraction** by multiplying numerator and denominator by $R$: $$ \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R} $$ **Step 3 — Define the index.** This is the formal definition of **NDVI**: $$ NDVI = \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R} $$ **Step 4 — Boundedness proof.** Since $-1 \le \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R} \le 1$ for non-negative $NIR, R$, NDVI is naturally clamped. **Step 5 — Symmetry check.** For dense vegetation $NIR \gg R \Rightarrow NDVI \to 1$. For water $NIR \ll R \Rightarrow NDVI \to -1$. For bare soil $NIR \approx R \Rightarrow NDVI \approx 0$. ### 3.2 Derivation of SAVI from NDVI Huete (1988) recognized that NDVI is biased by **soil background brightness** in sparse canopies. He introduced a soil-brightness correction factor $L$ (default $L = 0.5$): $$ SAVI = \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R + L} \cdot (1 + L) $$ Substituting $L = 0.5$: $$ SAVI = \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R + 0.5} \cdot 1.5 $$ The factor $(1+L)$ rescales SAVI back into the $[-1, 1]$ domain for consistency with NDVI. ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Problem:** A drone multispectral camera records the following **reflectance** values for a single pixel of a paddy crop: $B = 0.04,\ G = 0.09,\ R = 0.05,\ RE = 0.32,\ NIR = 0.48$. Compute (a) NDVI, (b) GNDVI, (c) NDRE, (d) EVI, and (e) NDWI. **Given (Reflectance values):** $B = 0.04$, $G = 0.09$, $R = 0.05$, $RE = 0.32$, $NIR = 0.48$ **(a) NDVI Calculation:** $$ NDVI = \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R} $$ Substitute the given reflectance values: $$ NDVI = \frac{0.48 - 0.05}{0.48 + 0.05} $$ Compute the numerator first: $$ 0.48 - 0.05 = 0.43 $$ Compute the denominator next: $$ 0.48 + 0.05 = 0.53 $$ Divide the numerator by the denominator: $$ NDVI = \frac{0.43}{0.53} = 0.8113 $$ $$ \boxed{NDVI \approx 0.81} $$ This indicates a *highly vigorous, dense paddy canopy*. **(b) GNDVI Calculation:** $$ GNDVI = \frac{NIR - G}{NIR + G} $$ Substitute the given reflectance values: $$ GNDVI = \frac{0.48 - 0.09}{0.48 + 0.09} $$ Compute the numerator: $$ 0.48 - 0.09 = 0.39 $$ Compute the denominator: $$ 0.48 + 0.09 = 0.57 $$ Divide the numerator by the denominator: $$ GNDVI = \frac{0.39}{0.57} = 0.6842 $$ $$ \boxed{GNDVI \approx 0.68} $$ **(c) NDRE Calculation:** $$ NDRE = \frac{NIR - RE}{NIR + RE} $$ Substitute the given reflectance values: $$ NDRE = \frac{0.48 - 0.32}{0.48 + 0.32} $$ Compute the numerator: $$ 0.48 - 0.32 = 0.16 $$ Compute the denominator: $$ 0.48 + 0.32 = 0.80 $$ Divide the numerator by the denominator: $$ NDRE = \frac{0.16}{0.80} = 0.20 $$ $$ \boxed{NDRE = 0.20} $$ This relatively low NDRE confirms that even though canopy is dense (high NDVI), chlorophyll content in the deeper leaves is moderate. **(d) EVI Calculation:** $$ EVI = 2.5 \cdot \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + 6R - 7.5B + 1} $$ Substitute the given reflectance values: $$ EVI = 2.5 \cdot \frac{0.48 - 0.05}{0.48 + 6(0.05) - 7.5(0.04) + 1} $$ Compute the numerator inside the fraction: $$ 0.48 - 0.05 = 0.43 $$ Compute each term in the denominator: $$ 6R = 6 \times 0.05 = 0.30 $$ $$ 7.5B = 7.5 \times 0.04 = 0.30 $$ Sum the denominator: $$ 0.48 + 0.30 - 0.30 + 1 = 1.48 $$ Form the inner fraction: $$ \frac{0.43}{1.48} = 0.2905 $$ Multiply by 2.5: $$ EVI = 2.5 \times 0.2905 = 0.7264 $$ $$ \boxed{EVI \approx 0.73} $$ **(e) NDWI Calculation:** $$ NDWI = \frac{G - NIR}{G + NIR} $$ Substitute the given reflectance values: $$ NDWI = \frac{0.09 - 0.48}{0.09 + 0.48} $$ Compute the numerator: $$ 0.09 - 0.48 = -0.39 $$ Compute the denominator: $$ 0.09 + 0.48 = 0.57 $$ Divide the numerator by the denominator: $$ NDWI = \frac{-0.39}{0.57} = -0.6842 $$ $$ \boxed{NDWI \approx -0.68} $$ The strongly negative NDWI indicates this paddy pixel is dominated by vegetation, not open water. ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix | Calculated Value | Interpretation | Agronomic Action | |-------------------|----------------|-------------------| | NDVI $\in [0.7, 1.0]$ | Dense, healthy crop | Maintain current management | | NDVI $\in [0.4, 0.7]$ | Moderate growth | Schedule scouting | | NDVI $\in [0.0, 0.4]$ | Sparse vegetation or soil | Investigate irrigation / sowing | | NDVI $< 0.0$ | Water, cloud, or non-vegetation | Mask or exclude from analysis | | NDRE $< 0.2$ despite high NDVI | Chlorophyll stress in dense canopy | Top-dress nitrogen | | NDWI strongly negative in wet crop | Possible waterlogging | Check drainage | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Band Math Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Multispectral Image Acquisition
Sentinel-2 / UAV / Hyperspectral] A1[Radiometric Calibration
DN to Radiance] A2[Atmospheric Correction
6S / FLAASH / Sen2Cor] A3[Surface Reflectance Cube
Stacks of B G R RE NIR SWIR] B0[Band Selection Module
Choose Index-Specific Bands] B1[Band Math Engine
Pixel-wise Arithmetic] B2[Index Library
NDVI, EVI, NDRE, NDWI, SAVI, MSAVI] B3[Derived Index Image
Geo-referenced Tiff] C0[Thresholding Layer
Domain Specific Cutoffs] C1[AI ML Classifier
Random Forest CNN] C2[Decision Support Map
Prescription Zones] C3[Field Robot VRT Actuator
Sprayer Drone UGV] D0[Validation Layer
Ground Truth Chlorophyll Meter] A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 A3 --> B0 B0 --> B1 B1 --> B2 --> B3 B3 --> C0 C0 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 B3 --> D0 C2 --> D0 ``` ### 4.2 Concept Map of Major Band Math Indices ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Band Math Family] --> B1[Vegetation Indices] A --> B2[Water Indices] A --> B3[Soil Indices] A --> B4[Chlorophyll Indices] B1 --> C1[NDVI] B1 --> C2[EVI] B1 --> C3[SAVI] B1 --> C4[MSAVI] B1 --> C5[WDRVI] B2 --> D1[NDWI] B2 --> D2[NDMI] B3 --> E1[BSI] B3 --> E2[NBR] B4 --> F1[CIgreen] B4 --> F2[CIredEdge] B4 --> F3[GNDVI] B4 --> F4[MCARI] ``` ### 4.3 Pixel-wise Band Math Operational Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Loop over all pixels x y] P1[Read Band values
B G R RE NIR SWIR] P2[Apply Formula
Example NDVI] P3[Compute Index Value] P4[Store in Output Image] P5[Next Pixel] P6[End of Image] P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P0 P5 --> P6 ``` ### 4.4 Decision Tree for Index Selection (Agronomist's Quick Selector) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What is your goal?} Q1 -->|Green biomass| Q2{Canopy dense?} Q1 -->|Water stress| Q3[Use NDWI or NDMI] Q1 -->|Soil exposure| Q4[Use BSI] Q1 -->|Chlorophyll| Q5{LAI high?} Q2 -->|No| R1[Use NDVI or SAVI] Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Use EVI or WDRVI] Q5 -->|No| R3[Use GNDVI or CIgreen] Q5 -->|Yes| R4[Use NDRE or CIredEdge] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** *Define Band Math. List any four common spectral indices derived through Band Math in precision agriculture.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Band Math is a pixel-wise arithmetic operation performed on two or more spectral bands of a remote sensing image to generate a new derived image (index) that highlights a specific biophysical property. Four common indices: 1. **NDVI** – Normalized Difference Vegetation Index: $\frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R}$ 2. **NDWI** – Normalized Difference Water Index: $\frac{G - NIR}{G + NIR}$ 3. **EVI** – Enhanced Vegetation Index: $2.5 \cdot \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + 6R - 7.5B + 1}$ 4. **BSI** – Bare Soil Index: $\frac{(SWIR + R) - (NIR + B)}{(SWIR + R) + (NIR + B)}$ **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why are simple ratios and normalized differences preferred over single-band reflectance values in precision agriculture monitoring?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. Ratios and normalized differences **cancel out multiplicative noise** caused by varying illumination and atmospheric conditions, providing radiometric stability. 2. They **enhance the contrast** between vegetated and non-vegetated surfaces because healthy vegetation exhibits a strong differential response between Red (absorbed) and NIR (reflected) wavelengths. 3. They produce **bounded, normalized values** (typically in $[-1, 1]$) that are easier to threshold, visualize, and feed into decision-support algorithms. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Choice A]** **(a)** Derive the **NDVI** formula starting from the **Simple Ratio**. Explain the physical significance of using Red and NIR bands. (7 marks) **(b)** For a cotton field pixel, the surface reflectance values are: $R = 0.06$, $NIR = 0.42$, $G = 0.10$, $B = 0.04$, $RE = 0.30$. Compute **NDVI, GNDVI, NDRE, and SAVI**. Interpret the agronomic status of the pixel. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of NDVI (7 marks):** **Step 1** — Define the Simple Ratio (Jordan 1969, Rouse et al. 1974): $$ SR = \frac{NIR}{R} $$ This ratio exploits the physiological fact that chlorophyll in healthy mesophyll strongly **absorbs Red** light (low $R$) while the spongy mesophyll **scatters NIR** (high $NIR$). **Step 2** — Identify the drawback: SR is non-linear, unbounded, and non-Gaussian, making thresholding difficult. **Step 3** — Apply normalization by subtracting 1 in the numerator and adding 1 in the denominator: $$ NDVI = \frac{NIR/R - 1}{NIR/R + 1} $$ **Step 4** — Multiply numerator and denominator by $R$: $$ NDVI = \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R} $$ **Step 5** — Prove boundedness: for non-negative $NIR, R$, $-1 \le NDVI \le 1$. **Physical Significance:** - **Red band (0.63 – 0.69 µm):** Strongly absorbed by chlorophyll $a$ and $b$ — low reflectance implies high chlorophyll content. - **NIR band (0.76 – 0.90 µm):** Strongly scattered by leaf internal cell structure — high reflectance implies dense, healthy mesophyll. - The **difference** $(NIR - R)$ quantifies photosynthetic vigour; the **sum** $(NIR + R)$ normalizes for total scene brightness (soil/atmospheric effects). **(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):** **Given:** $R = 0.06$, $NIR = 0.42$, $G = 0.10$, $B = 0.04$, $RE = 0.30$ **Computation of NDVI:** $$ NDVI = \frac{0.42 - 0.06}{0.42 + 0.06} = \frac{0.36}{0.48} = 0.75 $$ **Computation of GNDVI:** $$ GNDVI = \frac{0.42 - 0.10}{0.42 + 0.10} = \frac{0.32}{0.52} = 0.615 $$ **Computation of NDRE:** $$ NDRE = \frac{0.42 - 0.30}{0.42 + 0.30} = \frac{0.12}{0.72} = 0.167 $$ **Computation of SAVI (with $L = 0.5$):** $$ SAVI = 1.5 \cdot \frac{0.42 - 0.06}{0.42 + 0.06 + 0.5} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{0.36}{0.98} = 0.551 $$ **Agronomic Interpretation:** - NDVI = 0.75 indicates **dense, vigorously growing cotton canopy**. - GNDVI = 0.62 indicates **moderate-to-high chlorophyll content** in upper leaves. - NDRE = 0.17 (low) suggests that **deep-layer chlorophyll is still developing** — typical of a mid-vegetative cotton crop. - SAVI = 0.55 corrects for soil background; the lower value compared to NDVI confirms partial soil exposure in the pixel. **Conclusion:** The pixel represents a healthy but not yet full-canopy cotton field; nitrogen top-dressing can still be effective. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Choice B]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end Band Math processing pipeline** for precision agriculture. Mention the role of atmospheric correction. (7 marks) **(b)** Differentiate between **NDVI, EVI, and SAVI**. For a wheat field pixel with $R = 0.08$, $NIR = 0.40$, $B = 0.05$, compute all three indices. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) End-to-End Band Math Pipeline (7 marks):** The pipeline is illustrated in the block diagram below (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes). The major stages are: 1. **Image Acquisition:** Capture multi-band imagery via satellite (Sentinel-2, Landsat), UAV-mounted multispectral cameras (MicaSense RedEdge, Parrot Sequoia), or field robots. 2. **Radiometric Calibration:** Convert raw sensor **DN (Digital Numbers)** to *top-of-atmosphere radiance* using sensor-specific calibration coefficients. This step removes sensor-internal noise. 3. **Atmospheric Correction:** This is the **most critical step**. Algorithms such as **6S (Second Simulation of Satellite Signal in the Solar Spectrum)**, **FLAASH**, or **Sen2Cor** (used operationally for Sentinel-2) remove scattering and absorption effects of gases and aerosols. The output is *surface reflectance* (range 0.0 – 1.0), which is comparable across dates, sensors, and locations. 4. **Band Selection:** Choose the spectral bands relevant to the target biophysical variable (e.g., Red + NIR for NDVI; Red + NIR + Blue for EVI). 5. **Band Math Engine:** Performs pixel-by-pixel arithmetic using the chosen index formula. Modern GIS tools (QGIS Raster Calculator, ArcGIS Raster Calculator, Google Earth Engine) implement this engine. 6. **Index Generation:** The output is a *derived raster image* in which each pixel's DN represents the index value. 7. **Decision Layer:** The index image is either thresholded (rule-based) or fed into a Machine Learning / Deep Learning model (Random Forest, U-Net CNN) to produce prescription maps for variable rate applications. 8. **Validation:** Ground-truth sampling (SPAD chlorophyll meter, gravimetric soil moisture, manual LAI) is used to assess index reliability. **Role of Atmospheric Correction:** Without it, aerosols and water vapour can artificially inflate or deflate reflectance, distorting index values and leading to wrong agronomic decisions. Atmospheric correction ensures that an NDVI of 0.7 in Kerala and an NDVI of 0.7 in Punjab are *physically comparable*. **(b) Difference and Computation (7 marks):** | Feature | NDVI | EVI | SAVI | |---------|------|-----|------| | **Formula** | $\frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R}$ | $2.5 \cdot \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + 6R - 7.5B + 1}$ | $1.5 \cdot \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R + 0.5}$ | | **Bands used** | 2 (NIR, R) | 3 (NIR, R, B) | 2 (NIR, R) | | **Soil correction** | No | Partial (via Blue band) | Yes (via $L$ factor) | | **Saturation at high LAI** | Yes (saturates ~LAI 3) | Reduced | Reduced | | **Atmospheric correction built-in** | No | Yes (Blue term) | No | | **Best suited for** | General biomass | Dense canopies | Sparse canopies | **Computation (Given $R = 0.08$, $NIR = 0.40$, $B = 0.05$):** **NDVI:** $$ NDVI = \frac{0.40 - 0.08}{0.40 + 0.08} = \frac{0.32}{0.48} = 0.667 $$ **EVI:** $$ EVI = 2.5 \cdot \frac{0.40 - 0.08}{0.40 + 6(0.08) - 7.5(0.05) + 1} $$ Compute each denominator term: $$ 6 \times 0.08 = 0.48 $$ $$ 7.5 \times 0.05 = 0.375 $$ Sum the denominator: $$ 0.40 + 0.48 - 0.375 + 1 = 1.505 $$ Form the inner fraction: $$ \frac{0.32}{1.505} = 0.2126 $$ Multiply by 2.5: $$ EVI = 2.5 \times 0.2126 = 0.5315 $$ **SAVI (with $L = 0.5$):** $$ SAVI = 1.5 \cdot \frac{0.40 - 0.08}{0.40 + 0.08 + 0.5} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{0.32}{0.98} = 0.4898 $$ **Result Table:** | Index | Value | |-------|-------| | NDVI | **0.667** | | EVI | **0.532** | | SAVI | **0.490** | **Interpretation:** The pixel shows moderate wheat vigour. NDVI > EVI > SAVI, which is expected because SAVI applies a soil-brightness penalty for partially exposed soil. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Using raw DN instead of reflectance** — KTU examiners will deduct up to **2 marks** if you compute NDVI directly from Digital Numbers without mentioning the need for atmospheric correction. > 2. **Inverted formula** — Writing NDVI as $\frac{R - NIR}{R + NIR}$ (wrong sign) is a very common slip. Always keep the order as `NIR - R` in the numerator. > 3. **Forgetting the EVI scaling constant 2.5** — Omitting the multiplier makes the value fall in a totally different range and shows lack of conceptual clarity. > 4. **No physical interpretation** — Computing the index without stating *what the value means agronomically* will attract a 1-mark deduction under Bloom's *Analyzing* level. > 5. **Wrong band assignment** — E.g., using Green in place of Red Edge for NDRE. Memorize the band-to-variable mapping. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Band Math = pixel-wise arithmetic on spectral bands** to extract agronomic meaning from raw imagery. - **NDVI** is the *workhorse* of precision agriculture: $\frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R}$, range $[-1, 1]$. - **EVI** adds a **Blue** band and a scaling factor of **2.5** to reduce soil and atmospheric noise in dense canopies. - **SAVI** introduces a soil-brightness factor **$L = 0.5$** for sparse vegetation. - **NDRE** uses the **Red Edge** band to penetrate dense canopies and sense deep chlorophyll. - **NDWI / NDMI** use Green/SWIR with NIR to detect **water/moisture stress**. - **Chlorophyll Indices (CI)** of the form $\frac{NIR}{G} - 1$ are **linearly** correlated with leaf chlorophyll. - **Always apply atmospheric correction** before computing indices — surface reflectance is mandatory. - **Always interpret the result** — a number alone is incomplete; KTU expects an agronomic statement. - **Use reflectance (0–1)** in formulas, not raw DN (typically 0–10,000 for 16-bit sensors). - **Indices are bounded or unbounded**: ratio-based indices are bounded, simple ratio and CI are unbounded — know the difference. - **For dense canopies:** prefer EVI / NDRE over NDVI (NDVI saturates). - **For sparse canopies:** prefer SAVI / MSAVI (soil-corrected).

topics.title: Interpretation of Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT861 - SENSORS, AI AND ROBOTICS IN PRECISION AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interpretation of Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Interpretation of Analysis** in Precision Agriculture refers to the systematic process of converting raw, structured, or unstructured data obtained from agricultural sensors, remote-sensing platforms, AI/ML models, and robotic field operations into **actionable agronomic intelligence**. It involves statistical inference, pattern recognition, vegetation-index mapping, and model-output evaluation to support crop management decisions such as irrigation scheduling, fertilizer dosing, pest control, and yield forecasting.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), this topic is mapped to:

> **CO3:** Apply analytical and AI-based tools to interpret agricultural data for precision farming decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor examining a patient's blood test report. The numbers (hemoglobin, sugar, cholesterol) on the report are not useful until the doctor **interprets** them: *"Hemoglobin is low, prescribe iron supplements."* 

Similarly, in precision agriculture, a drone may capture a field's multispectral image, and a soil sensor may report nitrogen levels — but until a trained agronomist (or an AI model) **interprets** these values, the farmer cannot decide what action to take.

- **Data** = raw numbers from sensors
- **Analysis** = processed numbers (averages, indices, predictions)
- **Interpretation** = the *meaning* and *agronomic action* derived from the analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** "Interpretation" is the bridge between *data analysis* (mathematical computation) and *decision-making* (real-world farming action). It is the highest cognitive level in Bloom's Taxonomy (Evaluate / Analyze) for this module.

### 1.3 Core Components of Interpretation

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **Descriptive Statistics** | Mean, median, mode, variance, range of sensor values |
| **Vegetation Index Mapping** | NDVI, EVI, SAVI for crop-health assessment |
| **Trend & Anomaly Detection** | Time-series patterns, outlier identification |
| **AI Model Output Decoding** | Confusion matrix, accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score |
| **Geospatial Interpretation** | GPS-tagged heat maps, zone classification (VRA maps) |
| **Decision Translation** | Converting metrics into agronomic recommendations |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard reference range constants used in precision-agriculture interpretation are: **NDVI healthy crop > 0.6**, **NDVI stressed crop 0.2–0.5**, **NDVI bare soil < 0.2**, **Soil pH optimum 6.0–7.5**, and **EC threshold for salinity > 4 dS/m**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Interpretation

The interpretation pipeline in precision agriculture follows a strict six-stage logic:

1. **Data Ingestion** — Collect raw readings from soil sensors, weather stations, UAVs, and satellites.
2. **Pre-processing** — Remove noise, handle missing values, normalize scales.
3. **Statistical Analysis** — Compute descriptive statistics, correlations, and regression.
4. **Index Computation** — Calculate vegetation indices (NDVI, EVI, SAVI) from spectral bands.
5. **Model Evaluation** — Decode the output of AI/ML models using performance metrics.
6. **Decision Mapping** — Translate quantitative results into agronomic prescriptions (kg/ha, mm of water, days to harvest).

### 2.2 Why Interpretation Matters

Without proper interpretation, data is just a *pile of numbers*. For example:
- A soil-N sensor reading of **120 kg/ha** is meaningless until compared against the **critical threshold (150 kg/ha)** and the **crop's growth stage**.
- A drone NDVI map showing a **0.3 patch** in a wheat field must be interpreted as **nitrogen deficiency** or **water stress** based on context (soil moisture, growth stage, weather).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | Same as variable | Central tendency of sensor readings |
| 2 | Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}{n}$ | Unit² | Spread of data |
| 3 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^{2}}$ | Same as variable | Data dispersion |
| 4 | Coefficient of Variation (CV) | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ | % | Field uniformity |
| 5 | Pearson Correlation | $r = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^{2} \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}}$ | dimensionless ($-1$ to $+1$) | Sensor vs. yield relationship |
| 6 | Linear Regression Slope | $b = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$ | unit of $y$ per unit of $x$ | Yield prediction |
| 7 | Linear Regression Intercept | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ | unit of $y$ | Baseline value |
| 8 | **NDVI** (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | dimensionless ($-1$ to $+1$) | Crop vigour |
| 9 | **EVI** (Enhanced Vegetation Index) | $EVI = G \cdot \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + C_1 \cdot Red - C_2 \cdot Blue + L}$ | dimensionless | Reduces soil/atmosphere noise |
| 10 | **SAVI** (Soil-Adjusted Vegetation Index) | $SAVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red + L}(1 + L)$ | dimensionless | Sparse canopy correction |
| 11 | Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | dimensionless | Model overall correctness |
| 12 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | dimensionless | False-alarm control |
| 13 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | dimensionless | Missed-detection control |
| 14 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | dimensionless | Balanced metric |
| 15 | R² (Coefficient of Determination) | $R^{2} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}}{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Regression model fit |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard EVI coefficients: $G = 2.5$, $C_1 = 6$, $C_2 = 7.5$, $L = 1$. Standard SAVI soil-correction factor $L = 0.5$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Variable Rate Application (VRA):** NDVI zones are interpreted into nitrogen-application maps.
- **Smart Irrigation:** Soil-moisture sensor trends are interpreted into irrigation schedules.
- **Yield Prediction:** Regression models interpret soil + weather data into tonnage forecasts.
- **Disease Detection:** CNN-based image classifiers interpret leaf images into disease labels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: NDVI and Its Interpretation Thresholds

The NDVI is derived from the principle that **healthy vegetation strongly reflects Near-Infrared (NIR)** light and **strongly absorbs Red** light (due to chlorophyll). Bare soil and water reflect both bands almost equally.

**Step 1 — Define Reflectance Bands**

Let $R_{NIR}$ be the reflectance in the NIR band (≈ 0.7–1.3 μm) and $R_{Red}$ be the reflectance in the Red band (≈ 0.6–0.7 μm), both measured by a multispectral sensor.

**Step 2 — Form the Difference**

$$
\Delta R = R_{NIR} - R_{Red}
$$

This difference is large for healthy green vegetation, small for stressed crops, and near zero for bare soil.

**Step 3 — Normalize the Difference**

To bound the index between $-1$ and $+1$, divide by the sum:

$$
NDVI = \dfrac{R_{NIR} - R_{Red}}{R_{NIR} + R_{Red}}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Bound**

For any non-negative $R_{NIR}$ and $R_{Red}$:
- If $R_{NIR} \gg R_{Red}$ (lush canopy): $NDVI \to +1$
- If $R_{NIR} \approx R_{Red}$ (bare soil): $NDVI \approx 0$
- If $R_{NIR} \ll R_{Red}$ (water/snow): $NDVI \to -1$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**

Given $R_{NIR} = 0.48$ and $R_{Red} = 0.08$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NDVI &= \dfrac{0.48 - 0.08}{0.48 + 0.08} \\
&= \dfrac{0.40}{0.56} \\
&\approx 0.714
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $0.714 > 0.6 \Rightarrow$ **healthy, dense canopy** — no immediate agronomic action needed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Linear Regression for Yield Prediction

A common interpretation task is to predict crop yield $Y$ (tonnes/ha) from a soil-nitrogen sensor reading $X$ (kg/ha).

**Step 1 — Hypothesis**

Assume a linear model:

$$
Y_i = a + bX_i + \varepsilon_i
$$

where $\varepsilon_i$ is a small random error and $a, b$ are parameters to be estimated.

**Step 2 — Minimize Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**

The Least-Squares method minimizes:

$$
SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - a - bX_i)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Partial Derivatives**

Setting $\dfrac{\partial SSE}{\partial a} = 0$ and $\dfrac{\partial SSE}{\partial b} = 0$:

$$
\dfrac{\partial SSE}{\partial a} = -2\sum(Y_i - a - bX_i) = 0
$$

$$
\dfrac{\partial SSE}{\partial b} = -2\sum X_i(Y_i - a - bX_i) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Normal Equations**

From the first equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum Y_i &= na + b\sum X_i \\
