### Step 2 — Apply Field Efficiency

$$C_e = \eta_f \cdot C_t$$

### Step 3 — Final KTU-Standard Form

$$\boxed{\;C_e = \eta_f \cdot \frac{W \cdot v}{10}\;} \quad [\text{ha}/\text{h}]$$

### Engineering Interpretation
- Field efficiency $\eta_f$ accounts for **turning time, overlap, refilling, and obstacle avoidance**.
- For a robotic system with autonomous turning, $\eta_f$ can rise to **0.90**, vs **0.75** for manual operation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Robotic Selection for Farm Operations

| Operation | Robotic Class | Key Sensors | AI Technique | Typical Throughput | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tillage | Autonomous tractor | GNSS-RTK, IMU | Path planning | 1.5 – 3 ha/h | High |
| Seeding | Precision seed drill | Encoder, seed-tube sensor | Prescription map | 800 – 1500 seeds/min | Medium |
| Transplanting | Robotic transplanter | Vision, depth camera | Pose estimation | 3000 – 5000 plants/h | Medium |
| Weeding | Inter-row hoe / spot-spray | RGB camera | CNN detection | 0.5 – 1 ha/h | Very High |
| Spraying | UAV / ground sprayer | Multispectral | Variable rate | 5 – 10 ha/h | High |
| Fruit picking | 6-DOF arm | RGB-D | Grasp-point detection | 1 fruit / 5 – 8 s | Very High |
| Milking | Rotary/box robot | Teat-cup positioner | Vision + force | 60 – 90 cows/h | Medium |
| Sorting | Delta arm + vision | Hyperspectral | Defect classifier | 5 – 15 t/h | High |
| Pruning | Manipulator + cutter | LiDAR, vision | Branch segmentation | 100 trees/h | Low |
| Surveillance | UAV / ground rover | Thermal, RGB | Anomaly detection | 50 – 100 ha/h | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Agricultural Robot

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **entire functional flow** of a farm robot from field perception to actuation. It is the **most exam-relevant diagram** for a 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENV[FIELD ENVIRONMENT]
        CROPS[ROW_CROPS]
        WEEDS[WEEDS]
        OBSTACLES[OBSTACLES_AND_HUMANS]
        TERRAIN[SOIL_AND_TERRAIN]
    end

    subgraph PER[PERCEPTION_LAYER]
        GNSS[GNSS_RTK_RECEIVER]
        LIDAR[LASER_SCANNER]
        CAM[RGB_AND_DEPTH_CAMERA]
        IMU[IMU_SENSOR]
        SOIL[SOIL_NPK_SENSOR]
        MULTISPEC[MULTISPECTRAL_CAMERA]
    end

    subgraph FUSE[SENSOR_FUSION_UNIT]
        EKF[EXTENDED_KALMAN_FILTER]
        SLAM[VISUAL_LIDAR_SLAM]
    end

    subgraph COG[COGNITION_AI_LAYER]
        CNN[CNN_OBJECT_DETECTION]
        SEG[U_NET_SEMANTIC_SEGMENTATION]
        PATH[A_STAR_RRT_PLANNER]
        VRA[VRTC_DECISION_ENGINE]
    end

    subgraph ACT[ACTUATION_LAYER]
        DRIVE[DIFFERENTIAL_DRIVE_BASE]
        ARM[6_DOF_MANIPULATOR]
        GRIPPER[SOFT_GRIPPER]
        NOZZLE[VARIABLE_RATE_NOZZLE]
        CUTTER[MECHANICAL_CUTTER]
    end

    subgraph COMM[COMMUNICATION_LAYER]
        ROS[ROS2_MIDDLEWARE]
        MQTT[MQTT_CLOUD_BRIDGE]
        FMIS[FARM_MGMT_INFO_SYSTEM]
    end

    CROPS --> CAM
    WEEDS --> CAM
    OBSTACLES --> LIDAR
    TERRAIN --> SOIL
    CROPS --> MULTISPEC

    GNSS --> EKF
    LIDAR --> SLAM
    CAM --> SLAM
    IMU --> EKF
    SOIL --> VRA
    MULTISPEC --> CNN

    EKF --> PATH
    SLAM --> PATH
    CNN --> VRA
    SEG --> VRA
    PATH --> DRIVE
    VRA --> NOZZLE
    VRA --> ARM
    ARM --> GRIPPER
    ARM --> CUTTER

    DRIVE --> ROS
    NOZZLE --> ROS
    ROS --> MQTT
    MQTT --> FMIS
```

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Decision Tree for Selective Fruit Harvesting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Fruit_Harvester_at_Canopy] --> SCAN[Capture_RGBD_Frame]
    SCAN --> DETECT{CNN_Detects_Mature_Fruit}
    DETECT -->|No_Fruit| MOVE[Advance_Robot_Position]
    DETECT -->|Yes| CLASSIFY[Classify_Maturity_Class]
    CLASSIFY --> RIPE{Ripe_or_Overripe}
    RIPE -->|No| SKIP[Skip_and_Continue]
    RIPE -->|Yes| PLAN[Compute_Grasp_Point_via_Depth]
    PLAN --> APPROACH[Move_6DOF_Arm_to_Pregrasp]
    APPROACH --> GRASP[Close_Soft_Gripper]
    GRASP --> TWIST[Apply_Twist_and_Pull]
    TWIST --> DETACH{Fruit_Detached}
    DETACH -->|No| RETRY[Regrasp_with_Higher_Force]
    DETACH -->|Yes| PLACE[Place_in_Conveyor]
    PLACE --> LOG[Log_Harvest_to_FMIS]
    LOG --> MOVE
    RETRY --> APPROACH
    MOVE --> START
```

---

## 4.3 Robotic Weeding System — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[INPUT]
        ROW[Row_Navigation_Camera]
        WEED[Weed_Detection_Camera]
    end

    subgraph PROC[PROCESSING]
        NAV[Row_Centering_Controller]
        DET[YOLOv8_Detection]
        IK[Inverse_Kinematics_Solver]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT]
        WHEEL[Drive_Wheel_Actuator]
        TOOL[Micro_Spray_or_Laser_Tool]
    end

    ROW --> NAV
    WEED --> DET
    NAV --> WHEEL
    DET --> IK
    IK --> TOOL
```

---

## 4.4 Dairy Milking Robot — Closed-Loop Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    COW[Cow_Enters_Milking_Box] --> ID[RFID_Identification]
    ID --> FEED[Dispense_Concentrate_Feed]
    FEED --> CLEAN[Teat_Cleaning_Rotating_Brush]
    CLEAN --> LOC[3D_Camera_Teat_Localization]
    LOC --> ATTACH[Robotic_Arm_Attaches_Teat_Cups]
    ATTACH --> MILK[Sensor_Monitors_Milk_Flow_and_Quality]
    MILK --> DETACH[Detach_on_Low_Flow]
    DETACH --> DISINFECT[Post_Milking_Teat_Disinfection]
    DISINFECT --> EXIT[Cow_Releases_to_Exit_Gate]
    EXIT --> DATA[Upload_Milk_Yield_and_Health_to_FMIS]
```

---

## 4.5 Architecture Summary Table

| Layer | Inputs | Outputs | Failure Mode | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Perception** | Camera, LiDAR, GNSS | Pose + Map | Sensor occlusion | Multi-sensor redundancy |
| **Cognition** | Fused data | Decisions | Misclassification | Confidence thresholds |
| **Actuation** | Decisions | Motion | Mechanical jam | Torque limiters + E-stop |
| **Communication** | All layer events | Cloud sync | Network loss | Edge-buffered autonomy |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define agricultural robotics. List any four farm operations where robots are deployed.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**
Agricultural robotics refers to the application of autonomous or semi-autonomous mechatronic systems equipped with sensors, AI-based controllers, and actuators to perform farming tasks with minimal human intervention.

Four farm operations where robots are deployed:
1. **Soil preparation** — autonomous tractors with GPS-guided implements.
2. **Sowing / Transplanting** — robotic seeders and transplanters.
3. **Weeding & Spraying** — vision-guided mobile weeding bots and UAV sprayers.
4. **Harvesting** — selective fruit-picking manipulators.
5. **Post-harvest sorting** — vision-guided delta-arm graders.
6. **Dairy farming** — automatic milking and feeding robots.

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between automation and robotics in the context of precision agriculture.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Automation | Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Pre-programmed execution of repetitive tasks | Reactive, perception-driven adaptive execution |
| **Intelligence** | Rule-based, fixed sequence | AI-driven, learns and adapts |
| **Sensing** | Minimal or none | Multi-modal sensors (vision, LiDAR, GNSS) |
| **Decision-making** | Deterministic | Probabilistic / learned |
| **Example** | Auto-steering tractor on a fixed A-B line | Robot that detects a ripe tomato and selectively picks it |
| **Flexibility** | Low — task-specific | High — multi-functional |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |

Modern precision agriculture **integrates both**: automation provides reliability, robotics provides adaptability.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of an autonomous tractor system** for precision agriculture. Label all major components. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **pure-pursuit steering law** used for row-following in an autonomous tractor. Explain how the lookahead distance affects steering behaviour. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Block Diagram:** (Draw Figure 4.1 from SECTION 4 of these notes — 3 marks for neat labeled diagram)

**Components & Their Functions (4 marks):**

1. **GNSS-RTK Receiver** — provides absolute position with ±2 cm accuracy.
2. **IMU** — measures acceleration and angular velocity for dead-reckoning.
3. **Wheel Encoders** — measure wheel rotation for odometry.
4. **Controller (ECU)** — fuses sensor data via EKF and computes steering/throttle commands.
5. **Steering Actuator** — executes steering angle corrections.
6. **Throttle / Transmission Actuator** — controls speed.
7. **Implement Controller (ISOBUS)** — operates attached implement (plough, sprayer, seeder).
8. **HMI / Tablet** — operator interface for path planning and monitoring.
9. **Obstacle Detection (LiDAR/Radar)** — safety layer for collision avoidance.
10. **Telemetry Unit** — wireless data upload to FMIS.

**Working:** Operator defines the field boundary and AB-lines on the tablet. The controller fuses GNSS, IMU, and wheel-encoder data via the EKF to estimate pose. The pure-pursuit algorithm computes the steering correction, which is sent to the steering actuator. The implement controller triggers seed/fertilizer/spray based on the prescription map. Closed-loop control maintains sub-decimetre accuracy.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Derivation:** (Present the full derivation from SECTION 3.1 — 5 marks)

**Lookahead Distance Analysis (2 marks):**

The steering angle is given by:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{e}{L}\right)$$

- **Small $L$ (e.g., 0.5 m):** The robot reacts aggressively to small errors — high responsiveness but oscillatory behaviour at higher speeds.
- **Large $L$ (e.g., 5.0 m):** Smooth, stable steering but slow correction — risk of running over rows at sharp turns.
- **Optimal $L$:** Empirical rule: $L \approx 1.5 \times v$, where $v$ is in m/s. For a tractor at 2.5 m/s, $L \approx 3.75$ m.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a robotic fruit-picking system** with reference to its perception, decision, and actuation layers. Discuss the key challenges in selective harvesting. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A robotic weeder operates at a swath width of **4 m** and travel speed of **1.8 m/s**. The target herbicide dose is **150 L/ha**. Compute **(i)** the spray discharge rate in L/min, and **(ii)** the effective field capacity in ha/h assuming field efficiency of **0.85**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Working Principle (5 marks):**

The robotic fruit-picking system is a **cyber-physical system** operating in three layers:

1. **Perception Layer:**
   - RGB-D cameras capture fruit appearance and 3-D position.
   - CNN-based detectors (YOLO, Mask R-CNN) localize fruits and classify maturity.
   - Stereo vision or structured light provides depth for grasp-point estimation.
   - Sunlight variation handled by HDR imaging and polarization filters.

2. **Decision Layer:**
   - Edge AI processor (NVIDIA Jetson, Google Coral) runs inference in real-time.
   - Inverse kinematics solver computes joint angles for the 6-DOF arm.
   - Grasp planner chooses between suction-cup or soft-finger gripper based on fruit type.
   - Path planner avoids collisions with branches and other fruits.

3. **Actuation Layer:**
   - 6-DOF serial manipulator executes the trajectory.
   - Soft gripper closes with controlled force ($F_{grip} = \mu N$).
   - Wrist rotation + gentle pull detaches fruit without stem damage.
   - Fruit placed on conveyor → crates/boxes.

**Key Challenges (2 marks):**
- **Occlusion:** Leaves and branches hide 40–60 % of fruits in canopies.
- **Variability:** Fruit size, shape, and orientation vary widely.
- **Delicacy:** Excessive force bruises produce — economic loss.
- **Outdoor conditions:** Lighting changes, wind, dust, temperature.
- **Throughput:** Human pickers still outperform robots in many crops.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- $W = 4$ m, $v = 1.8$ m/s, $q_a = 150$ L/ha, $\eta_f = 0.85$.

**(i) Spray Discharge Rate:**

$$Q = \frac{q_a \cdot W \cdot v}{166.67}$$

$$Q = \frac{150 \times 4 \times 1.8}{166.67} = \frac{1080}{166.67} \approx 6.48 \, \text{L/min}$$

**(ii) Effective Field Capacity:**

First convert $v$ to km/h:

$$v = 1.8 \, \text{m/s} \times 3.6 = 6.48 \, \text{km/h}$$

Then:

$$C_e = \eta_f \cdot \frac{W \cdot v}{10} = 0.85 \cdot \frac{4 \times 6.48}{10} = 0.85 \cdot 2.592 = 2.20 \, \text{ha/h}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Spray discharge rate ≈ **6.48 L/min**.
- Effective field capacity ≈ **2.20 ha/h**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing m/s with km/h in field-capacity formula — always convert.
> 2. **Forgetting field efficiency:** Computing only theoretical capacity $C_t$, not effective $C_e$.
> 3. **Missing feedback loop:** In block-diagram questions, omitting the verification/sensor layer loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing automation with robotics:** Writing about auto-steering only in a "robotics" question.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why":** Derivation without an interpretation paragraph loses the application mark.
> 6. **Pipe symbol misuse in tables:** Always use `\lvert \rvert` or `\mid` in markdown/LaTeX tables.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Agricultural robot = Perception + Cognition + Actuation + Communication** — four layers; always draw all four.
- **GNSS-RTK** gives ±2 cm accuracy; **LiDAR-SLAM** handles GNSS-denied zones (orchards, greenhouses).
- **CNN (YOLOv8)** is the KTU-preferred object-detection backbone for fruit/weed identification.
- **Pure-pursuit steering law:** $\theta = \tan^{-1}(e/L)$ — small $L$ = responsive but oscillatory.
- **Slip ratio** = $(v_{wheel} - v_{robot})/v_{robot} \times 100\%$; target **< 15 %** on tilled soil.
- **Variable-rate spray** $Q = q_a \cdot W \cdot v / 166.67$ — convert units carefully.
- **Effective field capacity** $C_e = \eta_f \cdot W \cdot v / 10$; field efficiency $\eta_f$ = 0.7 – 0.9.
- **NDVI** = $(NIR - RED)/(NIR + RED)$ — primary vegetation index for drone-based scouting.
- **6-DOF manipulators** dominate fruit picking; **delta robots** dominate sorting; **Ackermann** dominates tractors.
- **Soft grippers** reduce bruising; suction grippers suit smooth-surfaced fruits.
- **Rotary milking robots** can serve **60–90 cows/hour** with yield gains of **10–15 %**.
- **UAV spraying** swath width = **3–7 m**, typical flight height = **1–3 m** above canopy.
- **ISO 18497** — safety standard for autonomous agricultural machinery (cite in 14-mark answers).
- **Throughput, pick success, slip %, path-tracking RMSE, cycle time** — five metrics examiners expect.
- **ROS 2** is the standard middleware; **MQTT** is the cloud bridge; **FMIS** is the data sink.
- **Selective harvesting** is the hardest unsolved problem — expect this as a research-direction question.
- **Labour reduction** (~30 %), **herbicide reduction** (~77 %), **24/7 operation** — the three pillars of justification.
- **Course Outcome CO4** maps directly to this topic — design + feasibility evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT861 - SENSORS, AI AND ROBOTICS IN PRECISION AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Sensors and Actuators?

A **sensor** is a physical device that detects and responds to some type of input from the physical environment. The specific input could be light, heat, motion, moisture, pressure, or any one of a great number of other environmental phenomena. The output is generally a signal that is converted into a human-readable display at the sensor location or transmitted electronically over a network for reading or further processing.

An **actuator** is a component of a machine that is responsible for moving and controlling a mechanism or system. It requires a control signal and a source of energy to function. The control signal is relatively low energy and may be electric voltage or current, pneumatic or hydraulic pressure, or even human power. When it receives a control signal, an actuator responds by converting the source energy into mechanical motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Reference:** In precision agriculture, sensors act as the *sensory nervous system* of a farm, perceiving soil, crop, and ambient conditions, while actuators function as the *muscular system*, executing irrigation, fertilization, spraying, or mechanical interventions based on sensor feedback.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

**Imagine a smart greenhouse:**

A **sensor** is like the **human skin**. When you touch a hot kettle, your skin senses the temperature and sends a signal through nerves to your brain. Similarly, a soil moisture sensor "touches" the soil, senses its wetness, and sends an electrical signal to a microcontroller or cloud server.

An **actuator** is like the **human hand**. Once the brain (controller) decides "the soil is too dry," it commands the hand (actuator — like a solenoid valve or motorized pump) to open the water tap. The actuator converts electrical energy into physical action.

In the absence of either, the system is incomplete:
- **Sensors without actuators** = A greenhouse that *knows* it's dry but cannot water itself.
- **Actuators without sensors** = A greenhouse that *waters* blindly, even during a rainstorm.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

### 1.3.1 Classification of Sensors Used in Precision Agriculture

| Category | Examples | Measured Parameter |
|----------|----------|--------------------|
| **Soil Sensors** | TDR, FDR, Capacitive probes, EC sensors | Moisture, temperature, pH, NPK, EC |
| **Weather/Air Sensors** | Thermistor, Hygrometer, Anemometer, Pyranometer | Air temperature, humidity, wind speed, solar radiation |
| **Crop Sensors** | Multispectral cameras, NDVI sensors, Chlorophyll meters | Crop health, biomass, leaf area index |
| **Position/Proximity** | GPS, IMU, Ultrasonic, LiDAR | Location, distance, terrain mapping |
| **Mechanical** | Load cells, Strain gauges, Accelerometers | Weight, force, vibration |

### 1.3.2 Classification of Actuators Used in Precision Agriculture

| Category | Examples | Function |
|----------|----------|----------|
| **Hydraulic Actuators** | Hydraulic cylinders, Pumps | Steering of tractors, controlling implements |
| **Pneumatic Actuators** | Pneumatic cylinders, Compressors | Sprayer control, seed metering |
| **Electrical Actuators** | DC/Stepper/Servo motors, Solenoid valves, Relays | Variable rate applicators, robotic arms |
| **Thermal Actuators** | Heaters, Peltier modules | Greenhouse climate control |
| **Mechanical Actuators** | Racks, Pinions, Lead screws | Adjustable platform/valve positioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Expected Knowledge):**
> - **Sensitivity:** $\frac{d(\text{Output})}{d(\text{Input})}$ — output change per unit input change
> - **Accuracy:** Closeness to true value, expressed as % of full-scale
> - **Precision:** Repeatability of measurement under identical conditions
> - **Range:** Minimum and maximum measurable input
> - **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change
> - **Linearity:** How closely output follows a straight-line relationship with input
> - **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for the same input during increasing vs. decreasing cycles
> - **Response Time:** Time taken to reach 90% (or 95%) of the final output value

## 1.4 Signal Conditioning — The Bridge Between Sensor and Actuator

Raw sensor output is rarely directly usable. Most sensors produce signals that need **signal conditioning**:
1. **Amplification** (e.g., a 0–50 mV thermocouple output needs to be amplified to 0–5 V)
2. **Filtering** (removing electrical noise, e.g., 50/60 Hz power-line interference)
3. **Linearization** (correcting non-linear sensor response)
4. **Cold-junction compensation** (for thermocouples)
5. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** for microcontroller interfacing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Course Outcome Alignment (CO4):** This topic directly maps to KTU Course Outcome CO4: *"Select and integrate appropriate sensors and actuators for precision agriculture automation systems."* Mastery of sensor principles, signal conditioning, and actuator selection is essential for this outcome.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principles of Major Sensor Types in Precision Agriculture

### 2.1.1 Soil Moisture Sensors

**a) Time Domain Reflectometry (TDR)**
- Sends a high-frequency electromagnetic pulse along a waveguide (rod) inserted in soil.
- The pulse reflects from the end of the rod.
- The **travel time** $t$ of the pulse is measured.
- Travel time is related to the **dielectric constant** $\varepsilon_r$ of the soil.

**Why this works:** Water has a very high dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$), while dry soil has $\varepsilon_r \approx 3$–$5$. Even a small change in moisture dramatically changes the bulk dielectric constant, which changes pulse travel time.

**b) Frequency Domain Reflectometry (FDR) / Capacitive Sensors**
- Soil acts as the dielectric of a capacitor.
- The capacitance $C$ depends on the soil's dielectric permittivity.
- An oscillator circuit uses this capacitor — its frequency $f$ changes with moisture.

**c) Tensiometer**
- A water-filled tube with a porous ceramic tip inserted in soil.
- Measures soil water **tension** (matric potential) in **kPa** or **bar**.
- Indicates how hard plant roots must "pull" to extract water.

**d) Gypsum Block / Granular Matrix Sensor (GMS)**
- Two electrodes embedded in a porous material.
- Electrical resistance between electrodes varies with soil moisture.

### 2.1.2 Temperature Sensors

**a) Thermistor (NTC type — Negative Temperature Coefficient)**
- Resistance decreases as temperature increases.
- Mathematical model: **Steinhart–Hart Equation**

**b) Thermocouple**
- Two dissimilar metals joined at a junction (Seebeck effect).
- Generates a small voltage (microvolts to millivolts) proportional to temperature difference.

**c) RTD — Resistance Temperature Detector (e.g., PT100)**
- Platinum wire with resistance $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $0\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
- Highly accurate and linear.

**d) Infrared (IR) / Thermal Sensors**
- Measure emitted IR radiation from objects (non-contact).
- Used for canopy temperature in irrigation scheduling.

### 2.1.3 Optical / Crop-Health Sensors

- **NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index):**
  $$NDVI = \frac{(NIR - Red)}{(NIR + Red)}$$
  where NIR is the reflectance in the near-infrared band and Red is the reflectance in the red band. Healthy vegetation: NDVI $\approx 0.7$–$0.9$. Bare soil: NDVI $\approx 0.1$–$0.2$.

- **Multispectral and Hyperspectral Cameras** detect reflectance in multiple bands for crop stress detection.

### 2.1.4 Position Sensors

- **GPS (Global Positioning System):** Triangulation using ≥4 satellites gives latitude, longitude, altitude.
- **RTK-GPS (Real-Time Kinematic):** Provides centimeter-level accuracy using a base station correction.
- **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit):** Combines accelerometers, gyroscopes, and magnetometers for orientation and motion tracking.

### 2.1.5 Gas and Chemical Sensors

- **Electrochemical sensors** for $\text{NH}_3$, $\text{NO}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$ (livestock/greenhouse monitoring).
- **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared)** for $\text{CO}_2$ concentration.

## 2.2 Working Principles of Major Actuator Types

### 2.2.1 DC Motors
- Convert DC electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy.
- Speed $N$ (rpm) controlled by applied voltage; Torque depends on armature current.
- Used in variable-rate fertilizer spreaders, seed metering discs, drone propellers.

### 2.2.2 Stepper Motors
- Move in discrete angular steps (e.g., 1.8° per step = 200 steps/rev).
- **No feedback required** for open-loop positioning.
- Used in precision seed metering, valve opening, robotic arm joints.
- Holding torque maintains position when energized.

### 2.2.3 Servo Motors
- DC/AC motor with **encoder feedback** for closed-loop control.
- Used in agricultural robot arms, autopilot tractor steering.

### 2.2.4 Solenoid Valves
- Electromagnetic coil moves a plunger to open/close a fluid path.
- Critical for **drip irrigation automation** — the actuator that turns water ON/OFF.
- Two states: Normally Open (NO) or Normally Closed (NC).

### 2.2.5 Hydraulic & Pneumatic Actuators
- **Hydraulic:** Fluid (oil) under high pressure drives a piston. Produces very high force. Used in tractor steering, implement lifting, large sprayer booms.
- **Pneumatic:** Compressed air drives a piston. Cleaner, simpler. Used in seed singulation, sorting lines.

### 2.2.6 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) Control
- Average voltage to actuator is controlled by varying duty cycle.
- $$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$$
  where $D$ is the duty cycle ($0 \le D \le 1$).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | NDVI | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | NIR, Red = reflectance values | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | Thermistor (Steinhart–Hart) | $\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C [\ln(R)]^3$ | $T$ = absolute temp (K); $R$ = resistance ($\Omega$); $A,B,C$ = constants | K, $\Omega$ |
| 3 | RTD (PT100, linear approx) | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ | $\alpha \approx 0.00385$ for Pt; $R_0$ at 0°C | $\Omega$, °C |
| 4 | Thermocouple voltage | $V \approx S \cdot \Delta T$ | $S$ = Seebeck coefficient (V/°C) | V |
| 5 | Capacitive sensor (parallel plate) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_r$ varies with soil moisture | F (Farads) |
| 6 | TDR pulse travel | $\Delta t = \dfrac{2L \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$ | $L$ = rod length; $c$ = speed of light | seconds |
| 7 | Tensiometer water potential | $\psi = \rho g h$ | $h$ = water column height; $\rho$ = water density | Pa (or kPa, bar) |
| 8 | DC motor speed | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \phi}$ | $V$ = voltage; $I_a$ = armature current; $K\phi$ = motor constant | rpm |
| 9 | Servo torque | $\tau = K_t \cdot I$ | $K_t$ = torque constant; $I$ = current | N·m |
| 10 | PWM average voltage | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | $D$ = duty cycle | V |
| 11 | Sensor sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | $\Delta y$ = output change; $\Delta x$ = input change | varies |
| 12 | Wheatstone bridge balance | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$ | For zero galvanometer deflection | dimensionless |
| 13 | Strain gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G$ = gauge factor; $\varepsilon$ = strain | dimensionless |
| 14 | SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 15 | ADC resolution | $Q = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | $n$ = number of bits | V/LSB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A precision agriculture drone uses GPS (location), IMU (orientation), NDVI camera (crop health), and a centrifugal atomizer (actuator) — all coordinated by a microcontroller. Understanding each block's formula is essential for tuning, calibration, and troubleshooting.

## 2.4 Closed-Loop Feedback Architecture

A precision agriculture control loop typically looks like:

1. **Sensor** measures the controlled variable (e.g., soil moisture).
2. **Signal conditioning** amplifies and filters the signal.
3. **Controller (microcontroller/PLC)** compares measured value to a **setpoint** and computes an error.
4. **Actuator** (valve, motor) is driven to correct the error.
5. **Process** (the field, the crop, the irrigation line) responds.
6. Loop continues — this is a **closed-loop system**.

The classic PID controller equation:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where $e(t) = \text{setpoint} - \text{measured value}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capacitive Soil Moisture Sensor Working

**Problem Setup:**
A parallel plate capacitor is buried in soil. The capacitor has plate area $A$, separation $d$, and the soil between plates acts as a dielectric with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$.

**Step 1 — Capacitance of parallel plate capacitor in vacuum:**
The general formula for a parallel plate capacitor filled with a dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ is the permittivity of free space.

**Step 2 — Relationship between capacitance and oscillator frequency:**
This capacitor $C$ is part of an LC oscillator (often a 555 timer in astable mode or a CMOS inverter oscillator). The frequency of oscillation depends on $C$ as:

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of moisture:**
As soil moisture content $\theta$ (volumetric water content, $\text{m}^3/\text{m}^3$) increases, the bulk dielectric permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ increases, because water ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$ at 20°C) has a much higher permittivity than dry soil particles ($\varepsilon_r \approx 3$–$5$).

An empirical linearized relationship often used is:

$$\varepsilon_r \approx a \cdot \theta + b$$

where $a$ and $b$ are soil-type-specific calibration constants. For mineral soils, $a \approx 8.5$, $b \approx 3.0$ is a reasonable starting point (Topp equation alternative, simpler form).

**Step 4 — Combine into the final frequency relation:**

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 (a\theta + b) A}{d}}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the soil moisture content $\theta$:**

$$\theta = \frac{1}{a}\left[\frac{d}{(2\pi f)^2 L \varepsilon_0 A} - b\right]$$

**Step 6 — Voltage Output (after frequency-to-voltage conversion):**
A frequency-to-voltage (F/V) converter (e.g., LM2907) gives:

$$V_{out} = K_{fv} \cdot f$$

where $K_{fv}$ is the F/V gain in V/Hz. So the final analog output becomes:

$$V_{out} = K_{fv} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L \varepsilon_0 (a\theta + b) A}{d}}}$$

This is then digitized by the ADC and calibrated to give a moisture reading in %VWC (volumetric water content).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently test the relationship between capacitance, frequency, and the measured quantity. Knowing this chain helps you answer both conceptual and numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: NDVI from Reflectance Values

**Problem Setup:**
A multispectral camera measures reflectance from crop canopy at two wavelengths:
- Red band (centered at $\sim 660\ \text{nm}$): reflectance $R_{red}$
- Near-Infrared band (centered at $\sim 840\ \text{nm}$): reflectance $R_{NIR}$

**Step 1 — Healthy plant leaves reflect strongly in NIR** because of internal leaf mesophyll cell structure (scattering). They **absorb strongly in Red** because of chlorophyll absorption for photosynthesis.

So for healthy vegetation:
$$R_{NIR} \gg R_{red}$$

**Step 2 — Define NDVI:**

$$NDVI = \frac{R_{NIR} - R_{red}}{R_{NIR} + R_{red}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example:**
Suppose $R_{NIR} = 0.50$ and $R_{red} = 0.05$.

$$NDVI = \frac{0.50 - 0.05}{0.50 + 0.05} = \frac{0.45}{0.55} = 0.818$$

For bare soil, suppose $R_{NIR} = 0.30$ and $R_{red} = 0.25$:

$$NDVI = \frac{0.30 - 0.25}{0.30 + 0.25} = \frac{0.05}{0.55} = 0.091$$

For stressed vegetation, suppose $R_{NIR} = 0.35$ and $R_{red} = 0.15$:

$$NDVI = \frac{0.35 - 0.15}{0.35 + 0.15} = \frac{0.20}{0.50} = 0.400$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation scale (commonly used in precision agriculture):**

| NDVI Range | Interpretation |
|------------|----------------|
| $< 0.1$ | Bare soil, no vegetation |
| $0.1$ – $0.3$ | Sparse/stressed vegetation |
| $0.3$ – $0.6$ | Moderate vegetation, may need attention |
| $0.6$ – $0.9$ | Dense, healthy vegetation |

> [!NOTE]
> NDVI is the **single most-used remote sensing index** in precision agriculture. KTU frequently asks for its calculation and interpretation in Part B questions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: RTD (PT100) Resistance vs. Temperature

**Step 1 — Linear approximation (simple form):**

$$R_T = R_0 \left(1 + \alpha T\right)$$

**Step 2 — Numerical example:**
A PT100 has $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $0\ ^\circ\text{C}$, with $\alpha = 0.00385\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.
What is the resistance at $T = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$?

$$R_{50} = 100 \left(1 + 0.00385 \times 50\right)$$

$$R_{50} = 100 \left(1 + 0.1925\right)$$

$$R_{50} = 100 \times 1.1925 = 119.25\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — For higher accuracy, use the Callendar-Van Dusen equation:**

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3\right]$$

For $T \ge 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$, the $C$ term drops out (i.e., $C = 0$ for $T \ge 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$). Standard values for platinum:
- $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
- $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$
- $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-4}$ (for $T < 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$)

This equation matches the ITS-90 standard for platinum RTDs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection Guide for Common PA Applications

| Application | Primary Sensor(s) | Actuator(s) Controlled | Key Performance Criterion |
|-------------|-------------------|-------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Drip irrigation automation** | TDR/FDR soil moisture sensor, soil temperature | Solenoid valve, motorized ball valve | Moisture accuracy ±2% VWC |
| **Greenhouse climate control** | Air temp/humidity, $\text{CO}_2$ sensor, PAR sensor | Exhaust fans, heaters, shade nets, $\text{CO}_2$ valves | Response time < 1 min |
| **Variable Rate Fertilization (VRF)** | Optical N-sensor (crop reflectance), GPS | Spreader motor, rate controller | Spatial accuracy < 1 m (RTK-GPS) |
| **Autonomous tractor guidance** | RTK-GPS, IMU, LiDAR | Hydraulic steering valve, throttle actuator | Position error < 2.5 cm |
| **Weed detection & spot spraying** | Multispectral camera, AI processor | PWM-controlled solenoid sprayer nozzle | Latency < 100 ms |
| **Crop yield monitoring** | Load cell, optical yield sensor | Grain elevator, gate control | Mass flow error < 1% |
| **Livestock monitoring** | $\text{NH}_3$ sensor, body temperature, accelerometer | Ventilation fans, feeder motors | Sensor battery > 1 year |
| **Drone-based crop scouting** | RGB/NDVI camera, thermal camera | Drone motors, gimbal servos | Gimbal stabilization ±0.1° |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:**
A capacitive soil moisture sensor with a plate area $A = 50\ \text{cm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$ and plate separation $d = 5\ \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$ is connected in an LC oscillator with $L = 10\ \text{mH} = 10 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{H}$.

The dry soil has $\varepsilon_r = 4$, and saturated soil has $\varepsilon_r = 30$.
Take $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$.

**(a)** Calculate the oscillator frequency for dry and wet soil.

**(b)** If the system gives a frequency reading of $250\ \text{kHz}$, what is the dielectric constant of the soil?

### Solution:

**Part (a):**

Capacitance formula:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

For dry soil ($\varepsilon_r = 4$):

$$C_{dry} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 50 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{dry} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{dry} = \frac{17.708 \times 10^{-14}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.5416 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \approx 35.4\ \text{pF}$$

Frequency:

$$f_{dry} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC_{dry}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{10 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.5416 \times 10^{-11}}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{3.5416 \times 10^{-13}}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5.951 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$f_{dry} = \frac{1}{3.739 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 267.4\ \text{kHz}$$

For wet soil ($\varepsilon_r = 30$):

$$C_{wet} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 30 \times 50 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$= \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-1}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$= \frac{13.281 \times 10^{-13}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.6562 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F} \approx 265.6\ \text{pF}$$

Frequency:

$$f_{wet} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{10 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.6562 \times 10^{-10}}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{2.6562 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.6298 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_{wet} = \frac{1}{1.0241 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 97.6\ \text{kHz}$$

**Part (b):**

Given $f = 250\ \text{kHz} = 250 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Hz}$.

From $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$, solve for $C$:

$$C = \frac{1}{(2\pi f)^2 L} = \frac{1}{(2\pi \times 250 \times 10^3)^2 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$(2\pi f)^2 = (2\pi \times 250000)^2 = (1.5708 \times 10^6)^2 = 2.4674 \times 10^{12}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{2.4674 \times 10^{12} \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{1}{2.4674 \times 10^{10}} \approx 4.0526 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \approx 40.5\ \text{pF}$$

Now solve for $\varepsilon_r$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \implies \varepsilon_r = \frac{C \cdot d}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot A}$$

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{4.0526 \times 10^{-11} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 50 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{2.0263 \times 10^{-13}}{4.427 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 4.58$$

So the soil has a dielectric constant of $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.58$, indicating relatively dry soil with low moisture content.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sensor–Actuator Feedback Loop in Precision Agriculture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Field Process: Soil/Crop/Air] --> B[Sensor Array: Moisture, Temp, pH, NDVI]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning: Amplify, Filter, Linearize]
    C --> D[ADC: Analog to Digital Conversion]
    D --> E[Microcontroller / PLC: Decision Logic]
    E --> F[PWM / Driver Circuit]
    F --> G[Actuator: Valve, Motor, Pump, Sprayer]
    G --> A
    E -.Feedback / Telemetry.-> H[Cloud / Dashboard / Mobile App]
    H -.User Setpoint.-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The sensor "perceives" the field. The signal conditioning prepares the raw signal. The ADC digitizes it. The microcontroller compares with a setpoint and commands the actuator, which physically modifies the field — completing the loop.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Components Inside a Smart Irrigation Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeA[Power Subsystem]
        bat[Battery 12V] --> reg[Voltage Regulator 5V and 3.3V]
        sol[Solar Panel] --> chg[Charge Controller] --> bat
    end

    subgraph nodeB[Sensing Subsystem]
        s1[Soil Moisture FDR] --> sc1[Signal Conditioner]
        s2[Soil Temp DS18B20] --> sc2[Sensor Interface]
        s3[Air Humidity DHT22] --> sc3[Sensor Interface]
        sc1 --> muxc[Analog Mux and ADC]
        sc2 --> muxc
        sc3 --> muxc
    end

    subgraph nodeC[Control and Communication]
        muxc --> mcu[Microcontroller ESP32]
        mcu --> wifi[WiFi / LoRa Module]
        wifi --> cloud[(Cloud Server: MQTT)]
    end

    subgraph nodeD[Actuation Subsystem]
        mcu --> drv[MOSFET Driver]
        drv --> valve[Solenoid Valve 12V]
        mcu --> relay[Relay Module]
        relay --> pump[DC Water Pump]
    end

    reg --> mcu
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Control with PID

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint e.g. 30% VWC] --> sumJunction
    MV[Measured Value from Sensor] --> sumJunction
    sumJunction[Error: e equals SP minus MV] --> PID[PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    PID --> u[Control Signal u of t]
    u --> actA[Actuator: Valve or Pump]
    actA --> proc[Process: Field Soil]
    proc --> fb[Sensor: Moisture Probe]
    fb --> MV
```

---

## 4.4 Hierarchy Diagram: Classification of Sensors in PA

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[PA Sensors]
    root --> soil[Soil Sensors]
    root --> air[Air / Weather Sensors]
    root --> crop[Crop / Plant Sensors]
    root --> pos[Position and Navigation Sensors]

    soil --> s1[Moisture: TDR, FDR, Capacitive, Tensiometer]
    soil --> s2[Temperature: Thermistor, RTD]

    air --> a1[Temperature and Humidity: DHT, SHT]
    air --> a2[Wind: Anemometer]
    air --> a3[Rain: Tipping Bucket, Optical]
    air --> a4[Solar Radiation: Pyranometer]

    crop --> c1[Multispectral: NDVI Cameras]
    crop --> c2[Chlorophyll Meter SPAD]
    crop --> c3[Thermal IR Canopy Sensor]

    pos --> p1[GPS and RTK-GPS]
    pos --> p2[IMU Accelerometer Gyro]
    pos --> p3[LiDAR, Ultrasonic]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Hydraulic Steering System (Tractor Autosteer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrl[Autopilot Controller] --> valveBlock[Proportional Hydraulic Valve Block]
    valveBlock --> cylL[Left Steering Cylinder]
    valveBlock --> cylR[Right Steering Cylinder]
    cylL --> wheels[Wheels and Axle]
    cylR --> wheels
    wheels --> fb2[Wheel Angle Encoder]
    fb2 --> ctrl
    gps[RTK-GPS] --> ctrl
    imu[IMU] --> ctrl
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **closed-loop hydraulic actuation system**. The proportional valve adjusts flow direction to the cylinders, the wheels turn, and the encoder provides feedback. The controller uses RTK-GPS and IMU data to know the desired heading. This is the same principle used in **autonomous tractors in modern precision agriculture**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    amp --> filt[Filter<br/>Low-pass or Notch]
    filt --> lin[Linearizer<br/>Look-up Table]
    lin --> iso[Isolation<br/>Optocoupler]
    iso --> adc[ADC<br/>12-bit or 16-bit]
    adc --> mcu[Microcontroller]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a sensor. List four types of sensors used in precision agriculture with one application of each. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **sensor** is a device that detects a physical, chemical, or biological quantity and converts it into an electrical signal that can be measured, recorded, or processed.

| Sensor Type | Example | Application in PA |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|
| Soil moisture sensor | Capacitive FDR probe | Automated drip irrigation |
| Optical / multispectral camera | NDVI camera on drone | Crop health mapping |
| GPS / RTK-GPS | RTK receiver | Tractor autosteer / VRA |
| Thermistor / RTD | DS18B20 | Soil temperature monitoring |

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator. State one example of each in a precision agriculture spraying system. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Sensor | Actuator |
|-------|--------|----------|
| **Function** | Detects / measures a physical quantity | Converts energy into physical action |
| **Direction of energy** | Environment → device (input) | Controller → environment (output) |
| **Example in sprayer system** | Ultrasonic sensor to detect crop canopy height | Solenoid valve / nozzle control to start/stop spraying |
| **Signal type** | Output is a measurable electrical signal | Receives a control signal and produces motion/flow |

In a **precision sprayer**, an ultrasonic sensor measures distance to the crop, the controller decides which nozzles should be active, and **PWM-driven solenoid valves** are the actuators that actually open/close the spray nozzles.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries **14 marks** split into (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **TDR (Time Domain Reflectometry) soil moisture sensor** with a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship between travel time and dielectric constant. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A TDR sensor is an electronic device that measures soil volumetric water content by sending a high-frequency electromagnetic pulse along a waveguide and timing the reflection from the end of the rod.

**Working Principle (Step-by-step):**

1. A **pulse generator** (typically producing a fast-rising edge with rise time of $\sim 200\ \text{ps}$) launches an electromagnetic step pulse into a coaxial cable connected to stainless-steel rods (waveguides) embedded in the soil.

2. The pulse travels down the rods at a velocity determined by the **dielectric permittivity** $\varepsilon_r$ of the surrounding soil.

3. At the end of the rods, the pulse encounters an impedance mismatch and reflects back.

4. The TDR instrument measures the **two-way travel time** $\Delta t$ between launch and return of the pulse.

5. The bulk dielectric permittivity $\varepsilon_b$ (approximately equal to $\varepsilon_r$ of the soil) is computed.

**Derivation:**

The velocity of an EM wave in a medium with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ is:

$$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ is the speed of light in vacuum.

The pulse travels a length $L$ down the rod and a length $L$ back (total distance $2L$). The travel time is:

$$\Delta t = \frac{2L}{v} = \frac{2L \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$$

Solving for $\varepsilon_r$:

$$\varepsilon_r = \left(\frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2L}\right)^2$$

The volumetric water content $\theta$ is then obtained using the **Topp equation**:

$$\theta = -5.3 \times 10^{-2} + 2.92 \times 10^{-2} \varepsilon_r - 5.5 \times 10^{-4} \varepsilon_r^2 + 4.3 \times 10^{-6} \varepsilon_r^3$$

**Key Advantages:** High accuracy (±1–2% VWC), minimal soil-type calibration needed, real-time measurement, suitable for automated irrigation.

**Disadvantages:** Costlier than capacitive sensors, requires careful rod installation.

---

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the architecture of a **closed-loop precision irrigation system**. Define the role of the controller, the actuator, and the feedback path. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram).**

A closed-loop precision irrigation system continuously measures soil moisture, compares it with a desired setpoint, and activates actuators to correct deviations.

**Block-by-block explanation:**

1. **Field (Process Variable):** The actual soil moisture $\theta$ in the field is the variable being controlled.

2. **Sensor (TDR / FDR probe):** Measures soil moisture in real-time and produces an electrical signal (voltage or frequency).

3. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifies the weak signal, filters 50/60 Hz noise, and linearizes the output.

4. **ADC:** Converts the analog signal into a digital value (e.g., 12-bit, 0–4095 representing 0–3.3 V).

5. **Microcontroller / PLC (Controller):**
   - Receives digital moisture value $MV$.
   - Compares with user-set setpoint $SP$.
   - Computes error: $e = SP - MV$.
   - Applies **PID control law** to compute control output $u(t)$.
   - Sends appropriate command to the driver.

6. **Driver Circuit:** Interfaces the low-power microcontroller to the higher-power actuator (e.g., using a relay, MOSFET, or motor driver IC).

7. **Actuator (Solenoid valve / Motorized valve / Pump):** Physically opens/closes the water line to deliver or cut off irrigation.

8. **Feedback Path:** The sensor continuously monitors the field, providing the feedback that closes the loop.

**PID Equation:**

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Tuning example:** If $K_p = 5$, $K_i = 0.1$, $K_d = 0.5$, and at a moment $e(t) = 4$ (moisture is 4% below setpoint), the proportional term alone is $5 \times 4 = 20$, which gets converted to a PWM duty cycle (e.g., 20% of 255 ≈ 51).

**Advantages of closed-loop:** Self-correcting, water-saving, prevents over- and under-irrigation, energy-efficient.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is **NDVI**? Derive the formula and explain its use in precision agriculture. A multispectral camera measures $R_{NIR} = 0.45$ and $R_{red} = 0.08$ from a crop canopy. Calculate the NDVI and interpret the result. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The **Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI)** is a graphical indicator that uses near-infrared and red reflectance values to assess whether the target being observed contains live green vegetation or not.

**Formula Derivation:**
NDVI is defined as the normalized difference between NIR and Red reflectance:

$$NDVI = \frac{R_{NIR} - R_{red}}{R_{NIR} + R_{red}}$$

The "normalization" (dividing by the sum) makes the index dimensionless and bounded between $-1$ and $+1$. This removes the effect of varying illumination intensity between scenes.

**Physical Basis:**
- **Chlorophyll** in healthy leaves strongly absorbs Red light for photosynthesis → low $R_{red}$.
- **Mesophyll cell structure** in leaves strongly scatters and reflects NIR light → high $R_{NIR}$.
- The difference, when normalized, becomes a strong indicator of vegetation vigor.

**Numerical Calculation:**
Given $R_{NIR} = 0.45$ and $R_{red} = 0.08$:

$$NDVI = \frac{0.45 - 0.08}{0.45 + 0.08} = \frac{0.37}{0.53} \approx 0.698$$

**Interpretation:** NDVI = 0.698 falls in the range $0.6$–$0.9$, indicating **dense, healthy, green vegetation** with high photosynthetic activity. The field likely does not need immediate nitrogen top-dressing or pest intervention.

**Use in PA:**
1. **Variable Rate Application (VRA):** Generate prescription maps for fertilizer/pesticide.
2. **Drone/UAV-based crop scouting:** Detect stressed patches early.
3. **Yield prediction:** Statistical correlation between NDVI and final yield.
4. **Irrigation scheduling:** Combine with canopy thermal imaging for water stress.

---

**(b)** Explain the working of a **solenoid valve** as an actuator in a drip irrigation system. With a suitable diagram, describe **Normally Closed (NC)** and **Normally Open (NO)** types and state where each is preferred. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
A **solenoid valve** is an electromechanically operated valve that uses an electric current through a solenoid coil to control the opening/closing of a fluid (water) path.

**Construction:**
- A **solenoid coil** wound on a bobbin.
- A **ferromagnetic plunger** (or armature) that can move linearly.
- A **spring** that holds the plunger in its rest position.
- A **valve seat and orifice** that controls water flow.
- **Inlet and outlet** ports.

**Working:**
- When the coil is **de-energized**, the spring pushes the plunger onto the valve seat, blocking flow (in NC type).
- When the coil is **energized** (DC voltage applied, e.g., 12 V or 24 V), a magnetic field pulls the plunger against the spring, opening the orifice and allowing water to flow.
- When power is removed, the spring returns the plunger to its rest position, closing the valve.

**Types:**

| Type | Rest State (Power OFF) | Energized State (Power ON) | Preferred Use |
|------|------------------------|----------------------------|----------------|
| **Normally Closed (NC)** | Closed (no flow) | Open (water flows) | **Drip irrigation** (fail-safe: no water spillage on power failure) |
| **Normally Open (NO)** | Open (water flows) | Closed (no flow) | Cooling systems, exhaust lines (fail-safe: continue flow if power lost) |

**Why NC is preferred in drip irrigation:**
- In case of power failure, the valve **automatically closes**, preventing over-watering and water wastage.
- Most irrigation controllers are designed to energize the solenoid **only when watering is required** (default OFF), which aligns with NC type.

**Driver Interface:**
A microcontroller (e.g., Arduino, ESP32) cannot directly drive a 12 V solenoid. A **MOSFET driver** (e.g., IRF540N) or a **relay module** is used. The gate is driven by a GPIO pin through a resistor, and a **flyback diode** (1N4007) is connected across the coil to protect the transistor from back-EMF.

**PWM Control for Variable Flow:**
By varying the duty cycle $D$ of a high-frequency PWM signal, the average flow can be regulated proportionally — used in **precision fertigation** systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Sensor and Actuator Roles** — Many students write "solenoid valve senses water flow." Wrong. The solenoid valve is an **actuator**; a **flow meter** is the sensor.
> 2. **Skipping Units in Numerical Problems** — Always mention units for capacitance (F or pF), frequency (Hz), dielectric constant (dimensionless), and moisture (%VWC).
> 3. **Writing NDVI Formula without Explaining Red and NIR** — Don't just write the formula; explicitly state that $R_{red}$ is red-band reflectance and $R_{NIR}$ is near-infrared reflectance, and the wavelengths involved.
> 4. **Forgetting Closed-Loop in Automation Questions** — A "smart irrigation" question always expects a **feedback loop**. Drawing a one-way arrow from sensor to valve is incomplete. Always show feedback back to the controller.
> 5. **Ignoring Calibration** — Sensor outputs are not directly meaningful. Mention **calibration curves, look-up tables, or Topp equation** at least once for full marks.
> 6. **Using Wrong Direction for Hysteresis** — Hysteresis is the difference in output for the **same input** measured during **ascending** vs. **descending** cycles — not just "lag."
> 7. **Mixing Up Normally Open / Normally Closed** — Remember the mnemonic: "**NC = Normally Closed = Closed at rest = Energize to Open**." Test yourself before writing the answer.
> 8. **Not Mentioning Signal Conditioning** — A bare sensor connected to a controller is unrealistic in a board exam answer. Always mention **amplification, filtering, ADC** in between.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist (must memorize before KTU exam):**

- **Sensor** = detects input from environment, converts to electrical signal.
- **Actuator** = receives control signal, converts energy into physical action.
- **Soil moisture** sensors include **TDR** (travel-time), **FDR/Capacitive** ($C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$), **Tensiometer** (matric potential), **GMS** (resistance).
- **Temperature** sensors: **Thermistor** (NTC, Steinhart–Hart), **RTD/PT100** ($R_T = R_0(1+\alpha T)$), **Thermocouple** (Seebeck effect, $V = S \Delta T$).
- **NDVI** $= \dfrac{R_{NIR} - R_{red}}{R_{NIR} + R_{red}}$; range $-1$ to $+1$; healthy crop $\approx 0.7$–$0.9$.
- **GPS** gives lat/lon/alt; **RTK-GPS** improves to cm-level accuracy.
- **IMU** = Accelerometer + Gyroscope + Magnetometer.
- **Solenoid valve** = electromagnetic actuator for water/air control; **NC preferred in irrigation** (fail-safe).
- **DC motor** speed $\propto$ voltage; **Stepper** for open-loop precision positioning; **Servo** for closed-loop positioning.
- **PWM** average voltage $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$.
- **Hydraulic** actuator = high force, used in tractor steering; **Pneumatic** = compressed air, used in seed metering.
- **Closed-loop system** requires: Sensor → Signal Conditioner → ADC → Controller → Driver → Actuator → Process → back to Sensor.
- **PID equation:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d\, de/dt$.
- **Wheatstone bridge** balance condition: $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$ (used in strain-gauge load cells).
- **Strain gauge:** $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ (gauge factor $G$ typically $\approx 2$ for metal).
- **ADC resolution:** $Q = V_{ref}/2^n$ (for an $n$-bit ADC).
- **Sensitivity, Accuracy, Precision, Range, Resolution, Linearity, Hysteresis, Response Time** are the 8 key sensor performance metrics tested in KTU.
- **Signal conditioning chain:** Amplify → Filter → Linearize → Isolate → ADC → Controller.
- **Topp equation** gives soil moisture from dielectric constant: $\theta = -5.3 \times 10^{-2} + 2.92 \times 10^{-2} \varepsilon_r - 5.5 \times 10^{-4} \varepsilon_r^2 + 4.3 \times 10^{-6} \varepsilon_r^3$.
- **Calibration** is mandatory before any sensor is deployed in a real PA system — board examiners explicitly test this awareness.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Sensors and Actuators Notes**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech Agricultural Engineering / Related Streams.*
