topics.title: Control Electronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT861 - SENSORS, AI AND ROBOTICS IN PRECISION AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control Electronics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Electronics** is the interdisciplinary sub-domain of electronics engineering that deals with the design, analysis, and implementation of electronic circuits and embedded systems responsible for the *regulation*, *actuation*, and *feedback processing* of automated agricultural machinery and processes.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **Precision Agriculture (PEAGT861)**, control electronics forms the critical bridge between the *sensing layer* (which gathers field data) and the *actuation layer* (which physically performs agricultural tasks such as irrigation, spraying, seeding, harvesting, and steering).

The discipline encompasses:

1. **Signal Conditioning Electronics** — Amplifiers, filters, and analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) that prepare raw sensor signals for the controller.
2. **Embedded Control Units** — Microcontrollers (MCU) and microprocessors (MPU) that execute decision logic.
3. **Driver and Power Electronics** — MOSFET/IGBT gate drivers, H-bridges, and relay modules that switch high-current actuators.
4. **Actuator Interface Circuits** — PWM generators, current limiters, and optocouplers for safe load handling.
5. **Feedback and Closed-Loop Systems** — Error detectors, PID controllers, and compensators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Nervous System**
>
> Imagine a farmer standing in a wheat field. His **eyes** (sensors) detect that the soil is dry. This information travels via **nerves** (signal conditioning) to his **brain** (microcontroller). The brain decides to open the water valve. The **hand** (actuator) is commanded via motor signals (PWM) to turn the valve. The brain continuously watches the moisture reading (feedback) and adjusts the valve opening until the soil is optimally moist.

In precision agriculture, control electronics is essentially the **artificial nervous system** of the farm machinery. Without it, a smart tractor, an autonomous irrigation boom, or a drone-based sprayer would have no way to translate sensor data into physical action.

| Agriculture Application | Electronic Subsystem Used |
|---|---|
| Variable Rate Irrigation | Solenoid valve drivers + PWM motor control |
| Auto-Steering Tractor | GPS module + servo/brushless motor driver |
| Drone Spraying | ESC (Electronic Speed Controller) + flight controller |
| Greenhouse Climate Control | Relay modules + temperature/humidity sensors |
| Robotic Harvesting | Stepper/servo drivers + vision system interface |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** In KTU-style problems, the supply voltage commonly used in agricultural control boards is **12 V DC** (battery/solar) or **24 V DC** (tractors), stepped down to **3.3 V / 5 V** for logic-level microcontrollers. Always remember these as canonical rails: **24 V → 12 V → 5 V → 3.3 V**.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning in Precision Agriculture |
|---|---|
| **Actuator** | A device that converts electrical energy into physical action (e.g., valve, motor, pump) |
| **Transducer** | A device that converts one form of energy to another (sensor + actuator combined) |
| **H-Bridge** | A 4-switch circuit that drives a DC motor in both forward and reverse directions |
| **PWM** | Pulse Width Modulation — varying the duty cycle of a square wave to control average power |
| **PID** | Proportional–Integral–Derivative controller used for accurate closed-loop control |
| **Optocoupler** | An isolation device that separates high-voltage load circuits from low-voltage logic |
| **Dead Band** | The range of input values for which the controller produces no output change |
| **Duty Cycle (D)** | The fraction of time a PWM signal is HIGH, expressed as a percentage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Typical Agricultural Control System

A control electronics module in precision agriculture is structured into **five hierarchical layers**, each performing a distinct role:

### Layer 1 — Sensing Front-End
- **Function:** Convert physical/chemical variables (soil moisture, pH, temperature, NPK) into electrical signals (voltage, current, resistance, frequency).
- **Typical Output:** 0–5 V analog, 4–20 mA current loop, or digital (I²C, SPI).
- **Agriculture Example:** Capacitive soil moisture sensor (varying capacitance), NPK selective ion sensor (varying voltage).

### Layer 2 — Signal Conditioning
- **Function:** Amplify, filter, linearize, and offset the raw sensor signal.
- **Sub-Blocks:**
  * Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128, gain 1–1000)
  * Active low-pass filter (cut-off $f_c$)
  * Level shifter (5 V ↔ 3.3 V)
  * ADC front-end

### Layer 3 — Embedded Controller
- **Function:** Execute the control algorithm (often PID or rule-based) and generate output commands.
- **Common MCUs in Agriculture:** ATmega328P (Arduino Uno), ESP32 (Wi-Fi-enabled), STM32 (industrial-grade), Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040).

### Layer 4 — Driver / Power Electronics
- **Function:** Translate low-current logic signals (3.3 V, ~10 mA) into high-current/voltage signals capable of driving actuators.
- **Key Circuits:**
  * MOSFET/IGBT gate driver
  * H-bridge motor driver (e.g., BTS7960, L298N)
  * Solid-state relay (SSR) for AC line switching
  * Electromechanical relay (EMR) for high-current DC switching

### Layer 5 — Actuator / Load
- **Function:** Perform the physical work.
- **Examples in Agriculture:**
  * 12 V DC diaphragm pump (for fertigation)
  * 24 V DC solenoid valve (for drip irrigation)
  * Brushless DC (BLDC) motor (for autonomous tractor wheels)
  * Servo motor (for steering/nozzle angle)
  * Stepper motor (for seed metering)

## 2.2 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) — The Workhorse of Agricultural Actuator Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PWM in Precision Agriculture?**
> Most agricultural loads (motors, solenoids, heaters, LED grow lights) require *variable* power — not just ON/OFF. Linear regulation wastes enormous energy as heat. PWM delivers *variable average power* at high efficiency (>90%) by rapidly switching the supply.

The **average voltage** delivered to a DC load by a PWM signal is:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$$

where $D$ is the duty cycle (0 to 1), and $V_s$ is the supply voltage.

The **average power** dissipated in a resistive-like load is:

$$P_{avg} = D \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{R}$$

The **switching frequency** $f_{sw}$ must be chosen carefully:
- **Too low (< 1 kHz):** Audible noise, motor cogging, solenoid chatter.
- **Too high (> 50 kHz):** Excessive switching losses in MOSFETs, EMI issues.
- **Sweet spot for agriculture:** **1 kHz to 25 kHz** (above human hearing for motors).

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Frequencies Used in Practice:**
> - DC motor speed control: **1 kHz – 8 kHz**
> - Servo motor (hobby): **50 Hz** (frame rate, 1–2 ms pulse width)
> - LED grow lights: **> 200 Hz** (to avoid flicker stress on plants)
> - Solenoid valves: **> 100 Hz** to avoid mechanical resonance

## 2.3 H-Bridge Motor Driver — Bidirectional Control

An **H-bridge** uses four switches (S1, S2, S3, S4) arranged in an "H" configuration around a DC motor. By activating diagonal pairs, the current direction through the motor is reversed, enabling **forward, reverse, and braking (regenerative) modes**.

| S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Motor Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ON | OFF | OFF | ON | Forward |
| OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Reverse |
| ON | ON | OFF | OFF | Brake (low-side) |
| OFF | OFF | ON | ON | Brake (high-side) |
| OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Coast (free run) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Shoot-through hazard:** Never turn ON S1 and S2 (or S3 and S4) simultaneously — this short-circuits the supply and destroys the MOSFETs. All commercial H-bridge ICs (L298N, DRV8871) include **dead-time insertion** of typically **100 ns – 500 ns** to prevent this.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ | Average DC voltage from PWM | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $P_{avg} = D \cdot V_s^2 / R$ | Average power in resistive load | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $f_{sw} = 1 / T$ | PWM switching frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| 4 | $D = t_{ON} / T$ | Duty cycle definition | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_{out} = G \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$ | Instrumentation amplifier output | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $f_c = 1 / (2 \pi R C)$ | RC low-pass filter cut-off | Hertz (Hz) |
| 7 | $V_{res} = (ADC \times V_{ref}) / (2^n - 1)$ | ADC digital-to-voltage conversion | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID controller output | Volts or % |
| 9 | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Control error (Setpoint − Process Variable) | Same as sensor units |
| 10 | $\tau = R C$ | RC time constant | Seconds (s) |
| 11 | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | MOSFET conduction loss | Volts (V) |
| 12 | $P_{cond} = I^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} \cdot D$ | MOSFET conduction power loss | Watts (W) |
| 13 | $V_{OH} \geq V_{IH}$ and $V_{OL} \leq V_{IL}$ | Logic-level compatibility check | Volts (V) |
| 14 | $I_{coil} = V_{cc} / R_{coil}$ | Relay coil current | Amperes (A) |
| 15 | $BW = f_{sw} / 10$ | Rule-of-thumb signal bandwidth for PWM systems | Hertz (Hz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Domain Constraints:**
> - $0 \le D \le 1$ (duty cycle bounded between 0 and 1)
> - $f_{sw} > 10 \times f_{signal,max}$ (Nyquist-like criterion for PWM)
> - $V_{GS(th)} < V_{logic}$ for logic-level MOSFETs
> - $I_{driver} > I_{peak}$ for motor drivers

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern precision agriculture, control electronics enables:

1. **Variable Rate Technology (VRT):** PWM-driven solenoid valves modulate fertilizer/pesticide flow in real time based on prescription maps loaded from GIS data.
2. **Autonomous Navigation:** GPS-corrected steering using servo motors controlled by 50 Hz PWM signals.
3. **Greenhouse Automation:** Relay-driven fans, heaters, and vents maintain microclimate (temperature, humidity, CO₂) within ±0.5 °C of setpoint.
4. **Drone-Based Spraying:** Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs) regulate BLDC motor RPM at **12 kHz – 48 kHz** switching frequency for stable flight.
5. **Robotic Weeding:** Stepper motors drive precise tool movement with **open-loop positioning** (no encoder needed for low-speed applications).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Voltage of a PWM Signal

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the expression for the average DC voltage delivered to a load by a PWM waveform.

### Given:
- Supply voltage: $V_s$
- Switching period: $T$
- ON-time within each period: $t_{ON}$
- OFF-time: $t_{OFF} = T - t_{ON}$

### Step 1: Define the PWM waveform as a time function

$$
v(t) =
\begin{cases}
V_s, & 0 \le t \le t_{ON} \\
0, & t_{ON} < t \le T
\end{cases}
$$

This means the signal is at $V_s$ during the ON-time and 0 V during the OFF-time.

### Step 2: Define the duty cycle

The duty cycle $D$ is the ratio of the ON-time to the total period:

$$D = \frac{t_{ON}}{T}$$

By definition, $D$ is a pure number between 0 and 1.

### Step 3: Compute the average value over one period

The average (DC equivalent) of a periodic signal over one period $T$ is given by:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t)\, dt$$

### Step 4: Substitute the piecewise function

Split the integral into ON and OFF intervals:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{t_{ON}} V_s\, dt + \int_{t_{ON}}^{T} 0\, dt \right]$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the integral

The first integral gives $V_s \cdot t_{ON}$, and the second integral is zero:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \cdot V_s \cdot t_{ON}$$

### Step 6: Substitute $D = t_{ON}/T$

$$V_{avg} = V_s \cdot \frac{t_{ON}}{T} = V_s \cdot D$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s}$$

**Interpretation:** The PWM signal behaves electrically (on average) like a DC source whose value is the supply voltage scaled by the duty cycle. This is precisely why PWM is used to "dim" LED grow lights or "slow down" DC irrigation pumps without wasting energy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter Cut-off Frequency

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the cut-off frequency of an RC low-pass filter, which is the building block of every signal conditioning circuit in agricultural electronics.

### Given:
- Resistor $R$ in series, capacitor $C$ to ground, input $V_{in}$, output $V_{out}$ taken across $C$.

### Step 1: Apply the voltage divider in the frequency domain (s-domain)

The transfer function of an RC low-pass filter is:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1 / (sC)}{R + 1/(sC)} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

### Step 2: Substitute $s = j\omega$ for sinusoidal steady-state

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Step 3: Compute the magnitude response

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

### Step 4: Define the cut-off frequency where magnitude drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$

At the cut-off frequency $\omega_c$:

$$\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

This requires:

$$\omega_c RC = 1$$

### Step 5: Solve for $\omega_c$

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

Converting to Hz using $f_c = \omega_c / (2\pi)$:

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}}$$

**Interpretation:** For an agricultural NPK sensor whose output has a 100 Hz noise component, choosing $R = 1.6\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$ gives $f_c = 99.5\,\text{Hz}$, perfectly filtering the noise while passing the slow-changing nutrient signal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PID Controller Output

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the time-domain equation of a PID controller used for closed-loop irrigation control.

### Given:
- Error signal: $e(t) = SP - PV$
- Proportional gain: $K_p$
- Integral gain: $K_i$
- Derivative gain: $K_d$

### Step 1: Start with the parallel form of PID

The controller output is the sum of three terms:

$$u(t) = u_P(t) + u_I(t) + u_D(t)$$

### Step 2: Express each term

- **Proportional:** reacts to current error → $u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$
- **Integral:** reacts to accumulated past error → $u_I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau$
- **Derivative:** reacts to rate of change of error → $u_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$

### Step 3: Combine

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}}$$

**Interpretation:** For a soil-moisture-based drip irrigation valve:
- $K_p$ provides immediate response to dry soil.
- $K_i$ eliminates steady-state error (ensures soil reaches the exact setpoint moisture).
- $K_d$ damps oscillations caused by water transport delay between valve and root zone.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ADC Conversion Resolution

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the voltage resolution of an n-bit ADC used to digitize a sensor signal.

### Step 1: Total number of discrete output codes

An $n$-bit ADC produces $2^n$ possible codes, ranging from 0 to $2^n - 1$.

### Step 2: Voltage corresponding to one LSB (Least Significant Bit)

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

### Step 3: General conversion formula

For a digital code $N$:

$$V_{in} = N \cdot \Delta V = N \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{V_{in} = \frac{N \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}}$$

### Worked Example (KTU-style):

> **Problem:** A 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$ reads a soil moisture sensor output of **2.35 V**. Find the digital code $N$.

**Solution:**

$$N = \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}} = \frac{2.35 \times 1024}{5} = \frac{2406.4}{5} = 481.28$$

Rounded to the nearest integer: $N = 481$.

The **resolution** (LSB size) is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{5}{1024} = 4.88\,\text{mV}$$

This is the smallest voltage change the ADC can detect — a critical specification when choosing MCUs for low-amplitude agricultural sensor signals.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Actuator Driver Topologies

| Parameter | BJT Driver | MOSFET Driver | IGBT Driver | Relay Driver |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Switching Speed | Medium (µs) | Very Fast (ns) | Fast (µs) | Very Slow (ms) |
| Voltage Rating | < 100 V | Up to 1000 V | > 600 V | Any |
| Current Rating | < 10 A | 10–100 A | 50–1000 A | 5–30 A |
| ON-State Loss | High ($V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$) | Low ($R_{DS(on)} \approx 10\,\text{m}\Omega$) | Medium | Zero (contact) |
| Drive Complexity | Simple (base resistor) | Needs gate driver (10–15 V) | Needs gate driver | Needs flyback diode |
| Best For Agriculture | Small solenoid valves | DC pumps, BLDC ESCs | High-power tractor drives | Mains-powered greenhouse fans |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Agricultural Control Loop

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the closed-loop interaction between sensing, decision, and actuation subsystems in a precision agriculture application (e.g., variable-rate irrigation).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Field Sensor: Soil Moisture and pH] --> B[Signal Conditioning: Amplifier and Filter]
    B --> C[ADC: Analog to Digital Converter]
    C --> D[Microcontroller: ATmega328P or ESP32]
    D --> E[Control Algorithm: PID or Rule Based]
    E --> F[PWM Generator and Driver]
    F --> G[H Bridge or MOSFET Switch]
    G --> H[Actuator: Solenoid Valve or DC Pump]
    H --> I[Physical Process: Water Flow to Crop]
    I --> A
    D --> J[Display and HMI: LCD and Mobile App]
    D --> K[Wireless Link: WiFi or LoRa to Cloud]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The sensor reads the field state. The signal is conditioned, digitized, and fed to the MCU, which computes a control action. The driver switches the actuator, which alters the physical process. The *loop closes* through the sensor, providing feedback.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — PID Control System for Drip Irrigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Setpoint: Target Soil Moisture Percent] --> SUM[Comparator: Summing Junction]
    PV[Process Variable: Actual Soil Moisture] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal: e of t]
    ERR --> PID[PID Controller: Kp Ki Kd]
    PID --> CV[Control Variable: Valve Opening Percent]
    CV --> PLANT[Plant: Soil and Root Zone]
    PLANT --> PV
```

**Insight:** The summing junction subtracts the process variable from the setpoint, generating the error. The PID controller processes this error, and the output drives the plant, which in turn alters the process variable — completing the loop.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — H-Bridge Motor Driver Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC[DC Supply: 12V or 24V Battery] --> S1[Switch S1: High Side Left]
    VCC --> S3[Switch S3: High Side Right]
    S1 --> M[DC Motor: Pump or Wheel]
    S3 --> M
    M --> S2[Switch S2: Low Side Left]
    M --> S4[Switch S4: Low Side Right]
    S2 --> GND[Ground]
    S4 --> GND
    LOGIC[Logic Inputs: IN1 IN2 IN3 IN4 from MCU] --> S1
    LOGIC --> S2
    LOGIC --> S3
    LOGIC --> S4
    LOGIC --> DEADTIME[Dead Time Generator: 200ns]
    DEADTIME -.-> S1
    DEADTIME -.-> S2
    DEADTIME -.-> S3
    DEADTIME -.-> S4
```

**Reading the diagram:** Four switches in an "H" pattern control a DC motor. The dead-time generator inserts a small delay between switching OFF one transistor and switching ON the complementary one — this prevents *shoot-through*, a destructive short circuit.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Signal Conditioning Front-End

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Raw Sensor Output: mV Level or 4-20mA] --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier: Gain 100]
    IA --> LPF[Active Low Pass Filter: fc 100Hz]
    LPF --> LS[Level Shifter: 0-5V to 0-3.3V]
    LS --> ADC[M CU ADC Input: 12 bit]
    ADC --> PROC[Digital Processing in MCU]
    PROC --> ISO[Optocoupler: 5kV Isolation]
    ISO --> DRV[Actuator Driver Stage]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The weak, noisy sensor signal is first amplified by an instrumentation amplifier (rejects common-mode noise — important for field-deployed sensors near motors). The amplified signal is filtered, level-shifted to the MCU's ADC range, digitized, processed, and finally passed through an optocoupler (galvanic isolation) before driving the high-power actuator.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Control Layers in Smart Farming

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Layer 5: Cloud and AI: Crop Yield Prediction]
    L4[Layer 4: Edge Gateway: Raspberry Pi or Industrial PC]
    L3[Layer 3: Field Controllers: ESP32 and STM32 Nodes]
    L2[Layer 2: Sensor and Actuator Nodes: Modbus RTU]
    L1[Layer 1: Physical Field: Soil Crop Water Weather]
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

**Reading the diagram:** Data flows upward (telemetry) from the field through sensor nodes, field controllers, edge gateways, and finally to the cloud for AI analytics. Control commands flow downward from cloud decisions to actuator actions in the field.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Control Electronics with reference to precision agriculture systems. (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank, PEAGT861]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Control Electronics** in precision agriculture refers to the electronic circuits, embedded controllers, and power-driving systems that govern the *automated regulation* of agricultural processes.
>
> It performs three core functions:
> 1. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifying and filtering weak sensor signals.
> 2. **Decision Making:** Running control algorithms on a microcontroller (e.g., ESP32).
> 3. **Actuation:** Driving high-power loads (motors, valves, pumps) via drivers like H-bridges and SSRs.
>
> Example: A soil-moisture sensor → MCU → solenoid valve driver → drip irrigation system.

---

### Question 2: What is PWM? Why is it preferred over linear regulation for agricultural motor control? (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank, PEAGT861]`

**Model Answer:**

> **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** is a technique of varying the average power delivered to a load by rapidly switching the supply ON and OFF at a fixed frequency and varying the duty cycle.
>
> **Preference over linear regulation:**
> 1. **High efficiency (>90%)** — the switch is either fully ON (low loss) or fully OFF (no loss), unlike linear regulators that dissipate excess power as heat.
> 2. **Compact heat sinks** — critical in sealed, dust- and moisture-exposed agricultural enclosures.
> 3. **Precise digital control** — duty cycle is set by a microcontroller timer, enabling easy integration with sensor-based automation.
>
> Example: A **24 V DC irrigation pump** is speed-controlled by varying PWM duty cycle from 0% (off) to 100% (full speed).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Control Electronics for Automated Drip Irrigation

`[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank, PEAGT861]`

#### (a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a closed-loop control system for soil-moisture-based automated drip irrigation. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The closed-loop control system for automated drip irrigation consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Soil Moisture Sensor** — Capacitive or resistive sensor measures volumetric water content (VWC) of soil. Output: 0–3 V analog.
2. **Signal Conditioning Unit** — Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128) boosts the sensor signal; an RC low-pass filter (cut-off ≈ 1 Hz) removes high-frequency electrical noise from the field.
3. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — Embedded in the MCU (e.g., 12-bit ADC of ESP32). Digitizes the conditioned voltage.
4. **Microcontroller (MCU)** — Runs the **PID control algorithm**; compares measured moisture with the setpoint (e.g., 30% VWC).
5. **PWM Driver (MOSFET/Low-side switch)** — Drives a **12 V DC latching solenoid valve** through an N-channel MOSFET (e.g., IRLZ44N) and flyback diode.
6. **Solenoid Valve (Actuator)** — Opens/closes water flow in the drip line.
7. **Feedback Path** — Sensor continuously re-measures moisture after irrigation; loop closes.

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
Soil Moisture Sensor → Signal Conditioner → ADC → MCU (PID) → MOSFET Driver → Solenoid Valve → Drip Line → Soil
                                                                                                            ↑
                                  Feedback Signal ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ←
```

**Working:** When soil moisture falls below the setpoint, the PID algorithm increases the duty cycle of the PWM signal driving the MOSFET, which energizes the solenoid valve, releasing water onto the field. As moisture rises, the error decreases, and the valve gradually closes, achieving precise irrigation without overwatering.

---

#### (b) Design a PWM-based motor speed controller circuit for a 24 V, 5 A DC pump used in fertigation. Specify the operating frequency, duty cycle range, and the protection components required. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Design Specifications:**
- Load: 24 V DC diaphragm pump, 5 A rated current
- Controller: ESP32 MCU (3.3 V logic, 16-channel PWM)
- Switching device: N-channel power MOSFET — **IRF540N**
- Switching frequency: **$f_{sw} = 8\,\text{kHz}$** (above audible range, low switching loss)

**Circuit Components:**

| Component | Value/Part Number | Function |
|---|---|---|
| MOSFET | IRF540N ($V_{DS} = 100\,\text{V}$, $I_D = 33\,\text{A}$, $R_{DS(on)} = 44\,\text{m}\Omega$) | Main switching element |
| Gate Resistor ($R_G$) | 100 Ω | Limits ringing, controls dV/dt |
| Gate Pull-down Resistor | 10 kΩ | Ensures MOSFET OFF when MCU is in reset |
| Flyback/Freewheeling Diode | MUR460 (4 A ultrafast) | Protects MOSFET from inductive kickback |
| Gate Driver (optional) | TC4420 | If MCU pin cannot source sufficient gate current |
| Snubber Capacitor | 0.1 µF across MOSFET | Suppresses high-frequency ringing |
| Fuse | 6 A fast-blow | Short-circuit protection |

**Design Calculations:**

1. **Conduction Loss:**

$$P_{cond} = I^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} \cdot D = (5)^2 \cdot 0.044 \cdot 0.7 = 0.77\,\text{W}$$

2. **Switching Loss (approximate):**

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DS} \cdot I_D \cdot (t_{ON} + t_{OFF}) \cdot f_{sw}$$

Assuming $t_{ON} = t_{OFF} = 50\,\text{ns}$:

$$P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot 24 \cdot 5 \cdot (100 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 8000 = 0.48\,\text{W}$$

3. **Total Power Dissipation in MOSFET:**

$$P_{total} = 0.77 + 0.48 \approx 1.25\,\text{W}$$

A small TO-220 heat sink suffices.

4. **Duty Cycle Range:** **0% to 100%**, with linear mapping: 0% → pump OFF, 50% → half speed (≈12 V average), 100% → full speed (24 V).

5. **Protection Components:**
- **Flyback diode** — handles motor's back-EMF at turn-off.
- **TVS diode (P6KE30A)** — clamps transient voltage spikes from the inductive pump winding.
- **Fuse** — disconnects supply in case of MOSFET short.
- **Optocoupler (PC817)** — provides galvanic isolation between MCU and power stage (recommended in wet field environments).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative — Actuator Drivers and H-Bridge Design

`[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank, PEAGT861]`

#### (a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an H-bridge motor driver circuit. List its four operating modes. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram of H-Bridge:**

```
         V_supply (24V)
            |
        +---+---+
        |       |
       S1       S3
        |       |
        +---M---+   (DC Motor / Pump)
        |       |
       S2       S4
        |       |
        +---+---+
            |
           GND
```

**Components:**
- S1, S3: High-side switches (P-channel MOSFET or PNP, or N-channel with bootstrap)
- S2, S4: Low-side switches (N-channel MOSFET or NPN)
- Logic inputs IN1, IN2, IN3, IN4 are driven by the MCU through gate drivers.

**Four Operating Modes:**

| Mode | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Motor Terminal Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward** | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | +V_supply (left to right) |
| **Reverse** | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | −V_supply |
| **Brake (low-side)** | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | 0 V, current recirculates through S1+S2 |
| **Coast (free run)** | OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Motor coasts to a stop |

**Working Principle:** By closing diagonal pairs (S1+S4 or S2+S3), the motor sees a voltage of one polarity or the other, producing forward or reverse motion. To control *speed*, each diagonal pair is driven by a PWM signal. To *brake*, both low-side or both high-side switches are closed, dissipating the motor's kinetic energy.

**Agricultural Application:** The H-bridge is used in **autonomous tractor wheel motors**, **robotic arm joints** for fruit picking, and **reversible DC pumps** for nutrient dosing.

---

#### (b) Compare MOSFET, BJT, and IGBT as switching devices for agricultural actuator drivers. Justify which is most suitable for a 48 V, 30 A BLDC motor used in an autonomous electric tractor. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | BJT | Power MOSFET | IGBT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Device Type | Current-controlled | Voltage-controlled | Voltage-controlled |
| Drive Power | High (base current needed) | Very low (gate charge only) | Low (gate charge) |
| Switching Speed | Slow (µs) | Very fast (ns) | Medium (µs) |
| Conduction Loss | High ($V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5\,\text{V}$) | Low ($R_{DS(on)} \approx$ few mΩ) | Medium (similar to BJT) |
| Voltage Rating | < 1000 V | < 1000 V (commonly) | > 1000 V |
| Current Rating | < 500 A | < 200 A (single die) | > 1000 A |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Ruggedness | Excellent | Sensitive to SOA | Excellent |

**Justification for the 48 V, 30 A BLDC Tractor Motor:**

For a **48 V, 30 A BLDC motor** (P ≈ 1.44 kW per motor), the most suitable choice is a **3-phase MOSFET-based inverter (e.g., using IRFS7537 or similar 75 V, 100 A N-channel MOSFETs).**

**Reasons:**

1. **Voltage rating sufficient:** 48 V supply, MOSFET with $V_{DS} = 75\,\text{V}$ provides **> 50% derating margin** for safe operation with inductive transients.
2. **High switching frequency needed:** BLDC motors are driven by trapezoidal or FOC (Field-Oriented Control) at switching frequencies of **8 kHz – 25 kHz** for smooth torque. MOSFETs excel at this; BJTs and IGBTs would incur excessive switching losses.
3. **Low conduction loss at 30 A:** A MOSFET with $R_{DS(on)} = 5\,\text{m}\Omega$ dissipates:
$$P = I^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} = (30)^2 \cdot 0.005 = 4.5\,\text{W}$$ per switch, easily manageable with a TO-247 or D²PAK package.
4. **Efficiency:** Total inverter efficiency > 96% is achievable — critical for battery-powered tractors where range determines field operating hours.
5. **Direct gate drive from MCU/driver ICs:** MOSFETs can be driven directly by gate driver ICs (e.g., DRV8301, IRS2003) interfaced with STM32 MCUs.

**IGBTs are NOT preferred** because at 48 V, the IGBT's forward voltage drop (≈ 1.5 V) is a significant fraction of the supply, causing high loss. **BJTs are too slow** for modern FOC algorithms and require complex base-drive circuitry.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the flyback diode across inductive loads** (solenoid, relay coil, motor). Without it, the inductive kickback at turn-off can spike to hundreds of volts and destroy the switching transistor. Examiners *expect* you to mention this — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing duty cycle $D$ with switching frequency $f_{sw}$.** $D$ controls average power; $f_{sw}$ controls smoothness/audibility. Many students interchange them in numerical problems.
> 3. **Ignoring dead-time in H-bridges.** Stating "S1 and S2 are complementary" without mentioning the **200 ns dead time** to prevent shoot-through is considered an incomplete answer.
> 4. **Writing PID as "PI, PD, ID" or skipping the integral term's physical meaning.** Always explain: $K_p$ → fast response, $K_i$ → eliminates steady-state error, $K_d$ → damps oscillation.
> 5. **Voltage level mismatch** — connecting a **5 V MCU pin directly to a 12 V actuator** without a driver or level shifter. Examiners look for awareness of this.
> 6. **Not specifying units** in numerical answers (e.g., writing "$V_{avg} = 12 \times 0.5$" without stating it equals **6 V**). Always annotate units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Control Electronics" (Module 4):**

- ✅ **Control Electronics = Sensor + Signal Conditioning + MCU + Driver + Actuator + Feedback** — remember all six blocks.
- ✅ **PWM average voltage:** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ — *the* most-asked formula in this module.
- ✅ **Switching frequency** for DC motor: **1–8 kHz**; for servo: **50 Hz**; for LED: **> 200 Hz**.
- ✅ **H-Bridge modes:** Forward, Reverse, Brake, Coast. Always mention **dead time** to prevent shoot-through.
- ✅ **MOSFET vs BJT vs IGBT:** MOSFET for low-voltage high-frequency; IGBT for high-voltage high-current; BJT rarely used in modern designs.
- ✅ **Flyback diode is MANDATORY** across every inductive load (relay coil, solenoid, motor terminal).
- ✅ **PID:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ — know the role of each term.
- ✅ **ADC resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^n$ — smaller LSB = finer measurement.
- ✅ **RC low-pass cut-off:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ — used to filter sensor noise.
- ✅ **Voltage rails in agriculture:** **24 V (tractor battery), 12 V (auxiliary), 5 V / 3.3 V (logic)**.
- ✅ **Optocouplers** provide **galvanic isolation** between high-voltage actuator side and low-voltage MCU side — essential for safety in wet, dusty farm environments.
- ✅ **Opto-isolated SSR (Solid State Relay)** is preferred over EMR (Electromechanical Relay) for high-cycle applications like greenhouse fan switching (> 10,000 operations/year).
- ✅ **Dead band** in actuator control prevents valve/solenoid chattering near the setpoint.
- ✅ **Logic-level MOSFETs** ($V_{GS(th)} \approx 1–2\,\text{V}$) can be driven directly by 3.3 V MCUs; standard MOSFETs need 10 V gate drive.
- ✅ **Power dissipation in MOSFET** ≈ $I^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} \cdot D$ — important for heat-sink design.
- ✅ **BLDC motors in drones/tractors** are driven by **3-phase MOSFET inverters** with FOC (Field-Oriented Control) at 8–25 kHz PWM.

> **End of Notes — Control Electronics (PEAGT861, Module 4)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms and Programming for Microcontroller and Microprocessor-Based Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT861 - SENSORS, AI AND ROBOTICS IN PRECISION AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Algorithms and Programming for Microcontroller and Microprocessor-Based Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Algorithms and Programming for Microcontroller and Microprocessor-Based Robots

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithmic Framework for Robotic Embedded Systems**: A structured sequence of deterministic, finite-step computational procedures — typically expressed as state machines, interrupt service routines (ISRs), or control loops — designed to orchestrate sensor data acquisition, actuator command generation, and inter-module communication on resource-constrained embedded hardware (microcontrollers) or moderately capable single-board computers (microprocessors).

In the context of **precision agriculture robotics**, this includes:
- **Perception algorithms** (sensor data parsing, filtering, fusion)
- **Decision algorithms** (rule-based AI, threshold logic, ML inference)
- **Actuation algorithms** (motor control, PWM generation, PID loops)
- **Communication protocols** (I2C, SPI, UART, CAN, MQTT)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Farm Robot Brain" Analogy**
> Imagine a **smart tractor** as a living organism. The **microcontroller (MCU)** is like the **spinal cord and reflexes** — it handles fast, repetitive, low-level decisions (turning a wheel 5° left when an obstacle is 10 cm away). The **microprocessor (MPU)** is like the **brain and eyes** — it processes high-level information (satellite maps, weather APIs, crop health analysis) but reacts slightly slower. Both work together: the brain *plans*, the spine *reacts*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Microcontroller (MCU)** | Single-chip computer with CPU, RAM, ROM, I/O (e.g., Arduino Uno: ATmega328P, 16 MHz, 2 KB RAM) |
| **Microprocessor (MPU)** | CPU-only chip requiring external RAM/ROM (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4: Cortex-A72, 1.8 GHz, 4 GB RAM) |
| **RTOS** | Real-Time Operating System — guarantees deterministic response within deadlines |
| **ISR** | Interrupt Service Routine — code executed in response to a hardware/software event |
| **PWM** | Pulse Width Modulation — technique to simulate analog output using digital pulses |
| **PID Controller** | Proportional-Integral-Derivative control loop for precise actuation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU**: An **MCU runs bare-metal C/C++** with millisecond precision (loop time ~1–10 ms). An **MPU runs Linux/Python** with millisecond-to-second precision. Precision agriculture robots often use **hybrid architectures** — MPU for vision/AI, MCU for motor/sensor real-time control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Comparison: MCU vs MPU

```
┌─────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐
│       MICROCONTROLLER (MCU)    │      MICROPROCESSOR (MPU)      │
├─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Single-chip integration         │ CPU + external peripherals     │
│ Clock: 1–500 MHz                │ Clock: 0.5–3.0 GHz             │
│ RAM: 2 KB – 1 MB               │ RAM: 512 MB – 8 GB             │
│ Power: < 0.5 W                 │ Power: 1–15 W                  │
│ OS: Bare-metal / RTOS           │ OS: Linux / Windows / Android  │
│ Boot time: < 100 ms            │ Boot time: 5–30 s              │
│ Cost: $1 – $10                 │ Cost: $10 – $150               │
│ Examples: Arduino, ESP32, STM32 │ Examples: Raspberry Pi, Jetson  │
└─────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.2 Algorithm Classification for Agricultural Robots

#### A. Perception Algorithms (Layer 1)
- **Sensor Reading**: Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC), digital pin polling
- **Filtering**: Moving average, median filter, Kalman filter
- **Sensor Fusion**: Complementary filter, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)

#### B. Decision Algorithms (Layer 2)
- **Threshold logic**: `if soil_moisture < 30% then irrigate`
- **State machines**: IDLE → SCOUTING → SPRAYING → RETURNING
- **Path planning**: A*, Dijkstra, RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)

#### C. Actuation Algorithms (Layer 3)
- **PID control** for wheel speed, steering angle, drone altitude
- **PWM generation** for motor drivers, valve control
- **Trajectory tracking** for manipulators (spray arms)

### 2.3 The PID Controller — The Heart of Robotic Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PID?** A precision agriculture robot needs to follow a straight row at exactly 0.5 m/s despite wheel slip, slope, and wind. PID continuously corrects the error between *desired* and *actual* state.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $u(t)$ | $K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID output | varies |
| $e(t)$ | $r(t) - y(t)$ | Error = Reference − Measurement | varies |
| $\Delta t$ | $t_n - t_{n-1}$ | Sampling interval | seconds |
| $K_p, K_i, K_d$ | Tuning gains | Proportional, Integral, Derivative | dimensionless |
| $f_{PWM}$ | $\frac{1}{T_{PWM}}$ | PWM frequency (Arduino default: 490 Hz / 980 Hz) | Hz |
| $D$ | $\frac{t_{on}}{T_{PWM}} \times 100$ | Duty cycle (0–100%) | percent |
| $T_{sample}$ | $\frac{1}{f_{sample}}$ | Sensor sampling period | seconds |
| $V_{ADC}$ | $\frac{ADC_{value}}{2^{n} - 1} \times V_{ref}$ | ADC voltage conversion | volts |
| $\hat{x}_{k\vert k}$ | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$ | Kalman filter update | state vector |
| $f_{s,max}$ | $\frac{f_{clk}}{N_{prescaler} \times (TOP + 1)}$ | Timer sampling frequency (ATMega) | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often forget that PID must be tuned using **Ziegler-Nichols** or **manual tuning** to avoid oscillation. An un-tuned PID can cause a spray robot to oscillate and waste chemicals.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Autonomous Tractors** | PID for steering angle, GPS waypoint tracking |
| **Agri-Drones** | PID for altitude hold, waypoint navigation |
| **Greenhouse Robots** | State machines for seeding → watering → harvesting cycles |
| **Livestock Monitoring** | Sensor fusion (IMU + GPS) for collar-based health tracking |
| **Soil Probes** | RTOS-scheduled multi-sensor reading every 60 seconds |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Discrete PID Equation

The continuous-time PID equation is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

To implement on a digital microcontroller, we must **discretize** this equation because the MCU samples at discrete time steps $t_n = n \cdot \Delta t$.

**Step 1 — Discretize the integral term** using the rectangular (forward Euler) approximation:

$$\int_0^{t_n} e(\tau) d\tau \approx \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(t_k) \cdot \Delta t = \Delta t \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k$$

**Step 2 — Discretize the derivative term** using the backward difference:

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \approx \frac{e(t_n) - e(t_{n-1})}{\Delta t} = \frac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the continuous PID equation**:

$$u_n = K_p e_n + K_i \Delta t \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + K_d \frac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by defining effective gains** (often used in industrial controllers):

$$u_n = K_p \left[ e_n + \frac{\Delta t}{T_i} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + \frac{T_d}{\Delta t} (e_n - e_{n-1}) \right]$$

where $T_i = K_p / K_i$ is the *integral time constant* and $T_d = K_d / K_p$ is the *derivative time constant*.

**Step 5 — Incremental (velocity) form** (avoids integral windup issues):

$$\Delta u_n = u_n - u_{n-1} = K_p \left[ (e_n - e_{n-1}) + \frac{\Delta t}{T_i} e_n + \frac{T_d}{\Delta t} (e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2}) \right]$$

This is the form implemented inside an MCU's main loop or RTOS task.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — PID for Row-Following Weeding Robot

**Problem**: A weeding robot must maintain a lateral position $y = 0$ cm relative to a crop row. Vision sensor provides error $e$ in cm. Compute the steering correction after 3 iterations using $K_p = 2.5$, $K_i = 0.8$, $K_d = 0.3$, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s.

**Iteration data**:

| Step $n$ | Error $e_n$ (cm) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0 | +4.0 |
| 1 | +2.5 |
| 2 | +1.0 |

**Step 1 — Compute proportional term at $n=2$**:

$$P_2 = K_p \cdot e_2 = 2.5 \times 1.0 = 2.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute integral term at $n=2$**:

$$I_2 = K_i \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{2} e_k = 0.8 \times 0.1 \times (4.0 + 2.5 + 1.0) = 0.08 \times 7.5 = 0.6$$

**Step 3 — Compute derivative term at $n=2$**:

$$D_2 = K_d \cdot \frac{e_2 - e_1}{\Delta t} = 0.3 \times \frac{1.0 - 2.5}{0.1} = 0.3 \times (-15) = -4.5$$

**Step 4 — Total PID output**:

$$u_2 = P_2 + I_2 + D_2 = 2.5 + 0.6 + (-4.5) = -1.4$$

The negative sign indicates a **right-steer correction** to bring the robot back to the row center.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Algorithm Selection

| Application | MCU/MPU | Algorithm | Sampling Rate | Communication |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Soil moisture mapping | MCU | Threshold + ADC | 1 Hz | UART |
| Row-following weeding | MCU | PID + Vision | 20 Hz | I2C |
| Drone spraying | MPU + MCU | State Machine + PID | 50 Hz | UART + PWM |
| Yield prediction | MPU | Random Forest / CNN | 1/season | Wi-Fi / MQTT |
| Greenhouse climate | MCU | RTOS multi-task | 0.1 Hz | RS-485 |
| Auto-steering tractor | MPU + MCU | Kalman + PID | 100 Hz | CAN bus |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hybrid Architecture: MCU + MPU in an Agricultural Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSOR_LAYER["PERCEPTION LAYER - Sensors"]
        S1["soilMoisture\nAnalog Sensor"]
        S2["gpsModule\nUART GPS"]
        S3["cameraRGB\nCSI Camera"]
        S4["imu9Axis\nI2C IMU"]
        S5["lidar2D\nUART LiDAR"]
    end

    subgraph MCU_UNIT["REAL-TIME LAYER - Microcontroller ESP32"]
        A1["ADC Read\nSoil Moisture"]
        A2["PID Loop\nMotor Speed"]
        A3["PWM Output\nto Motor Driver"]
        A4["ISR Handler\nEncoder Feedback"]
        A5["I2C Master\nIMU Polling"]
    end

    subgraph MPU_UNIT["HIGH-LEVEL LAYER - Microprocessor Raspberry Pi"]
        B1["OpenCV Pipeline\nRow Detection"]
        B2["YOLOv8 Inference\nWeed Detection"]
        B3["Mission Planner\nState Machine"]
        B4["MQTT Publisher\nCloud Telemetry"]
        B5["Path Planner\nA-star Algorithm"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATOR_LAYER["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        M1["DC Motor\nLeft Wheel"]
        M2["DC Motor\nRight Wheel"]
        M3["Spray Pump\n12V Solenoid"]
        M4["Steering Servo\nPWM Control"]
    end

    S1 --> A1
    S4 --> A5
    S3 --> B1
    S2 --> B3
    S5 --> B3

    A1 -->|soil data via UART| B3
    A5 -->|IMU data via UART| B3
    B1 -->|row offset error| B3
    B2 -->|weed map coordinates| B3
    B3 -->|motor commands| A2
    B3 -->|spray trigger| M3
    B5 -->|waypoints| B3
    B3 -->|telemetry| B4

    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> M1
    A3 --> M2
    B3 --> M4
    A4 -->|encoder pulses| A2
```

### 4.2 RTOS Task Scheduling Flow for a Multi-Sensor Agri-Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RTOS_SCHED["RTOS Scheduler on ESP32 FreeRTOS"]
        T1["Task 1\nSoil Read\nPriority 1\nPeriod 1000ms"]
        T2["Task 2\nIMU Read\nPriority 2\nPeriod 20ms"]
        T3["Task 3\nMotor PID\nPriority 3\nPeriod 10ms"]
        T4["Task 4\nMQTX Send\nPriority 1\nPeriod 5000ms"]
        T5["Task 5\nSafety Watchdog\nPriority 4\nEvent-driven"]
    end

    T1 --> Q1["Shared\nQueue"]
    T2 --> Q1
    T3 --> Q2["Motor\nCommand Queue"]
    T1 --> Q2
    T5 -.->|kill signal| T3
    Q1 --> T4
```

### 4.3 PID Control Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Reference\nr t"] --> SUM[("+")]
    Y["Output\ny t"] --> FB[Feedback]
    FB --> SUM
    SUM -->|"error e t"| PID["PID Controller\nKp Ki Kd"]
    PID -->|"u t"| PLANT["Plant\nDC Motor"]
    PLANT --> Y
    DIST["Disturbance\nslope, wind, slip"] -.-> PLANT
```

### 4.4 Programmatic Flow — Autonomous Weeding State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> CALIBRATE : Power On
    CALIBRATE --> SCOUT : Sensors OK
    CALIBRATE --> ERROR : Sensor Fault
    ERROR --> [*] : E-Stop
    SCOUT --> DETECT_WEED : Vision Frame Ready
    SCOUT --> SCAN_ROW : No Weed Found
    DETECT_WEED --> SPRAY : Weed > Threshold
    SPRAY --> RETURN : Tank Empty Check
    SPRAY --> SCOUT : Spray Complete
    SCAN_ROW --> ALIGN_PID : Lateral Offset > 5cm
    ALIGN_PID --> SCOUT : Offset Corrected
    RETURN --> DOCK : Battery Low
    DOCK --> [*] : Charging
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

> Differentiate between a microcontroller and a microprocessor in the context of robotic systems used in precision agriculture.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Microcontroller (MCU) | Microprocessor (MPU) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Integration** | CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O on **one chip** | CPU only; needs external RAM/ROM |
| **Speed** | Lower (16–500 MHz) | Higher (1–3 GHz) |
| **Power** | < 0.5 W | 1–15 W |
| **Use case** | Motor control, sensor reading | Vision, AI, path planning |
| **Example** | ESP32, Arduino, STM32 | Raspberry Pi 4, Jetson Nano |

*(1 mark each for any 3 distinguishing points — full marks awarded for table-style answer)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**

> What is a PID controller? List the role of each of its three terms in agricultural robot steering.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **PID controller** is a feedback control algorithm that minimizes the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ between a desired reference $r(t)$ and actual output $y(t)$.

1. **P (Proportional) term** — $K_p e(t)$: Provides an immediate correction proportional to the current error. In steering: turns the wheel more if the row offset is large.
2. **I (Integral) term** — $K_i \int e \, dt$: Eliminates steady-state error (persistent drift). In steering: removes the small constant bias that makes the robot gradually drift off-row.
3. **D (Derivative) term** — $K_d \frac{de}{dt}$: Damps oscillations by reacting to the rate of change. In steering: prevents overshoot when the robot is rapidly correcting back to the row center.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note**: KTU ESE allows **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Both are answered below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, 14-Mark Compulsory Choice]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a microcontroller-based precision agriculture robot. Discuss the role of the RTOS scheduler in handling multiple sensor inputs. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the discrete-time PID equation from its continuous form and explain how it is implemented in an agricultural row-following robot. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q(a):**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            AGRICULTURAL ROBOT (MCU-based)            │
│  ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────────┐      │
│  │ Sensors │--->│   MCU   │--->│  Actuators   │      │
│  │ (ADC,   │    │ (ESP32) │    │ (Motors,     │      │
│  │ I2C,    │    │  +RTOS  │    │  Pumps,      │      │
│  │ UART)   │    │         │    │  Servos)     │      │
│  └─────────┘    └────┬────┘    └──────────────┘      │
│                      │                               │
│                      ▼                               │
│              ┌──────────────┐                        │
│              │  Wireless    │ (Wi-Fi/BLE to MPU)     │
│              │  Comm Module │                        │
│              └──────────────┘                        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**

1. The **MCU (ESP32)** acts as the central controller, polling sensors via ADC (soil moisture, temperature), I2C (IMU), and UART (GPS/LiDAR).
2. An **RTOS (FreeRTOS)** runs multiple concurrent tasks: a high-priority motor PID task (10 ms loop), a medium-priority sensor reading task (100 ms loop), and a low-priority telemetry task (5 s loop).
3. The **actuator layer** includes motor drivers (PWM-controlled), spray pumps (GPIO-triggered solenoids), and steering servos.
4. The **wireless module** forwards processed data to the gateway (Raspberry Pi or cloud) for higher-level AI inference.

---

**Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Step 1 — Start from the continuous PID equation (2 marks):**

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Discretize for digital implementation (3 marks):**

Replace continuous time $t$ with discrete sample index $n$, sample interval $\Delta t$.

$$u_n = K_p e_n + K_i \Delta t \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + K_d \frac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 3 — Implementation in a row-following robot (2 marks):**

1. **Sensors** (camera + IMU) measure lateral offset error $e_n$ every $\Delta t = 50$ ms.
2. The MCU computes the PID output $u_n$ which is the **steering angle correction** in degrees.
3. $u_n$ is sent as a PWM duty cycle to the **steering servo motor**.
4. The loop repeats continuously while the robot traverses the row.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Module 4]**

> **(a)** Explain the difference between **bare-metal programming** and **RTOS-based programming** for microcontrollers. When would you choose each in a precision agriculture robot? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a flowchart, explain the **state machine** algorithm for an autonomous spraying drone covering a field of 2 hectares. List the sensors required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q(a):**

**Bare-metal programming** means writing a single `while(1)` super-loop directly on the MCU without any operating system. Code is simple and deterministic but only handles one task effectively. (2 marks)

**RTOS-based programming** uses a real-time operating system like **FreeRTOS** to schedule multiple concurrent tasks, each with its own priority, stack, and period. (2 marks)

**Comparison and selection (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Bare-metal | RTOS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of tasks | 1–2 | Many (>3) |
| Complexity | Low | Medium–High |
| Determinism | Excellent | Excellent (with priority) |
| Use in agri-robot | Simple moisture probe | Multi-sensor weeding robot |
| Resource use | Minimal (~2 KB) | Higher (~20 KB RAM minimum) |

**Conclusion**: For a single-function sensor node (e.g., soil moisture logger), bare-metal suffices. For an autonomous weeding robot with motor control, vision handoff, and telemetry, RTOS is essential.

---

**Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Flowchart (4 marks) and state machine (3 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> POWER_ON
    POWER_ON --> SELF_TEST : Battery > 80%
    SELF_TEST --> PRE_FLIGHT : All Sensors OK
    SELF_TEST --> ABORT : Sensor Failure
    PRE_FLIGHT --> TAKEOFF : GPS Lock + Wind < 5m/s
    TAKEOFF --> SCAN_FIELD : Altitude 50m
    SCAN_FIELD --> WAYPOINT_NAV : Field Boundary Loaded
    WAYPOINT_NAV --> SPRAY_SECTOR : Enter Spray Zone
    SPRAY_SECTOR --> SPRAY_SECTOR : Pesticide Level OK
    SPRAY_SECTOR --> RTB : Tank Empty OR Sector Done
    RTB --> LANDING : Home Reached
    LANDING --> [*] : Power Off
```

**Sensors required (1 mark each, list 4):**

1. **GPS module** (waypoint navigation)
2. **Barometer** (altitude hold)
3. **IMU (gyroscope + accelerometer)** (orientation stability)
4. **Lidar / ultrasonic** (terrain following and obstacle avoidance)
5. **Tank level sensor** (pesticide quantity)
6. **Wind speed sensor** (flight safety)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - Students lose marks by **confusing MCU with MPU** — memorize their clock speed, RAM, and example chips.
> - In PID questions, **always show the discretization step** (continuous → discrete) — full 7 marks require the derivation, not just the formula.
> - For state machine questions, **draw the diagram first, then explain transitions** — the visual carries 4 of 7 marks.
> - Do not write generic "PID is a controller" answers — KTU expects **agriculture-specific context** (row-following, spraying, steering).
> - **RTOS priority inversion** is a high-yield concept — mention it if asked about task scheduling.
> - Always include **units** in your final numerical answers (degrees, cm, Hz, ms).

---

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] MCU vs MPU — integration, clock, RAM, power, examples
- [ ] Continuous PID → discrete PID derivation (3 steps)
- [ ] Three PID terms — purpose of P, I, D in agricultural robots
- [ ] Bare-metal vs RTOS programming — when to use which
- [ ] State machine notation — states, transitions, guards, actions
- [ ] Sensor-to-actuator pipeline in a hybrid robot
- [ ] PWM duty cycle formula $D = t_{on}/T_{PWM}$
- [ ] ADC voltage conversion $V_{ADC} = \frac{ADC}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref}$
- [ ] Sampling theorem consideration — sample at least **2× the bandwidth**
- [ ] RTOS scheduling algorithms — fixed priority, rate-monotonic, EDF
- [ ] Communication protocols — I2C (slow, 2-wire), SPI (fast, 4-wire), UART (point-to-point), CAN (robust, vehicles)
- [ ] Kalman filter — predict + update cycle for sensor fusion
- [ ] Path planning — A*, Dijkstra, RRT comparison
- [ ] Safety mechanisms — watchdog timer, E-stop, brown-out detection
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Remote Control and Autonomous Operation of Agricultural Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT861 - SENSORS, AI AND ROBOTICS IN PRECISION AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Remote Control and Autonomous Operation of Agricultural Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Remote Control Operation

**Remote Control (Teleoperation)** in agricultural robotics refers to the real-time, human-in-the-loop operation of agricultural machinery from a physically separated location, achieved through bidirectional wireless communication channels that transmit command signals (operator inputs) to the robot and feedback signals (telemetry, video, sensor data) back to the operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> Remote control systems use **Radio Frequency (RF)** transceivers in the **UHF (300 MHz – 3 GHz)** or **2.4 GHz ISM** bands, paired with feedback channels such as **FPV (First-Person View) video**, **telemetry overlays**, and **haptic control sticks**, to manipulate unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs) and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) deployed in open-field agriculture.

### 1.2 Formal Definition — Autonomous Operation

**Autonomous Agricultural Operation** is the capability of a field robot to perceive its environment, localize itself within a defined working envelope, plan an optimal task sequence, execute that plan through low-level motion controllers, and recover from faults — **all without continuous human intervention**, governed by an embedded decision-making architecture rooted in **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** and **Sensor Fusion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Classification — Six Levels of Vehicle Autonomy (Adapted from SAE J3016 for Agriculture):**
> 
> | Level | Designation | Human Role |
> |---|---|---|
> | **0** | No Automation | Operator controls all functions |
> | **1** | Driver Assistance | Adaptive cruise / line-keeping only |
> | **2** | Partial Automation | Auto-steer + speed control, human supervises |
> | **3** | Conditional Automation | Robot makes decisions in known fields, human fallback |
> | **4** | High Automation | Robot operates full mission in geofenced area |
> | **5** | Full Automation | No human needed, any environment |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — Remote Control = "Playing a Video Game with Real Stakes":**
> Think of piloting a tractor via remote control exactly like playing a farming simulation game (e.g., *Farming Simulator*), except the controller commands are sent through **radio waves** to a real, physical tractor kilometres away, and the camera feed on your screen is coming from a **live camera mounted on that tractor**. The moment your internet drops or the signal fades, the tractor *stops* — because the brain is still in *your* hands.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Autonomous Robot = "A Self-Driving Car in a Cornfield":**
> Imagine a self-driving Tesla, but instead of avoiding other cars on a highway, it has to drive through rows of maize, identify weeds between plants, and decide on its own which nozzle to spray. It uses **GPS to know where it is**, **cameras to "see" the crop rows**, and a **small onboard computer (its "brain")** to decide every micro-movement — the human operator is sitting at home drinking tea.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Mapping for KTU Examinations

| Term | Standard Definition | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | End-to-end delay between command and actuation | **< 100 ms** for safe teleop |
| **Bandwidth** | Maximum data rate of comm. channel | **≥ 2 Mbps** for HD video |
| **Geofence** | Virtual perimeter (GPS-defined) bounding robot | WGS-84 coordinates |
| **Waypoint** | Pre-defined GPS target point robot must visit | Lat/Long + Altitude |
| **SLAM** | Simultaneous Localization and Mapping | Position error **< 0.1 m** |
| **RTK-GPS** | Real-Time Kinematic GPS (cm-level accuracy) | Horizontal error **± 2 cm** |
| **ROS** | Robot Operating System (middleware) | Topic-based pub/sub |
| **Obstacle Avoidance** | Reactive maneuver to prevent collision | Min. clearance **≥ 0.5 m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The term **"Autonomy Spectrum"** is the most-tested concept in this module. Always classify a robot's capability using the 6-level table above. Marks are awarded for correctly identifying the *human-robot task allocation* boundary.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values

- **Speed of Light (Radio Propagation):** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$ — used to compute RF link delay
- **Earth's Radius (Geodesy):** $R_{\oplus} = 6{,}371 \ \text{km}$ — used in great-circle distance calculations
- **Standard GNSS Carrier Frequency (L1 band):** $f_{L1} = 1.57542 \ \text{GHz}$
- **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) reference distance:** $d_0 = 1 \ \text{m}$
- **WGS-84 Semi-major Axis:** $a = 6{,}378{,}137 \ \text{m}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **RTK-GPS (cm-level)** with **standard GPS (m-level)**. A tractor using only L1 GPS has a horizontal position uncertainty of **± 1.5 m to ± 3 m**, which is **inadequate for row-crop navigation**. RTK correction is mandatory for true autonomous row-following.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of Remote-Controlled Agricultural Robots

A teleoperated agricultural robot follows a **three-tier hierarchical architecture**:

### Tier 1 — Operator Console (Ground Station)
- **Hardware:** Laptop / tablet / handheld RC transmitter
- **Functions:** HMI (Human-Machine Interface), video display, joystick acquisition
- **Software Stack:** QGroundControl, Mission Planner, ROS RViz

### Tier 2 — Wireless Communication Link
- **Physical Layer:** RF modem (e.g., SiK 915 MHz, XBee 2.4 GHz, 4G LTE, 5G)
- **Data Link Layer:** MAVLink protocol (Micro Air Vehicle Link) — 8-byte overhead, **CRC-16** checksum
- **Network Layer:** UDP/IP for low latency (TCP avoided due to retransmission delay)

### Tier 3 — Robot Onboard Stack
- **Autopilot:** Pixhawk, Arduino Mega, NVIDIA Jetson
- **Actuators:** BLDC motors, hydraulic valves, PWM servo drivers (50 Hz, 1–2 ms pulse)
- **Sensors:** GPS, IMU, LiDAR, RGB-D camera

> [!NOTE]
> **MAVLink Protocol** is the *de facto* standard for UAV/UGV communication in precision agriculture. A MAVLink v2 packet is **11–279 bytes** long with a **24-byte header** and **2-byte signature**.

## 2.2 Architecture of Autonomous Agricultural Robots

The autonomous robot is governed by the classic **Sense → Plan → Act** paradigm (also called the **Deliberative Architecture**), with a modern hybrid addition of **Reactive Layer**:

### Layered Autonomy Stack (Top-Down)

1. **Mission Planner (High-Level):**
   - Inputs: Geo-referenced field map, task definition (e.g., "spray zone A, harvest zone B")
   - Outputs: Sequence of sub-goals / waypoints
   - Algorithm: Finite State Machine (FSM) or Behavior Tree

2. **Path Planner (Mid-Level):**
   - Inputs: Waypoints, obstacle map, robot footprint
   - Outputs: Collision-free trajectory (sequence of poses)
   - Algorithms: **A\***, **D\***, **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)**, **Dijkstra**

3. **Motion Controller (Low-Level):**
   - Inputs: Reference trajectory, current pose (from SLAM)
   - Outputs: Motor commands (velocity, steering angle)
   - Algorithms: **PID**, **Pure Pursuit**, **Stanley Controller**, **Model Predictive Control (MPC)**

4. **Reactive Obstacle Avoidance (Safety Layer):**
   - Inputs: Real-time LiDAR/camera scan
   - Outputs: Emergency stop / avoidance vector
   - Algorithms: **VFH (Vector Field Histogram)**, **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)**

> [!TIP]
> **Why a Hybrid Architecture?** A purely deliberative robot is *too slow* to avoid a sudden obstacle (a deer crossing a field). A purely reactive robot *cannot plan* a multi-row spraying mission. Hybrid architectures combine the *strategic foresight* of deliberative planning with the *tactical reflex* of reactive control — this is the **standard KTU answer** for "Why hybrid architecture is preferred."

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute value bars use `\vert` instead of `\|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | LaTeX Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | $d$ = distance (m), $f$ = freq. (Hz) | RF link budget for teleop |
| 2 | **Received Signal Strength (Log-Distance)** | $P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | $n$ = path loss exponent (2–4) | Predicting comm. range |
| 3 | **Time-To-Collision (TTC)** | $TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\vert v_{rel} \vert}$ | $\Delta d$ = gap (m), $v_{rel}$ = relative speed (m/s) | Emergency braking |
| 4 | **Pure Pursuit Curvature** | $\kappa = \dfrac{2 \sin(\alpha)}{L_d}$ | $\alpha$ = heading error, $L_d$ = look-ahead (m) | Row-following control |
| 5 | **Stanley Steering Law** | $\delta(t) = \psi(t) + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{k \cdot e_{fa}}{v(t)}\right)$ | $e_{fa}$ = cross-track error, $k$ = gain | Autonomous steering |
| 6 | **PID Control Output** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \!\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = gains | Motor speed control |
| 7 | **Haversine Distance (GPS)** | $d = 2 R_{\oplus} \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$ | $\phi$ = latitude, $\lambda$ = longitude | Field-to-field distance |
| 8 | **A* Heuristic (Admissible)** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $g$ = cost-so-far, $h$ = est. cost-to-go | Path search optimality |
| 9 | **LiDAR Field-of-View Coverage** | $A_{cov} = \pi r^2 \cdot \dfrac{\theta_{FOV}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{r^2 \theta_{FOV}}{2}$ | $r$ = range, $\theta_{FOV}$ = scan angle (rad) | Obstacle sweep area |
| 10 | **Mahalanobis Distance (Sensor Fusion)** | $D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ = covariance matrix | Outlier rejection in GPS+IMU |
| 11 | **Throughput (Comm. Link)** | $T = \dfrac{P_{payload}}{T_{frame} + T_{prop}}$ | $P_{payload}$ = packet size (bits) | MAVLink efficiency |
| 12 | **Bearing to Waypoint** | $\theta = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sin\Delta\lambda \cos\phi_2, \cos\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 - \sin\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos\Delta\lambda\right)$ | $\phi, \lambda$ in radians | Navigation heading |

## 2.4 Communication Link Analysis — Engineering Utility

The **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** equation is the workhorse for designing any teleoperation link:

$$FSPL_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

where the constant $32.44 = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi/c)$ is evaluated at $f$ in **MHz** and $d$ in **km**.

**Real-World Utility:** If a farmer wants to teleoperate a weeding robot from **500 m** away using a **2.4 GHz** control link, the path loss is:
- $20 \log_{10}(0.5) = -6.02$ dB
- $20 \log_{10}(2400) = 67.6$ dB
- Plus $32.44$ dB constant
- **Total FSPL = 94.02 dB**

A typical 2.4 GHz transmitter outputs **20 dBm (100 mW)**; with a receiver sensitivity of **−100 dBm**, the link margin is $20 - 94.02 - (-100) = 26$ dB — **sufficient** for a reliable link.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing frequency **quadruples** path loss per doubling of frequency. This is why **UHF (433 MHz, 915 MHz) is preferred over 2.4 GHz** for long-range agricultural teleoperation in open fields where Fresnel zone obstruction is minimal.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Haversine Distance for Field-to-Waypoint Navigation

The **Haversine formula** gives the great-circle distance between two GPS coordinates on a sphere — essential for autonomous tractors computing inter-row travel.

### Given:
- Point A: $(\phi_1, \lambda_1)$ in radians
- Point B: $(\phi_2, \lambda_2)$ in radians
- Earth's mean radius: $R_{\oplus} = 6{,}371{,}000$ m

### To Derive: $d$ — the orthodromic (shortest) distance between A and B.

**Step 1 — Define angular deltas:**

$$\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 \quad \text{(latitude difference in radians)}$$

$$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_2 - \lambda_1 \quad \text{(longitude difference in radians)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Haversine function $h$:**

$$h\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

This converts angular separation to a *chord-squared* ratio, which is numerically stable for small angles (avoiding floating-point cancellation errors of $\cos\phi_1 - \cos\phi_2$).

**Step 3 — Express the central angle $\Delta\sigma$:**

$$\Delta\sigma = 2 \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by Earth's radius to obtain distance:**

$$d = R_{\oplus} \cdot \Delta\sigma$$

**Step 5 — Expand the final formula:**

$$d = 2 R_{\oplus} \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$$

### Worked Numerical Example:

A tractor moves from Point A = (10.0525° N, 76.6190° E) to Point B = (10.0530° N, 76.6195° E) — a typical inter-row step in a Kerala paddy field.

**Step 1:** Convert to radians: $\phi_1 = 0.175352$ rad, $\lambda_1 = 1.336980$ rad, $\phi_2 = 0.175361$ rad, $\lambda_2 = 1.336988$ rad.

**Step 2:** Compute deltas:
$\Delta\phi = 0.000009$ rad, $\Delta\lambda = 0.000008$ rad.

**Step 3:** Evaluate inside square root:
- $\sin^2(0.0000045) \approx 2.025 \times 10^{-11}$
- $\cos(0.175352) \approx 0.98465$, $\cos(0.175361) \approx 0.98465$
- $\sin^2(0.000004) \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-11}$
- Product: $0.98465 \times 0.98465 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-11} \approx 1.551 \times 10^{-11}$

**Step 4:** Sum: $2.025 \times 10^{-11} + 1.551 \times 10^{-11} = 3.576 \times 10^{-11}$

**Step 5:** Square root: $\sqrt{3.576 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 5.980 \times 10^{-6}$

**Step 6:** $d = 2 \times 6{,}371{,}000 \times 5.980 \times 10^{-6} \approx 76.2$ m

**Result:** The tractor must travel approximately **76.2 m** to reach the next waypoint. With a forward velocity of **1.5 m/s**, this corresponds to a traversal time of:

$$t = \dfrac{76.2}{1.5} = 50.8 \ \text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The Haversine formula assumes a *spherical* Earth. For KTU-level precision, the **Vincenty formula** (ellipsoidal Earth, WGS-84) is used; however, the Haversine error is **< 0.5%** for fields of less than 10 km — acceptable for typical Kerala farm plots.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pure Pursuit Look-Ahead Steering for Row-Following

The **Pure Pursuit** algorithm is widely used in autonomous agricultural vehicles to follow crop rows with a fixed look-ahead distance.

### Setup:
- Robot pose: $(x_r, y_r, \theta_r)$ in the field frame
- Goal point (look-ahead point on the row): $(x_g, y_g)$
- Wheelbase: $L$ (m)
- Look-ahead distance: $L_d$ (m)

### Step 1 — Compute the vector from robot to goal:**

$$\Delta x = x_g - x_r, \quad \Delta y = y_g - y_r$$

### Step 2 — Transform into the robot's local frame (rotate by $-\theta_r$):**

$$x_{local} = \Delta x \cos\theta_r + \Delta y \sin\theta_r$$

$$y_{local} = -\Delta x \sin\theta_r + \Delta y \cos\theta_r$$

### Step 3 — Compute the heading angle error $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{y_{local}}{x_{local}}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Pure Pursuit curvature law:**

The geometric relationship between the look-ahead distance and the radius of the circular arc the robot must follow is:

$$\dfrac{L_d}{2} = R \sin\alpha$$

where $R$ is the turning radius. Solving for the curvature $\kappa = 1/R$:

$$\kappa = \dfrac{2 \sin\alpha}{L_d}$$

### Step 5 — Convert curvature to steering angle $\delta$ (Ackermann model):**

For a small-wheelbase vehicle (e.g., a weeding robot), the steering angle is approximated as:

$$\delta = \arctan(\kappa \cdot L)$$

### Step 6 — Final control law:**

$$\boxed{\delta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2 L \sin\alpha}{L_d}\right)}$$

### Tuning Insight:
- **Increasing $L_d$** → smoother path, larger corner-cutting (good for high-speed tractors)
- **Decreasing $L_d$** → tighter path tracking, more oscillation (good for low-speed, row-precise weeders)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Setting $L_d$ to **zero** causes division by zero — the robot will spin in circles. The minimum practical $L_d$ is **0.5 m** for an autonomous tractor, and **0.15 m** for a small ground robot.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Control Approaches

| Criterion | Remote Control | Teleop with Auto-Steer | Fully Autonomous |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Human Workload** | 100% (continuous) | 30% (monitoring) | < 5% (exception handling) |
| **Bandwidth Required** | ≥ 2 Mbps (video) | ≥ 500 kbps | ≤ 100 kbps (telemetry only) |
| **Latency Tolerance** | < 100 ms | < 250 ms | < 1 s |
| **Positioning Accuracy** | N/A (visual) | ± 5 cm (RTK-GPS) | ± 2 cm (RTK-GPS + SLAM) |
| **Cost (Relative)** | 1× | 5–8× | 15–30× |
| **Failure Mode** | Loss of link → stop | Sensor fault → fallback to manual | Redundant compute → safe stop |
| **KTU Example** | Drone scouting | Auto-steer tractor (John Deere AutoTrac) | Naio Technologies Oz weeding robot |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Architecture — Remote-Controlled Agricultural Robot

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete information flow of a teleoperated field robot, from operator command to actuator response and feedback loop closure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Console Ground Station] --> B[Command Encoding MAVLink v2 Packet]
    B --> C[RF Transmitter 2.4 GHz 20 dBm]
    C --> D[Wireless Channel Free Space Path Loss]
    D --> E[RF Receiver Onboard Robot]
    E --> F[MAVLink Decoder Payload Extraction]
    F --> G[Autopilot Firmware Pixhawk PX4]
    G --> H[Motor Mixer PWM Signal Generation]
    H --> I[BLDC Motor Drivers ESC 30 A]
    I --> J[Wheels and Steering Actuator]
    J --> K[Robot Moves in Field]
    K --> L[Onboard Sensors Camera IMU LiDAR GPS]
    L --> M[Telemetry Encoder MAVLink Stream]
    M --> N[RF Transmitter Telemetry 433 MHz]
    N --> O[Wireless Return Channel]
    O --> P[Ground Station Receiver]
    P --> A
```

### Block-Level Functional Notes:

- **Block A → B:** Operator joystick (e.g., Logitech Extreme 3D Pro) outputs analog voltages (0–3.3 V). An Arduino or RPi converts these to **MAVLink MANUAL_CONTROL** messages.
- **Block D:** The free-space path loss is computed via the FSPL equation; at 500 m and 2.4 GHz, this is ~94 dB.
- **Block G:** The autopilot runs a **200 Hz inner-loop PID controller** for attitude stabilization.
- **Block L → M:** Telemetry includes attitude (roll/pitch/yaw), GPS fix, battery voltage, and current draw — typically at **1–10 Hz** update rate.
- **Block P → A:** The closed loop latency budget: input acquisition (5 ms) + encoding (2 ms) + RF propagation (3.3 µs) + decoding (2 ms) + actuation (50 ms) + sensor read (10 ms) + return encoding (2 ms) + RF return (3.3 µs) + display (16 ms) ≈ **87 ms** — well within the 100 ms safety threshold.

## 4.2 Hybrid Autonomy Architecture — Sense-Plan-Act with Reactive Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission Definition Geo-fenced Field Map] --> B[Mission Planner FSM Behavior Tree]
    B --> C[Path Planner A-star RRT Pure Pursuit]
    C --> D[Motion Controller PID Stanley]
    D --> E[Actuator Layer PWM Hydraulic]
    E --> F[Physical Robot in Field]
    F --> G[Sensor Suite LiDAR Camera IMU RTK GPS]
    G --> H[Sensor Fusion EKF UKF]
    H --> I[SLAM and Localization]
    I --> C
    G --> J[Reactive Obstacle Avoidance VFH DWA]
    J --> K{Emergency Stop Trigger}
    K -->|Threat Detected| L[Hard Brake and Halt]
    K -->|Clear Path| D
    H --> M[Telemetry to Operator Cloud Dashboard]
    M --> N[Operator Notification Anomaly Alert]
    N --> B
```

### Block-Level Functional Notes:

- **Block A → B:** The mission is a JSON file specifying waypoints, action tags (spray, mow, weed), and constraints (no-spray buffer zones).
- **Block C:** The path planner uses the **A\*** algorithm with a grid resolution of **0.25 m** (matching row spacing for rice/maize).
- **Block H:** An **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** fuses GPS (1 Hz) with IMU (200 Hz) to produce a **50 Hz** pose estimate.
- **Block J:** The Vector Field Histogram (VFH) operates at **20 Hz** to detect obstacles within a **2 m** safety bubble.
- **Block K:** A watchdog timer enforces that the avoidance layer responds within **100 ms**; if not, an **E-STOP** is asserted.

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Remote vs Autonomous Mode Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Power On] --> B{GPS Fix Acquired}
    B -->|No| C[Wait for GPS Cold Start up to 90 s]
    C --> B
    B -->|Yes| D{Operator Mode Select}
    D -->|Manual| E[Remote Control Mode RC Link Active]
    D -->|Auto| F{Mission File Loaded}
    F -->|No| G[Operator Prompt Upload Mission]
    G --> F
    F -->|Yes| H[Autonomous Mode Start]
    H --> I[Navigate to First Waypoint]
    I --> J{Obstacle Detected}
    J -->|Yes| K[Replan Path A-star Re-search]
    K --> I
    J -->|No| L{Waypoint Reached}
    L -->|No| I
    L -->|Yes| M{More Waypoints}
    M -->|Yes| I
    M -->|No| N[Mission Complete Return to Home]
    E --> O{Link Loss Detected}
    O -->|Yes| P[Failsafe Hover Hold and Auto RTL]
    O -->|No| E
```

### Block-Level Functional Notes:

- **Block B → C:** Cold-start GPS acquisition can take up to **90 seconds** for a first-ever fix; a warm start is **< 10 seconds**.
- **Block O → P:** Failsafe behavior is *legally mandated* for agricultural UAVs above **250 g** under DGCA (India) regulations — Return-to-Launch (RTL) is the default.
- **Block I → J:** The obstacle check runs at **20 Hz**; the planner runs at **1 Hz** (asynchronously).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to "draw the architecture of an autonomous agricultural robot," always include the **reactive safety layer** as a *parallel* branch to the deliberative path. A common error is to draw it as a *subordinate* block — this loses marks because the reactive layer must have authority to *override* the deliberative layer in emergencies.
